This form provides the partner with the necessary information to report their share of partnership activity on their individual income tax return.

questions.educational: Partnerships are pass-through entities, meaning the partnership itself does not pay income tax. Instead, the partnership's income and deductions are passed through to the partners, who report their share on their individual income tax returns. The Schedule K-1 is crucial for ensuring that each partner accurately reports their share of the partnership's activities, maintaining the integrity of the pass-through taxation system.
questions.examTip: Remember that Schedule K-1 is the key document for partners to determine their individual tax liability related to the partnership. Pay close attention to the various boxes on the K-1, as they represent different types of income and deductions that may have varying tax treatments on the partner's individual return.
questions.memoryAid: K-1 = Key to Partnership Income

questions.question: Guaranteed payments to a partner for services are:
options: Not deductible by the partnership and not taxable to the partner
Deductible by the partnership and taxable to the partner as ordinary income
Subject to self-employment tax only if the partner is a general partner
questions.explanation: Guaranteed payments are deductible by the partnership and reported as ordinary income by the partner. For general partners, guaranteed payments are also subject to self-employment tax.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - Guaranteed payments are specifically deductible by the partnership under IRC Section 707(c) as if made to a non-partner, and they are taxable as ordinary income to the recipient partner.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - IRC Section 707(c) dictates that guaranteed payments are treated as if made to a non-partner for purposes of deductibility by the partnership and are included in the partner's ordinary income. This ensures that the partner is taxed on the compensation received for their services.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - While it's true that guaranteed payments to general partners are subject to self-employment tax, this is not the only requirement. Guaranteed payments to limited partners for services are also subject to self-employment tax if the services are integral to the partnership's business. The statement is therefore incomplete and misleading.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - Guaranteed payments are not treated as distributions. Distributions reduce a partner's basis, but guaranteed payments are treated as salary or wages for tax purposes, not as a return of capital.

questions.educational: Guaranteed payments are designed to compensate partners for services or capital without regard to partnership income. They are treated as if paid to a non-partner, ensuring the partnership can deduct the expense (subject to reasonableness) and the partner recognizes ordinary income. This treatment prevents partners from avoiding tax on compensation by disguising it as a distribution.
questions.examTip: Remember that guaranteed payments are reported on Schedule K-1 (Form 1065), box 4, and are subject to self-employment tax for partners providing significant services to the partnership, regardless of their general or limited status.
questions.memoryAid: Guaranteed = Gross Income (to partner) & Gone (deductible by partnership)

questions.question: A partner's basis in their partnership interest is increased by:
options: Partnership losses allocated to the partner
The partner's share of partnership income
questions.explanation: A partner's basis increases by their share of partnership income (including tax-exempt income) and additional contributions. Basis decreases for distributions, losses, and nondeductible expenses.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - Partnership losses allocated to a partner *decrease* their basis in the partnership interest. Losses represent a reduction in the partner's economic investment in the partnership.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - Distributions from the partnership *decrease* a partner's basis. Distributions represent a return of capital to the partner, reducing their investment.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - Guaranteed payments are treated as if paid to a non-partner for services or use of capital. While the partner will report the guaranteed payment as income, it does *not* increase their basis in the partnership interest. Guaranteed payments are already accounted for in the partner's distributive share of partnership income.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is CORRECT - A partner's share of partnership income *increases* their basis in the partnership interest. This is because the partner is taxed on their share of the income, whether or not it is distributed to them. The increase in basis prevents double taxation when the income is eventually distributed.

questions.educational: A partner's basis in their partnership interest is a critical calculation for determining gain or loss on the sale of the interest, as well as the deductibility of partnership losses. The basis is essentially a running tally of the partner's investment in the partnership, adjusted for contributions, distributions, income, and losses. Understanding basis is crucial for accurate tax reporting and planning, especially concerning loss limitations under IRC Section 704(d).
questions.examTip: On the EA exam, carefully distinguish between items that increase basis (contributions, income) and those that decrease basis (distributions, losses, nondeductible expenses). Pay close attention to the timing of these items, as basis is determined at the end of the partnership's tax year.
questions.memoryAid: C.I.D.L. - Contributions and Income increase basis; Distributions and Losses decrease basis.

questions.question: A cash distribution from a partnership to a partner is taxable to the extent it:
options: Exceeds the partner's basis in their partnership interest
Exceeds the fair market value of the partnership
questions.explanation: Cash distributions are tax-free to the extent of the partner's basis. If a cash distribution exceeds basis, the excess is treated as gain from the sale of the partnership interest (usually capital gain).

whyWrong.0: Why option A is CORRECT - Cash distributions from a partnership are generally tax-free to the extent of a partner's adjusted basis in their partnership interest. However, under IRC Section 731(a), if the cash distribution exceeds the partner's basis, the excess is treated as gain from the sale of the partnership interest, usually capital gain.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - Not all distributions are fully taxable. Distributions are tax-free to the extent of the partner's basis in the partnership interest. Only distributions exceeding basis are taxable, per IRC Section 731(a).
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - The fair market value of the partnership is irrelevant in determining the taxability of cash distributions to a partner. The key factor is the partner's adjusted basis in their partnership interest, not the overall value of the partnership.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - There is no $10,000 threshold that triggers taxability for partnership distributions. The taxability depends solely on whether the cash distribution exceeds the partner's adjusted basis in their partnership interest, regardless of the distribution amount.

questions.educational: Partnership taxation operates under a pass-through system, meaning the partnership itself doesn't generally pay income tax. Distributions are treated as a return of capital up to the partner's basis, preventing double taxation. This aligns with the broader concept of basis tracking, which is crucial for accurately determining gains and losses in various tax scenarios, including S corporations and individual investments.
questions.examTip: Remember that distributions reduce a partner's basis. On the EA exam, carefully track basis adjustments throughout the year to accurately determine the taxability of distributions.
questions.memoryAid: Basis Before Bucks: Distributions are tax-free up to your basis.

questions.question: Under the at-risk rules, a partner's deductible losses are limited to:
options: The amount the partner has at risk in the activity
The fair market value of the partnership interest
The partner's share of partnership liabilities
questions.explanation: Under IRC §465, losses are limited to the amount the partner has at risk, which includes cash contributions, adjusted basis of property contributed, and certain recourse liabilities. Non-recourse debt generally does not increase at-risk amount.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is CORRECT - The at-risk rules, governed by IRC §465, limit a partner's deductible losses to the amount they have at risk in the activity. This amount generally includes cash contributions, the adjusted basis of contributed property, and certain recourse liabilities for which the partner is personally liable.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - The fair market value of the partnership interest represents what a willing buyer would pay for the interest, but it doesn't determine the deductible loss limitations under the at-risk rules. The at-risk rules focus on the partner's economic risk of loss, not the overall value of their investment.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - While a partner's share of partnership liabilities can sometimes increase their at-risk amount, it's not the sole determinant of deductible losses. Specifically, only recourse liabilities (where the partner is personally liable) generally increase the at-risk amount. Nonrecourse liabilities typically do not, unless certain exceptions apply (e.g., qualified nonrecourse financing).
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - The partner's capital account balance represents their equity in the partnership, but it doesn't directly dictate the deductible loss limitations under the at-risk rules. A capital account can be positive or negative, and it doesn't necessarily reflect the amount the partner has at risk, which is based on their economic exposure to loss.

questions.educational: The at-risk rules prevent taxpayers from deducting losses exceeding their actual economic investment in an activity. This rule, along with the passive activity loss rules, aims to curb tax shelter abuses by ensuring that taxpayers only deduct losses to the extent they are truly at risk of losing money. Understanding these limitations is crucial for accurate tax planning and compliance, particularly when dealing with partnerships and other pass-through entities.
questions.examTip: Remember that nonrecourse debt generally does *not* increase a partner's at-risk amount unless it qualifies as 'qualified nonrecourse financing' related to real estate. This is a frequently tested exception on the EA exam.
questions.memoryAid: At-Risk = Cash + Property Basis + Recourse Debt (Generally). Think 'CPR' for the main components.

questions.question: The federal corporate income tax rate for C corporations is:
questions.explanation: The Tax Cuts and Jobs Act established a flat 21% corporate tax rate for C corporations, replacing the previous graduated rate structure that ranged from 15% to 35%.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - The graduated corporate tax rates ranging from 15% to 35% were eliminated by the Tax Cuts and Jobs Act (TCJA) of 2017. This option reflects the tax law prior to the TCJA.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - The Tax Cuts and Jobs Act (TCJA) of 2017 established a flat corporate income tax rate of 21% for C corporations, effective for tax years beginning after December 31, 2017. This simplifies corporate tax calculations and reduces the overall tax burden for many corporations.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - While some small businesses may qualify for preferential tax treatment, there is no provision in the current federal corporate income tax law that taxes the first $50,000 of corporate income at 15%. This option is factually incorrect.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - The federal corporate income tax rate for C corporations is not a flat 25%. The current rate, established by the Tax Cuts and Jobs Act, is 21%.

questions.educational: The flat 21% corporate tax rate was implemented to encourage economic growth by reducing the tax burden on businesses, allowing them to reinvest profits and expand operations. Understanding the current corporate tax rate is crucial for accurately calculating a C corporation's tax liability and advising clients on tax planning strategies, a key aspect of the Enrolled Agent's role.
questions.examTip: Remember that the 21% flat corporate tax rate applies to C corporations only. Pass-through entities like S corporations and partnerships are taxed at the individual level, so their income is subject to individual income tax rates.
questions.memoryAid: C-Corp = 21%

questions.question: For a tax-free incorporation under Section 351, the transferors must have control, meaning they own at least:
questions.explanation: Under Section 351, transferors must own at least 80% of the total combined voting power and 80% of each class of nonvoting stock immediately after the exchange for the transfer to be tax-free.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - While 50% represents a simple majority, Section 351 requires a significantly higher threshold of control for a tax-free incorporation. The 80% threshold ensures that the transferors maintain substantial ownership and control over the corporation after the exchange.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - Although 100% ownership would certainly satisfy the control requirement, it's not the minimum threshold. Section 351 allows for tax-free incorporation even if the transferors don't own all of the stock, as long as they meet the 80% control test.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - 51% ownership, while representing a majority, does not meet the stringent control requirements outlined in Section 351. The IRS requires a much higher level of ownership to ensure that the transferors effectively control the corporation after the exchange.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is CORRECT - Section 351 of the Internal Revenue Code dictates that for a transfer of property to a corporation to be tax-free, the transferors must control the corporation immediately after the exchange. Control is defined as owning at least 80% of the total combined voting power of all classes of stock entitled to vote and at least 80% of the total number of shares of all other classes of stock.

questions.educational: Section 351 allows taxpayers to transfer property to a corporation without recognizing a gain or loss. This facilitates business formation and restructuring. The control requirement ensures that the transfer is essentially a change in the form of ownership rather than a sale or disposition of property, which would trigger a taxable event. This rule connects to broader exam topics like corporate reorganizations and tax-free exchanges.
questions.examTip: Remember the 80% control test for both voting and non-voting stock when analyzing Section 351 incorporations on the EA exam. Pay close attention to the stock ownership percentages provided in the fact pattern.
questions.memoryAid: Think of "80% Control = Tax-Free Roll" to remember the key percentage for Section 351.

questions.question: Double taxation of C corporation earnings refers to:
options: Regular tax and alternative minimum tax
Tax at the corporate level and again when dividends are distributed to shareholders
questions.explanation: Double taxation means C corporation income is taxed first at the corporate level (21%) and again when distributed to shareholders as dividends (at preferential rates of 0%, 15%, or 20% plus possible 3.8% NIIT).

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - The alternative minimum tax (AMT) was repealed for C corporations by the Tax Cuts and Jobs Act (TCJA) of 2017. While regular tax is a corporate tax, it does not represent 'double taxation' in the context of C corporations.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - While C corporations are subject to both federal and state taxes, this is not what is meant by 'double taxation.' Double taxation specifically refers to the taxation of corporate profits at the entity level and again at the shareholder level when distributed as dividends.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is CORRECT - Double taxation is the key characteristic of C corporations. The corporation pays income tax on its profits, and then shareholders pay income tax again when those profits are distributed to them as dividends. This is a major disadvantage of the C corporation structure.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - Income tax can be a component of double taxation, but self-employment tax applies to pass-through entities like sole proprietorships and partnerships, not C corporations. Self-employment tax is paid by individuals on their business income, not by a corporation.

questions.educational: The concept of double taxation is central to understanding the implications of choosing a C corporation structure. Because the corporation is treated as a separate legal entity, its profits are taxed at the corporate level. When these profits are distributed to shareholders as dividends, the shareholders must then pay individual income tax on the dividend income, resulting in double taxation. This is a key difference compared to pass-through entities like S corporations or partnerships, where income is only taxed at the individual level.
questions.examTip: On the EA exam, remember that the double taxation of C corporations is a significant disadvantage that often leads businesses to choose other entity structures, especially smaller businesses. Be prepared to advise clients on the tax implications of different entity choices.
questions.memoryAid: C Corp = Corporate Tax + Shareholder Tax (Dividends)

questions.question: A C corporation that owns less than 20% of another corporation may deduct what percentage of dividends received?
questions.explanation: The dividends received deduction (DRD) is 50% if the corporation owns less than 20% of the paying corporation, 65% if it owns 20% to less than 80%, and 100% if it owns 80% or more (with affiliated group rules).

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - A 100% dividends received deduction (DRD) is only available when the receiving corporation owns 80% or more of the paying corporation, which is not the case here.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - The dividends received deduction (DRD) allows a corporation to deduct a portion of the dividends it receives from other corporations. When a corporation owns less than 20% of the distributing corporation, it can deduct 50% of the dividends received, per IRC Section 243(a)(1).
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - A 65% dividends received deduction (DRD) applies when the receiving corporation owns 20% or more, but less than 80%, of the distributing corporation. Since the question specifies ownership of less than 20%, this option is incorrect.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - An 80% dividends received deduction (DRD) is not a standard deduction percentage. The correct percentages are 50%, 65%, and 100%, depending on the ownership percentage.

questions.educational: The dividends received deduction (DRD) mitigates the effects of triple taxation. Without it, corporate earnings could be taxed at the distributing corporation level, again when distributed as dividends to the receiving corporation, and potentially a third time when the receiving corporation distributes those earnings to its shareholders. Understanding the DRD is crucial for accurately calculating a C corporation's taxable income, a key area tested on the SEE exam.
questions.examTip: On the EA exam, carefully note the ownership percentage between the corporations when calculating the dividends received deduction. A seemingly small difference in ownership can significantly impact the allowable deduction.
questions.memoryAid: DRD Percentages: Less than 20% = 50%; 20-80% = 65%; 80%+ = 100%. Think 'Low to High, Small to Big' for ownership and deduction.

questions.question: Corporations with expected tax liability of $500 or more must make estimated tax payments:
questions.explanation: Corporations expecting to owe $500 or more in tax must make quarterly estimated payments. The payments are due on the 15th day of the 4th, 6th, 9th, and 12th months of the corporation's tax year.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - Corporations are not required to make estimated tax payments monthly. The IRS requires quarterly payments, not monthly, for corporations anticipating a tax liability of $500 or more.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - Corporations cannot wait until year-end to pay their taxes if they expect to owe $500 or more. The IRS mandates timely payments throughout the year via estimated tax payments to avoid penalties.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is CORRECT - Corporations expecting to owe $500 or more in federal income tax are required to make estimated tax payments on a quarterly basis. These payments are generally due on the 15th day of the 4th, 6th, 9th, and 12th months of the corporation's tax year, although specific dates may vary slightly due to weekends or holidays.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - Corporations are not required to make estimated tax payments twice a year. The IRS requires quarterly payments, not semi-annual, for corporations anticipating a tax liability of $500 or more.

questions.educational: The requirement for corporations to make estimated tax payments stems from the pay-as-you-go system of taxation. This system aims to ensure that tax liabilities are met throughout the year, rather than in a lump sum at the end, aligning with the government's need for consistent revenue flow. Understanding estimated tax payment rules is crucial for C corporations, as failure to comply can result in penalties, a common area tested on the SEE exam.
questions.examTip: On the EA exam, pay close attention to the specific due dates for corporate estimated tax payments, as they can differ slightly from individual due dates and are a frequent source of questions.
questions.memoryAid: Think 'C-Corps Pay Quarterly' to remember that C corporations make estimated tax payments four times a year.

questions.question: The accumulated earnings tax is imposed on corporations that:
options: Accumulate earnings beyond reasonable business needs to avoid shareholder taxes
questions.explanation: The accumulated earnings tax (20%) is imposed on corporations that accumulate earnings beyond the reasonable needs of the business to avoid shareholder-level taxes on dividends. The first $250,000 ($150,000 for service corporations) is generally exempt.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is CORRECT - The accumulated earnings tax (AET) is specifically designed to penalize corporations that retain earnings beyond what is reasonably necessary for their business operations, with the intent of avoiding dividend distributions and the resulting tax liability for shareholders. This tax is levied in addition to the regular corporate income tax.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - The number of shareholders a corporation has is irrelevant to the accumulated earnings tax. S corporations have limitations on the number and type of shareholders, but C corporations do not. The AET focuses on the purpose of retained earnings, not the shareholder base.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - Having net operating losses (NOLs) does not automatically trigger the accumulated earnings tax. While a company with NOLs might retain earnings, the AET is only applied if the accumulation is deemed unreasonable and for the purpose of avoiding shareholder taxes. NOLs can even be a valid reason for accumulating earnings.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - The amount of dividends paid is not a direct trigger for the accumulated earnings tax. While excessive accumulation of earnings *instead* of paying dividends is a key factor, simply paying dividends exceeding $1 million does not, in itself, trigger the tax. The focus is on the *unreasonable* accumulation, not the dividend payout amount.

questions.educational: The accumulated earnings tax (AET) exists to prevent corporations from being used as tax shelters for their shareholders. Without it, high-income individuals could incorporate their businesses, accumulate profits within the corporation (taxed at the corporate rate), and avoid paying higher individual income tax rates on dividends. This tax encourages companies to distribute profits to shareholders or invest them in legitimate business activities.
questions.examTip: When answering questions about the accumulated earnings tax, remember to focus on the *intent* behind the earnings accumulation. The IRS will look at factors like past dividend history, expansion plans, and working capital needs to determine if the accumulation is reasonable.
questions.memoryAid: AET = Avoid Shareholder Taxes (Accumulated Earnings Tax is designed to prevent avoidance of shareholder taxes)

questions.question: A personal holding company (PHC) is subject to a penalty tax of:
questions.explanation: A PHC is subject to a 20% penalty tax on undistributed personal holding company income. A PHC is a corporation where more than 50% of stock is owned by 5 or fewer individuals and 60%+ of income is passive (dividends, interest, rents, royalties).

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - The penalty tax rate for a personal holding company is not 15%. This rate may be confused with other tax rates, but it's crucial to remember the specific PHC penalty tax.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - A 10% rate is not the penalty tax rate for a personal holding company. While 10% might appear in other tax calculations, it doesn't apply here.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - 35% is the maximum corporate income tax rate, but it is not the penalty tax rate for a personal holding company. Confusing the general corporate tax rate with the PHC penalty is a common mistake.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is CORRECT - A personal holding company (PHC) is subject to a 20% penalty tax on its undistributed personal holding company income (UPHC income). This penalty is designed to discourage companies from being used to shelter passive income of high-income individuals.

questions.educational: The personal holding company (PHC) tax exists to prevent individuals from using corporations to avoid paying individual income tax on passive income. By accumulating passive income within a corporation and not distributing it, individuals could potentially defer or avoid higher individual tax rates. The PHC rules force distribution of earnings or subject the corporation to a penalty tax, aligning with the broader goal of tax fairness and preventing tax avoidance schemes.
questions.examTip: When tackling PHC questions on the EA exam, first determine if the corporation meets the stock ownership and income tests. If it does, remember the 20% penalty tax applies to undistributed PHC income, not the corporation's entire taxable income.
questions.memoryAid: PHC = 20% Penalty (Think: PHC - 'P'enalize 'H'igh 'C'oncentration of passive income with 20%)

questions.question: A calendar-year C corporation's Form 1120 is due on:
questions.explanation: Form 1120 for a calendar-year C corporation is due on April 15 (the 15th day of the 4th month after the tax year ends). An automatic 6-month extension to October 15 is available by filing Form 7004.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - March 15 is the due date for partnerships (Form 1065) and S corporations (Form 1120-S), not C corporations. Confusing these due dates is a common mistake on the exam.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - A calendar-year C corporation's Form 1120 is due on April 15, which is the 15th day of the fourth month following the close of its tax year (December 31). This is a standard filing deadline outlined in the IRS instructions for Form 1120.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - September 15 is not the original due date for a calendar-year C corporation's Form 1120. It could be a due date after filing for an extension, but the question asks for the original due date.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - April 18 is incorrect. While due dates can sometimes shift due to weekends or holidays, the standard due date for a calendar-year C corporation is April 15. April 18 is not a consistently recurring due date.

questions.educational: Understanding corporate filing deadlines is crucial for Enrolled Agents. C corporations, unlike pass-through entities, have a separate tax liability, and their filing deadlines reflect this. The April 15 deadline allows the IRS to collect corporate taxes in a timely manner, contributing to federal revenue. Knowing these deadlines helps you advise clients on tax compliance and avoid penalties.
questions.examTip: Pay close attention to the entity type in the question. The filing deadlines for partnerships, S corporations, and C corporations are different, and the EA exam frequently tests your knowledge of these distinctions.
questions.memoryAid: C Corps file in April (C-A). Partnerships & S-Corps file in March (P/S-M).

questions.question: An S corporation can have no more than:
questions.explanation: An S corporation is limited to 100 shareholders. A husband and wife (and their estates) are treated as one shareholder. Family members can elect to be treated as one shareholder as well.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - An S corporation is not limited to 500 shareholders. This number is significantly higher than the actual limit of 100.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - An S corporation is not limited to 75 shareholders. While this was the limit in the past, it has since been increased.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - An S corporation is not limited to 35 shareholders. This was the limit many years ago, but the current limit is higher.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is CORRECT - An S corporation, as defined under Subchapter S of the Internal Revenue Code, is limited to a maximum of 100 shareholders. This limitation is a key factor in determining eligibility to elect S corporation status, allowing small businesses to avoid double taxation.

questions.educational: The S corporation structure, governed by Subchapter S of the Internal Revenue Code, aims to provide small businesses with the benefits of incorporation (like limited liability) while avoiding the double taxation inherent in C corporations. The 100-shareholder limit, along with other restrictions like the type of shareholders allowed, ensures that S corporation status is reserved for smaller, closely-held businesses. Understanding these eligibility requirements is crucial for advising clients on the most appropriate business structure for their needs.
questions.examTip: Remember that family attribution rules can affect the shareholder count; a husband and wife are treated as a single shareholder for S corporation purposes, which can be beneficial when advising clients close to the 100-shareholder limit.
questions.memoryAid: S-corp: 'S'tands for 'Small' - think 100 shareholders max.

questions.question: Which of the following may be a shareholder in an S corporation?
questions.explanation: S corporation shareholders must be individuals (U.S. citizens or residents), certain trusts, or estates. Partnerships, corporations, and nonresident aliens cannot be S corporation shareholders.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - C corporations are not eligible to be S corporation shareholders. Allowing C corporations as shareholders would create a tiered structure, complicating tax reporting and potentially leading to tax avoidance, which is prohibited under Subchapter S rules.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - An individual U.S. citizen is a permissible shareholder in an S corporation. S corporations are designed to allow small businesses to be taxed at the individual level, and U.S. citizens and residents are explicitly allowed as shareholders to facilitate this.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - Partnerships are not eligible to be S corporation shareholders. Similar to C corporations, allowing partnerships as shareholders would create complex ownership structures and potentially circumvent the single level of taxation intended for S corporations.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - Nonresident aliens are specifically prohibited from being S corporation shareholders. Allowing nonresident aliens as shareholders would complicate the tax reporting and enforcement process, as their income is subject to different tax treaties and regulations.

questions.educational: S corporations are pass-through entities designed to avoid double taxation, meaning profits and losses are passed directly to the shareholders' individual income tax returns. To maintain this structure and prevent tax avoidance, strict rules govern who can be a shareholder. These rules ensure that the income is taxed at the individual level and that the IRS can effectively track and collect taxes.
questions.examTip: Remember the specific types of entities that are *not* allowed as S corporation shareholders (C corporations, partnerships, nonresident aliens) – these are frequently tested on the EA exam.
questions.memoryAid: Think of 'SIN' - S-corp shareholders cannot be 'S' Corporations, 'I'nternational (Non-Resident Aliens), or 'N'on-individual entities (like Partnerships).

questions.question: To elect S corporation status effective for the current tax year, Form 2553 must be filed:
options: By the end of the tax year
Within 30 days of forming the corporation
By the 15th day of the 3rd month of the tax year
questions.explanation: Form 2553 must be filed by the 15th day of the 3rd month of the tax year for which the election is to be effective (March 15 for calendar-year corporations). Late elections may be accepted under certain relief provisions.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - Filing Form 2553 with the first tax return is incorrect. The election must be made before the tax return is due, not with it. The IRS requires the election to be made earlier in the tax year to allow for proper processing and compliance.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - While it's true that late S-corp elections can sometimes be made, the general rule is that Form 2553 must be filed *before* the end of the tax year for the election to be effective for that year. Waiting until the end of the year will generally result in the election being effective for the *following* tax year.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - The 30-day timeframe applies to certain other corporate actions, but not to the S corporation election. The deadline for filing Form 2553 is tied to the tax year, not the date of incorporation.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is CORRECT - To elect S corporation status and have it be effective for the current tax year, Form 2553 must be filed by the 15th day of the 3rd month of the tax year (e.g., March 15th for a calendar-year corporation). This deadline allows the IRS sufficient time to process the election and ensures the corporation is treated as an S corporation for the entire tax year.

questions.educational: The S corporation election allows a qualifying corporation to pass its income, losses, deductions, and credits through to its shareholders, avoiding double taxation at the corporate level. This election is made by filing Form 2553, and the timing of the filing is crucial for determining when the S corporation status becomes effective. Understanding the election requirements and deadlines is a key part of the Business Taxation section of the SEE exam, particularly regarding pass-through entities.
questions.examTip: Remember that the S-corp election deadline is strict, but late election relief may be available under certain circumstances. As an Enrolled Agent, be prepared to advise clients on the implications of late elections and the steps needed to request relief.
questions.memoryAid: Think '3/15' - the 15th day of the 3rd month is the S-corp election deadline for calendar-year corporations.

questions.question: S corporations are generally:
options: Subject to federal income tax at the entity level
Subject to self-employment tax on all income
Pass-through entities not subject to entity-level tax
questions.explanation: S corporations are generally pass-through entities. Income, deductions, and credits pass through to shareholders who report them on their individual returns. The S corporation itself generally does not pay federal income tax.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - S corporations are generally not subject to federal income tax at the entity level. Instead, their income and losses are passed through to the shareholders to be reported on their individual income tax returns. There are exceptions, such as the built-in gains tax under IRC Section 1374, but the *general* rule is pass-through taxation.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - Not all S corporation income is subject to self-employment tax. Only the shareholder's salary as an employee is subject to self-employment tax. Distributions are generally not subject to self-employment tax, although the IRS may reclassify distributions as wages in certain circumstances.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is CORRECT - S corporations are pass-through entities, meaning the corporation's income, losses, deductions, and credits are passed through to its shareholders. Shareholders then report these items on their individual income tax returns, avoiding double taxation at the corporate level (unlike C corporations). This is the primary benefit of electing S corporation status.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - S corporations are not taxed at a 15% flat rate. The income and losses pass through to the shareholders, who are taxed at their individual income tax rates. The S corporation itself generally does not pay federal income tax, so there is no flat rate applicable at the entity level.

questions.educational: S corporation taxation aims to avoid the double taxation inherent in C corporations. By electing S corporation status, a business can pass its income and losses directly to its owners, who then report these items on their individual tax returns. This aligns with the broader tax principle of taxing income once at the individual level, simplifying the tax structure for smaller businesses.
questions.examTip: Remember that while S corporations generally avoid entity-level tax, exceptions like the built-in gains tax and passive investment income tax can apply. Be prepared to identify these situations on the exam.
questions.memoryAid: S Corp = Single Tax (generally)

questions.question: An S corporation shareholder-employee must receive:
options: Reasonable compensation for services before taking distributions
questions.explanation: The IRS requires shareholder-employees to receive reasonable compensation (W-2 wages) for services performed before taking additional distributions. This prevents avoiding payroll taxes by taking all income as distributions.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - S corporation shareholder-employees who perform services for the corporation must receive reasonable compensation in the form of wages subject to payroll taxes. Taking only distributions would be an attempt to avoid these taxes, which is not permissible.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - There is no fixed percentage of profits that must be paid as compensation to a shareholder-employee. The requirement is for *reasonable* compensation, which is determined based on the services provided, not a formula tied to profits.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is CORRECT - The IRS requires S corporation shareholder-employees to receive reasonable compensation for the services they provide to the corporation before taking distributions. This is to ensure that shareholder-employees pay their fair share of employment taxes (Social Security and Medicare) on the income they earn through their labor. Distributions are considered a return on investment and are not subject to these taxes.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - While paying at least minimum wage might be a factor in determining reasonable compensation, it is not the sole determining factor. Reasonable compensation must reflect the actual value of the services provided, which could be significantly higher than minimum wage depending on the shareholder-employee's role and responsibilities.

questions.educational: The reasonable compensation requirement for S corporation shareholder-employees is a critical aspect of S corporation taxation. It prevents taxpayers from avoiding payroll taxes by disguising wages as distributions. Understanding this concept is essential for accurately preparing S corporation tax returns and advising clients on proper tax planning strategies, aligning with the SEE exam's focus on compliance and ethical considerations.
questions.examTip: On the EA exam, be prepared to analyze scenarios involving S corporation shareholder-employee compensation and determine whether the compensation is reasonable based on factors like industry standards, experience, and responsibilities. Remember that the IRS scrutinizes situations where distributions are disproportionately high compared to wages.
questions.memoryAid: Think 'W-2 First, Then Distributions' for S corps. Wages (W-2) must be paid to shareholder-employees before distributions can be taken.

questions.question: Unlike partnership interests, S corporation shareholder basis does NOT include:
questions.explanation: S corporation shareholder basis includes contributions and share of income but does NOT include the shareholder's share of entity-level debt. This differs from partnerships, where partner basis includes their share of partnership liabilities.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - Cash contributions by a shareholder directly increase their basis in the S corporation stock. This is a fundamental principle of basis determination, reflecting the shareholder's investment.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - A shareholder's share of S corporation income (both taxable and tax-exempt) increases their basis in the S corporation stock. This prevents double taxation when the stock is eventually sold; the income has already been taxed at the shareholder level.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - The basis of property contributed to an S corporation by a shareholder generally becomes part of the shareholder's stock basis. The adjusted basis of the property carries over, reflecting the shareholder's investment in the corporation.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is CORRECT - Unlike partnerships, S corporation shareholders generally do NOT include the corporation's liabilities in their basis. This is a key difference between the two entity types, stemming from the limited liability afforded to S corporation shareholders. The debt is considered a corporate obligation, not a personal one for basis purposes.

questions.educational: The difference in debt treatment between partnerships and S corporations is crucial. In partnerships, partners have at-risk basis that includes their share of recourse debt, allowing them to deduct losses beyond their capital contributions. S corporations, however, do not pass through debt basis to shareholders, limiting loss deductions to the shareholder's actual investment and direct loans to the corporation. This distinction reflects the different liability structures and risk profiles of the two entity types.
questions.examTip: Remember that while a direct loan *from* a shareholder to the S corporation increases the shareholder's basis, the S corporation's debt to third parties does not. This is a frequently tested area on the SEE exam.
questions.memoryAid: S Corp: No Share of Debt (NSD)

questions.question: A calendar-year S corporation's Form 1120-S is due on:
questions.explanation: Form 1120-S for a calendar-year S corporation is due on March 15 (the 15th day of the 3rd month after the tax year ends). This earlier deadline allows shareholders to receive Schedule K-1s before their individual return deadlines.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - September 15 is the due date for partnerships (Form 1065) and S corporation returns filed with an extension. This is not the original due date for an S corporation's Form 1120-S.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - Form 1120-S, U.S. Income Tax Return for an S Corporation, is due on March 15 for a calendar-year S corporation. This deadline allows shareholders sufficient time to prepare their individual income tax returns (Form 1040) using the information reported on Schedule K-1 issued by the S corporation.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - April 15 is the due date for individual income tax returns (Form 1040). While shareholders use information from the S corporation to file their individual returns, the S corporation's return is due earlier.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - April 18 is sometimes the due date for individual income tax returns (Form 1040) due to weekend or holiday adjustments. However, the S corporation's return (Form 1120-S) is due on March 15, regardless of these adjustments.

questions.educational: The earlier filing deadline for S corporations (March 15) compared to individuals (April 15) is designed to ensure that shareholders receive their Schedule K-1s in time to accurately report their share of the S corporation's income, deductions, and credits on their individual income tax returns. This timing is crucial for compliance and avoids potential penalties for underreporting or late filing. Understanding the flow-through nature of S corporation income is a key concept tested on the EA exam.
questions.examTip: Remember that the due date for S corporations and partnerships is earlier than the due date for individuals. On the exam, pay close attention to the entity type and the tax year end to determine the correct filing deadline.
questions.memoryAid: S-corps Submit in Spring (March 15).

questions.question: An S election can be voluntarily revoked if shareholders holding more than:
questions.explanation: An S election may be revoked with the consent of shareholders holding more than 50% of the outstanding shares (both voting and nonvoting). The revocation can specify a prospective effective date.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - A revocation of an S election requires more than a simple minority of shareholders. Consent from shareholders holding only 25% of the shares is insufficient to terminate the S corporation status.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - An S corporation election can be voluntarily revoked if shareholders holding more than 50% of the corporation's outstanding shares consent to the revocation. This ensures that a majority of ownership agrees to the change in tax status.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - While unanimous consent would certainly allow for revocation, it is not required. Requiring 100% consent would make revocation unnecessarily difficult, as even a single dissenting shareholder could prevent it.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - While 67% represents a supermajority, the IRS only requires consent from shareholders holding more than 50% of the shares to revoke an S election. This threshold is lower than 67%, making this option incorrect.

questions.educational: The S corporation election allows a business to be taxed similarly to a partnership, avoiding corporate income tax. However, this election can be revoked, effectively returning the entity to C corporation status. The requirement of more than 50% shareholder consent ensures that the decision to revoke is supported by a majority of the ownership, balancing the interests of all shareholders and preventing a small minority from dictating the tax status of the company.
questions.examTip: Remember that the revocation of an S election requires more than 50% consent, but the election itself requires 100% consent of shareholders at the time of election. Pay close attention to the wording of the question to determine whether it's referring to the election or revocation process.
questions.memoryAid: Revocation needs 'MORE than HALF' (50%+) to reverse the S election.

questions.question: Which event would cause automatic termination of S corporation status?
options: Issuing a second class of stock
questions.explanation: S corporation status terminates automatically if the corporation ceases to meet eligibility requirements. Issuing a second class of stock violates the one-class-of-stock requirement and causes immediate termination.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - S corporations are limited to 100 shareholders. Having 99 shareholders is within the allowable limit and does not cause automatic termination.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - An S corporation is permitted to have only one class of stock, although voting rights within that class can differ. Issuing a second class of stock violates this requirement under IRC Section 1361(b)(1)(D) and results in immediate termination of S corporation status.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - The amount of revenue an S corporation earns does not affect its S corporation status. There is no revenue limit that triggers automatic termination.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - An S corporation can have as few as one shareholder. Having only one shareholder does not violate any S corporation requirements and does not cause automatic termination.

questions.educational: S corporations are designed to allow small businesses to operate as corporations while avoiding double taxation. The restrictions on the number and type of shareholders, as well as the one-class-of-stock rule, are in place to ensure that S corporation status is used by smaller, closely held businesses and not larger, publicly traded companies. Understanding these limitations is crucial for advising clients on the suitability of S corporation status.
questions.examTip: Pay close attention to the requirements for S corporation eligibility and the events that can trigger termination. The IRS closely scrutinizes S corporation compliance, so be prepared to advise clients on maintaining their S corporation status.
questions.memoryAid: Think of '100, 1, and NO' - 100 shareholders max, 1 class of stock, and NO ineligible shareholders (like C corps or partnerships).

questions.question: The Accumulated Adjustments Account (AAA) is used to:
options: Determine the tax-free portion of distributions from S corporations with E&P
questions.explanation: The AAA tracks previously taxed S corporation income that can be distributed tax-free. It is important for S corporations that have accumulated earnings and profits from C corporation years, as it determines the ordering of distributions.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is CORRECT - The Accumulated Adjustments Account (AAA) is a corporate-level account that tracks the undistributed net income of an S corporation that has been taxed to the shareholders. Distributions from an S corporation with accumulated earnings and profits (E&P) are tax-free to the extent of the AAA, preventing double taxation of S corporation income.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - The Accumulated Adjustments Account (AAA) has no bearing on the corporate tax rate. Corporate tax rates are determined by federal tax law and applied to C corporations, not S corporations.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - While distributions from the AAA can affect a shareholder's stock basis, the AAA itself does not track the tax basis of shareholders. Shareholder basis is tracked separately by each individual shareholder and is affected by various factors including contributions, distributions, and pass-through income and losses.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - The Accumulated Adjustments Account (AAA) is a corporate-level account and is not used to calculate self-employment tax. Self-employment tax is calculated on an individual's net earnings from self-employment, which for S corporation shareholders, is generally limited to wages received as an employee of the S corporation.

questions.educational: The AAA is crucial for S corporations with prior C corporation earnings and profits (E&P). Without the AAA, distributions could be taxed as dividends (due to the E&P), even though the S corporation's income has already been taxed to the shareholders. Understanding the AAA is essential for proper tax planning and compliance for S corporations, a frequent topic on the SEE exam.
questions.examTip: On the EA exam, pay close attention to the ordering rules for S corporation distributions, especially when E&P is present. Remember that distributions come first from the AAA, then from E&P, and finally as a return of capital.
questions.memoryAid: AAA: Avoids Additional Assessment (of taxes on S-corp distributions).

questions.question: Most farmers may use the cash method of accounting:
options: Only for the first 5 years of operation
Only if gross receipts are under $1 million
Only if they do not have inventory
questions.explanation: Farming businesses can generally use the cash method of accounting regardless of their gross receipts. This is a special exception for farmers, though C corporations engaged in farming must use accrual if gross receipts exceed $27 million.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is CORRECT - Generally, farming businesses, including individuals, partnerships, and S corporations, can use the cash method of accounting regardless of their gross receipts. This is a specific exception under IRC Section 447 and 448 designed to simplify accounting for farmers, recognizing the unique challenges of agricultural businesses.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - There is no limitation on the number of years a farmer can use the cash method. If eligible, they can use it indefinitely, not just for the first 5 years of operation.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - While a gross receipts test exists for many businesses to determine eligibility for the cash method, this limitation generally does not apply to farming businesses. The option is incorrect because farmers can use the cash method regardless of whether their gross receipts are under $1 million (unless they are a C corporation with gross receipts exceeding $27 million).
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - While inventory can complicate accounting, farmers are generally allowed to use the cash method even if they have inventory. The presence of inventory does not automatically disqualify a farming business from using the cash method.

questions.educational: The cash method of accounting recognizes income when received and expenses when paid, offering simplicity. Farming businesses receive a special exception to use the cash method due to the complexities and uncertainties inherent in agricultural operations, such as weather dependency and fluctuating commodity prices. This exception acknowledges the practical difficulties farmers face in accurately tracking inventory and matching revenues with expenses under the accrual method.
questions.examTip: Remember the special rules for farmers regarding accounting methods. While most businesses face gross receipts limitations for using the cash method, farming businesses generally do not, unless they are a C corporation exceeding the gross receipts threshold. Pay close attention to the entity type in the question stem.
questions.memoryAid: Farmers are 'cash cows' - they often get to use the cash method regardless of receipts.

questions.question: Farm income averaging allows farmers to:
options: Elect to have current year farm income taxed at lower rates by averaging over prior 3 years
Exclude farm income from self-employment tax
questions.explanation: Schedule J allows farmers to average the current year's farm income over the three prior years, potentially taxing it at lower marginal rates if income fluctuates significantly year to year.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - Farm income averaging has nothing to do with depreciation methods. Depreciation is a separate calculation used to deduct the cost of assets over their useful life, and the choice of depreciation method (e.g., accelerated vs. straight-line) is independent of income averaging.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - Farm income averaging, as detailed on Schedule J (Form 1040), allows qualifying farmers and fishermen to elect to spread a portion of their current year's farm income back over the previous three years. This can result in a lower overall tax liability if the farmer's income fluctuates significantly, as it can shift income from a high-income year to lower-income years, potentially reducing the tax rate applied to that income.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - Farm income averaging does not defer income indefinitely. It only allows income to be shifted to prior years for tax calculation purposes. The income is still recognized in the current year, but the tax is calculated as if it were earned in the prior three years.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - Farm income averaging does not exclude farm income from self-employment tax. All net earnings from self-employment, including farm income, are subject to self-employment tax (Social Security and Medicare). Farm income averaging only affects the income tax calculation, not the self-employment tax calculation.

questions.educational: Farm income averaging (Schedule J) is a specific provision designed to alleviate the tax burden on farmers whose income can vary significantly from year to year due to factors like weather, market prices, and crop yields. By averaging income, farmers can avoid being pushed into higher tax brackets in unusually profitable years, which aligns with the broader tax principle of fairness and economic stability for agricultural businesses.
questions.examTip: Remember that farm income averaging is an election made on Schedule J and only impacts income tax, not self-employment tax. Be prepared to identify scenarios where it would be beneficial for a farmer based on fluctuating income levels.
questions.memoryAid: FIA = Farm Income Averaging. Think: 'Fair Income Adjustment' for farmers.

questions.question: A simple trust is one that:
options: Has no more than $100,000 in assets
Must distribute all income currently and cannot make charitable contributions
questions.explanation: A simple trust is required to distribute all income currently, makes no distributions of principal, and makes no charitable contributions. If any of these conditions are not met, the trust is a complex trust.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - A simple trust can have multiple beneficiaries. The number of beneficiaries is not a determining factor in classifying a trust as simple or complex.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - The value of the assets held by the trust is not a criterion for determining whether a trust is simple or complex. There is no asset limit for simple trusts.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is CORRECT - A simple trust, as defined under IRC Subchapter J, *must* distribute all of its income currently. It also cannot make distributions of the trust's principal (corpus) or make charitable contributions. If the trust instrument allows for accumulation of income, distributions of principal, or charitable contributions, it is classified as a complex trust.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - Simple trusts are not exempt from income tax. While the beneficiaries are taxed on the income distributed to them, the trust itself may also be subject to tax on any income not required to be distributed. The trust acts as a conduit, but it still files Form 1041.

questions.educational: Trusts are governed by Subchapter J of the Internal Revenue Code and are classified as either simple or complex based on their distribution requirements. Simple trusts act as a pass-through entity for income, ensuring beneficiaries are taxed on the earnings. Understanding the nuances of trust taxation is crucial for Enrolled Agents, as they advise clients on estate planning and minimizing tax liabilities related to trusts.
questions.examTip: On the EA exam, pay close attention to the wording of trust questions. Focus on whether the trust *must* distribute all income currently, as this is the key characteristic of a simple trust.
questions.memoryAid: SIC: Simple = Income Currently distributed.

questions.question: An estate must file Form 1041 if it has:
questions.explanation: An estate must file Form 1041 if it has gross income of $600 or more for the tax year, or if any beneficiary is a nonresident alien. The estate's tax year can be a calendar year or fiscal year.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - The value of the estate's assets is not a determining factor for the requirement to file Form 1041. The filing requirement is based on the estate's gross income, not its total assets.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - While any income can trigger a filing requirement in certain situations (such as a nonresident alien beneficiary), the general rule for domestic estates is based on a specific gross income threshold. 'Any income' is too broad and doesn't reflect the actual threshold.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is CORRECT - An estate is required to file Form 1041, U.S. Income Tax Return for Estates and Trusts, if it has gross income of $600 or more for the tax year, regardless of the amount of taxable income. This threshold is set by the IRS to ensure that estates with significant activity are properly accounted for.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - The threshold for requiring an estate to file Form 1041 is $600 of gross income, not $1,000. This lower threshold ensures that more estates are required to report their income and pay any applicable taxes.

questions.educational: The requirement for estates to file Form 1041 is designed to ensure that income earned by the estate during the administration process is properly reported and taxed. Estates are considered separate taxable entities, and the $600 threshold is a de minimis level established by the IRS to balance compliance with administrative burden. Understanding these filing requirements is crucial for Enrolled Agents advising executors and administrators of estates.
questions.examTip: Remember the $600 gross income threshold for estate tax return filing (Form 1041) – it's a frequently tested point on the SEE exam. Also, be aware of the exception for nonresident alien beneficiaries, which triggers a filing requirement regardless of income level.
questions.memoryAid: Think of '600' as 'Six Hundred for the Soon-to-be-Gone' (estate).

questions.question: 501(c)(3) organizations with gross receipts of $200,000 or more must file:
questions.explanation: Tax-exempt organizations with gross receipts of $200,000 or more, or total assets of $500,000 or more, must file Form 990. Smaller organizations may file Form 990-EZ or Form 990-N.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - Form 990-EZ is a shorter version of Form 990 and is used by smaller tax-exempt organizations. While an organization with gross receipts of $200,000 or more might have used Form 990-EZ in the past, current IRS guidelines require Form 990 for organizations exceeding this threshold or having total assets of $500,000 or more.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - Form 990-N (e-Postcard) is for very small tax-exempt organizations, specifically those with gross receipts normally not more than $50,000. Since the question specifies gross receipts of $200,000 or more, Form 990-N is not the correct form.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - Form 1120 is the U.S. Corporate Income Tax Return, used by for-profit corporations to report their income, deductions, and calculate their income tax liability. 501(c)(3) organizations are tax-exempt and generally do not file Form 1120 unless they have unrelated business income (UBI). The question doesn't mention UBI, and the primary filing requirement is for information reporting, not income tax reporting.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is CORRECT - Form 990, Return of Organization Exempt From Income Tax, is the correct form for 501(c)(3) organizations with gross receipts of $200,000 or more, or total assets of $500,000 or more. This form provides the IRS and the public with information about the organization's mission, programs, and finances, ensuring transparency and accountability.

questions.educational: Form 990 serves as a crucial tool for transparency and accountability within the non-profit sector. By requiring larger tax-exempt organizations to disclose their financial activities, the IRS and the public can assess whether these organizations are operating in accordance with their stated missions and adhering to relevant regulations. This aligns with the broader goal of maintaining public trust and ensuring the integrity of the tax-exempt system, a key area covered in the SEE2 Business Taxation section.
questions.examTip: On the EA exam, pay close attention to the financial thresholds (gross receipts and total assets) that trigger different filing requirements for tax-exempt organizations. Knowing these thresholds will help you quickly eliminate incorrect answer choices.
questions.memoryAid: 990 for the BIG non-profits (over $200k gross receipts or $500k assets); N for the 'Nearly Nothing' (under $50k) ones.

questions.question: Unrelated Business Income Tax (UBIT) applies to:
options: Income from a trade or business regularly carried on that is not substantially related to the exempt purpose
questions.explanation: UBIT is imposed on income from a trade or business that is regularly carried on and not substantially related to the organization's exempt purpose. Common exemptions include investment income, volunteer-run activities, and thrift shops.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - UBIT does not apply to *all* income of tax-exempt organizations. Many types of income, such as donations and income related to the organization's exempt purpose, are excluded. UBIT only targets income from activities unrelated to the exempt purpose that are regularly carried on as a trade or business.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - Unrelated Business Income Tax (UBIT) is specifically designed to tax income generated by a tax-exempt organization from a trade or business that is regularly carried on and is not substantially related to the organization's exempt purpose. This prevents unfair competition with taxable businesses.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - The amount of donations received by a tax-exempt organization does not directly trigger UBIT. While large donations may have other tax implications, UBIT is concerned with income from unrelated business activities, not the size of contributions.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - Investment income, such as dividends, interest, and rents from real property, is generally excluded from UBIT under IRC Section 512(b), unless the investment activity itself constitutes an unrelated trade or business (e.g., debt-financed property).

questions.educational: UBIT exists to prevent tax-exempt organizations from gaining an unfair competitive advantage over for-profit businesses. By taxing income derived from activities unrelated to their exempt purpose, the tax code aims to level the playing field. Understanding UBIT is crucial for Enrolled Agents advising non-profit clients, as it directly impacts their tax compliance and planning strategies.
questions.examTip: When calculating UBIT, remember to consider the $1,000 specific deduction allowed under IRC Section 512(b)(12) before applying the corporate tax rates. This can significantly reduce the UBIT liability.
questions.memoryAid: UBIT = Unrelated, Business, Income, Tax. Focus on those three key elements to determine if income is subject to UBIT.

questions.question: Under the passive activity rules, an individual who actively participates in rental real estate can deduct up to how much of rental losses against non-passive income?
questions.explanation: Individuals who actively participate in rental real estate activities can deduct up to $25,000 of rental losses against non-passive income. This allowance phases out between $100,000 and $150,000 of modified AGI.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - $10,000 is not the correct amount that can be deducted against non-passive income for actively participating in rental real estate activities. The correct amount is $25,000, subject to AGI limitations.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - $15,000 is not the correct amount that can be deducted against non-passive income for actively participating in rental real estate activities. The correct amount is $25,000, subject to AGI limitations.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - $50,000 is not the correct amount that can be deducted against non-passive income for actively participating in rental real estate activities. The correct amount is $25,000, subject to AGI limitations. This amount might be confused with other deductions or credits.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is CORRECT - Individuals who actively participate in rental real estate activities can deduct up to $25,000 of rental losses against non-passive income. Active participation requires the individual to be involved in management decisions or arranging for others to provide services. This deduction is phased out for taxpayers with modified adjusted gross income (MAGI) between $100,000 and $150,000.

questions.educational: The passive activity loss rules, governed by IRC Section 469, were enacted to prevent taxpayers from using losses from passive activities to offset income from active businesses or investments. Rental real estate is generally considered a passive activity, but the $25,000 allowance for active participants provides relief, recognizing the common practice of individuals managing their own rental properties. Understanding these rules is crucial for accurately determining a client's taxable income and potential tax liabilities.
questions.examTip: Remember the AGI phase-out range ($100,000 - $150,000) for the $25,000 rental real estate loss allowance. A question might ask about the deductible loss given a specific AGI within or outside this range.
questions.memoryAid: Think of '25 grand for hands-on land' to remember the $25,000 deduction for actively participating in rental real estate.

questions.question: Under current tax law, entertainment expenses are:
questions.explanation: The Tax Cuts and Jobs Act eliminated the deduction for entertainment expenses. Business meals remain 50% deductible, but expenses for entertainment, amusement, or recreation are no longer deductible.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - The 50% deduction applies to business meals, not entertainment expenses. The Tax Cuts and Jobs Act (TCJA) specifically eliminated the deduction for entertainment expenses, so this percentage is no longer applicable.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - While some business expenses are 100% deductible, entertainment expenses are not among them. The TCJA eliminated the deduction for entertainment expenses, making this option incorrect.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - The 80% deduction used to apply to certain meal and entertainment expenses prior to 2018, but the TCJA changed this. Currently, entertainment expenses are not deductible at all, and business meals are 50% deductible, making this option incorrect.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is CORRECT - The Tax Cuts and Jobs Act (TCJA) of 2017 eliminated the deduction for entertainment expenses, including those related to amusement or recreation. This change significantly impacted businesses, as these expenses were previously deductible under certain conditions.

questions.educational: The disallowance of entertainment expense deductions stems from a desire to simplify tax law and address perceived abuses of the prior rules. Before the TCJA, complex rules governed the deductibility of entertainment, often requiring a direct connection to business discussions. By eliminating the deduction, Congress aimed to create a clearer and more easily enforceable standard, aligning with the broader goal of tax simplification.
questions.examTip: Remember that while entertainment expenses are generally not deductible, business meals are still 50% deductible, provided they are ordinary and necessary and the taxpayer (or an employee) is present. Pay close attention to the nuances between 'entertainment' and 'business meals' on the exam.
questions.memoryAid: No Entertainment Ever (NEE) - No Entertainment Expenses are deductible.

questions.question: Nonresidential real property is depreciated using MACRS over:
questions.explanation: Nonresidential real property (commercial buildings) is depreciated over 39 years using the straight-line method under MACRS. Residential rental property is depreciated over 27.5 years.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - 31.5 years was the recovery period for nonresidential real property placed in service before May 13, 1993. The current recovery period is 39 years.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - 27.5 years is the recovery period for residential rental property under MACRS, not nonresidential real property.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - 40 years is the recovery period under the Alternative Depreciation System (ADS) for nonresidential real property. The question specifically asks about MACRS, not ADS.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is CORRECT - Under the Modified Accelerated Cost Recovery System (MACRS), nonresidential real property, such as commercial buildings, is depreciated using the straight-line method over a recovery period of 39 years. This applies to property placed in service after December 31, 1986.

questions.educational: Depreciation is a crucial concept for business taxation, allowing businesses to deduct the cost of assets over their useful lives. MACRS aims to provide a standardized and accelerated depreciation schedule, encouraging investment. The longer recovery period for nonresidential real property reflects its typically longer economic life compared to residential rental property.
questions.examTip: Remember the depreciation periods for different types of property under MACRS, as the exam frequently tests the correct recovery period for assets like residential rental property (27.5 years) and nonresidential real property (39 years).
questions.memoryAid: Think of '39' as 'business fine' because commercial buildings are often used by businesses, and '27.5' as 'rent alive' because residential rental properties are where people live.

questions.question: A business using the accrual method can deduct a bad debt when:
options: Only when the debt is sold to a collection agency
An invoice is 30 days past due
The debt becomes wholly or partially worthless
questions.explanation: Business bad debts can be deducted when they become wholly or partially worthless. Cash basis businesses generally cannot deduct bad debts because the income was never included in income when not collected.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - Selling the debt to a collection agency is not the determining factor for deducting a bad debt. The key is the worthlessness of the debt, not its transfer to another party. The business may still have a bad debt deduction even if they don't sell the debt.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - While partial worthlessness can trigger a bad debt deduction, this option is incomplete. The debt must be deemed worthless, not just partially uncollectible. Uncollectible implies difficulty in collecting, while worthless implies no value.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - An invoice being 30 days past due is not sufficient to claim a bad debt deduction. Delinquency is a factor, but the debt must be deemed wholly or partially worthless based on specific facts and circumstances, not just the passage of time.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is CORRECT - Accrual method taxpayers can deduct bad debts when they become wholly or partially worthless because they have already recognized the income associated with the receivable. This aligns with the matching principle, allowing expenses to be recognized in the same period as the related revenue.

questions.educational: The bad debt deduction allows accrual basis taxpayers to offset previously recognized income when a receivable becomes uncollectible. This deduction is crucial for accurately reflecting a business's true profitability. The IRS requires taxpayers to demonstrate the worthlessness of the debt, often through documentation of collection efforts or the debtor's financial condition.
questions.examTip: Remember that the key to deducting a bad debt is proving its worthlessness; keep detailed records of collection attempts and debtor financial status to support your deduction on Form 1120 or Schedule C.
questions.memoryAid: BAD: Business Accrual Debts are Deductible when Worthless.

questions.question: A self-employed individual can deduct 100% of health insurance premiums:
options: As a business expense on Schedule C
Only if the business has a net profit
questions.explanation: Self-employed individuals can deduct 100% of health insurance premiums for themselves, spouse, and dependents as an adjustment to income, limited to net self-employment income. It is not deducted on Schedule C.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - While a self-employed individual's business activities are reported on Schedule C, the health insurance deduction for self-employed individuals is an adjustment to gross income, not a business expense directly reducing business profit on Schedule C. This is a separate deduction taken on Schedule 1 of Form 1040.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - Self-employed individuals can deduct health insurance premiums paid for themselves, their spouse, and dependents as an adjustment to gross income (above-the-line) on Schedule 1 of Form 1040. This deduction reduces adjusted gross income (AGI), which can impact eligibility for other tax benefits.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - The self-employed health insurance deduction is an adjustment to gross income, not an itemized deduction. Itemized deductions are reported on Schedule A and are taken instead of the standard deduction. The adjustment to income is more beneficial as it reduces AGI regardless of whether the taxpayer itemizes.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - While the deduction is limited to the self-employment income derived from the trade or business that established the plan, the ability to deduct the health insurance premiums isn't solely contingent on the business having a net profit. The deduction can still be taken up to the amount of self-employment income, even if the overall business operates at a loss. The deduction cannot exceed the taxpayer's self-employment income.

questions.educational: The self-employed health insurance deduction is an incentive to encourage self-employed individuals to obtain health insurance coverage. By allowing this deduction as an adjustment to gross income, Congress aims to reduce the tax burden on self-employed individuals and promote access to healthcare. This deduction directly impacts the calculation of adjusted gross income (AGI), a crucial figure used to determine eligibility for various other tax credits and deductions, such as the child tax credit or deductions for student loan interest.
questions.examTip: Remember that the self-employed health insurance deduction is limited to the amount of self-employment income and cannot create a net operating loss (NOL). Be prepared to calculate the deductible amount based on provided income and premium information.
questions.memoryAid: Think "Above the Line, Health is Fine!" to remember that the self-employed health insurance deduction is an adjustment to income (above the line).

questions.question: The mid-quarter convention for MACRS depreciation applies when:
