This transaction triggers a capital gain or loss, reflecting the difference between the fair market value of the assets received and the shareholder's basis in the stock. Understanding the tax implications of liquidations is crucial for advising clients on business structuring and exit strategies.
questions.examTip: Remember that liquidations are generally taxable at the shareholder level, and the gain or loss is capital in nature. Pay close attention to the shareholder's basis in the stock and the fair market value of the assets received to calculate the correct gain or loss.
questions.memoryAid: Liquidations = Like Selling (stock back to the company)

questions.question: A shareholder's basis in property received in a complete liquidation is:
options: The fair market value of the property at distribution
The corporation's basis in the property
Zero until the property is sold
The shareholder's basis in the stock surrendered
questions.explanation: Under IRC §334(a), a shareholder receiving property in a complete liquidation takes a basis equal to the fair market value of the property at the time of distribution.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is CORRECT - When a corporation liquidates completely, shareholders are treated as having sold their stock back to the corporation. Under IRC §334(a), the shareholder's basis in the property received is its fair market value at the time of distribution, reflecting the price they would pay for it in an arm's-length transaction.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - The corporation's basis in the property is irrelevant to the shareholder's basis after a complete liquidation. The shareholder is essentially purchasing the asset at its fair market value, establishing a new basis independent of the corporation's historical cost.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - The shareholder's basis is not zero until the property is sold. The basis is established immediately upon distribution at the fair market value, which is crucial for calculating gain or loss when the shareholder eventually disposes of the property.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - While the shareholder's stock basis is important for determining the gain or loss recognized on the liquidation, it does not determine the basis of the assets received. The asset's basis is determined by its fair market value at distribution, not the shareholder's investment in the corporation.

questions.educational: Corporate liquidations represent a significant event, effectively terminating the corporation's existence. The tax code aims to treat these transactions as if the shareholders sold their stock and purchased the assets at their fair market value. This ensures that any unrealized appreciation or depreciation is taxed at the shareholder level, aligning with the principle of taxing all economic gains and losses.
questions.examTip: Remember that in complete liquidations, the shareholder's basis in distributed property is always fair market value. This is a common area for calculation questions on the SEE exam, so practice applying this rule.
questions.memoryAid: Liquidation = Like-New Basis (Fair Market Value)

questions.question: For a tax-free liquidation of a subsidiary under IRC §332, the parent corporation must own:
options: More than 50% of the subsidiary's stock
At least 80% of voting power and 80% of value of all stock
At least two-thirds of all outstanding shares
questions.explanation: Under IRC §332(b), the parent must own at least 80% of total combined voting power AND at least 80% of total value of all outstanding stock (excluding nonvoting, nonparticipating preferred).

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - While owning more than 50% grants control, IRC §332 requires a higher threshold of ownership for a tax-free liquidation. The 80% ownership test ensures substantial economic alignment between the parent and subsidiary.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - IRC §332(b)(1) explicitly states that the parent corporation must own at least 80% of the total combined voting power and at least 80% of the total value of all outstanding stock of the subsidiary to qualify for a tax-free liquidation. This requirement ensures a significant economic interest and control by the parent.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - A two-thirds ownership (approximately 66.67%) does not meet the specific 80% ownership threshold required by IRC §332 for a tax-free liquidation. The statute is very specific about the 80% threshold.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - While 100% ownership would certainly qualify under IRC §332, it is not the minimum requirement. The statute allows for some minority ownership, as long as the 80% thresholds are met.

questions.educational: IRC §332 allows for the tax-free liquidation of a subsidiary into its parent corporation, preventing double taxation on the subsidiary's assets. This provision encourages corporate restructuring and simplification. The 80% ownership requirement ensures that the parent has sufficient control and economic interest in the subsidiary to justify the tax-free treatment; otherwise, it would be treated as a taxable sale.
questions.examTip: Remember the 80% ownership test for both voting power and value when dealing with corporate liquidations on the EA exam. Pay close attention to the wording of the question to identify whether it's asking about a §332 liquidation.
questions.memoryAid: Think '80/80' for Section 332: 80% voting power and 80% value.

questions.question: In a Section 332 liquidation of a subsidiary, the parent corporation:
options: Takes a FMV basis in assets received
Recognizes no gain or loss on the liquidation
Recognizes gain to the extent FMV exceeds basis
questions.explanation: Under IRC §332(a), a parent receiving property in a complete liquidation of an 80%-owned subsidiary recognizes no gain or loss. Under IRC §334(b), the parent takes a carryover basis in assets.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - In a Section 332 liquidation, the parent corporation takes a carryover basis in the assets received from the subsidiary, not a fair market value (FMV) basis. Section 334(b)(1) dictates the carryover basis rule.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - Section 332(a) of the Internal Revenue Code explicitly states that no gain or loss is recognized by a parent corporation upon the receipt of property in a complete liquidation of a subsidiary, provided the parent owns at least 80% of the subsidiary's stock. This non-recognition rule is a key element of Section 332 liquidations.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - Section 332 is designed to allow tax-free liquidations of subsidiaries into their parent corporations. Recognizing gain to the extent FMV exceeds basis would contradict the purpose of Section 332.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - Section 332(a) clearly states that *neither* gain *nor* loss is recognized by the parent corporation in a qualifying liquidation. This option incorrectly suggests that a loss could be recognized.

questions.educational: Section 332 liquidations facilitate the tax-free reorganization of corporate structures. The rationale behind this provision is to allow a parent corporation to absorb a subsidiary without triggering immediate tax consequences, promoting business efficiency. This is related to corporate reorganizations, which are also generally tax-free, and contrasts with taxable liquidations where gains and losses are recognized.
questions.examTip: Remember that Section 332 liquidations require the parent to own at least 80% of the subsidiary's stock. If the 80% ownership test isn't met, the liquidation will be taxable, and the parent will recognize gain or loss.
questions.memoryAid: 332: Tax-Free To You (Parent Corporation)

questions.question: In a Section 332 liquidation, the subsidiary corporation:
options: Recognizes no gain or loss on distribution to the 80% parent
Files a final return showing capital gain
Recognizes all gains and losses on distributed assets
questions.explanation: Under IRC §337(a), a subsidiary recognizes no gain or loss on distributions to an 80% distributee in a Section 332 liquidation. However, gain may be recognized on distributions to minority shareholders.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is CORRECT - In a Section 332 liquidation, IRC §337(a) explicitly states that the subsidiary corporation recognizes no gain or loss on the distribution of property to its 80% or more corporate parent. This non-recognition rule is a key component of tax-free corporate reorganizations and liquidations.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - A Section 332 liquidation is designed to be tax-free at the subsidiary level when distributing assets to an 80% or more corporate parent; therefore, the subsidiary does not recognize capital gain and would not report it on a final return in this scenario.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - Section 337(a) specifically prevents the subsidiary from recognizing all gains and losses on distributed assets to the 80% parent in a Section 332 liquidation. This is the core principle that makes the liquidation tax-free at the subsidiary level.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - While a subsidiary might recognize gain in other liquidation scenarios, Section 337(a) dictates that in a Section 332 liquidation involving an 80% or more corporate parent, the subsidiary recognizes neither gain nor loss on the distribution of assets.

questions.educational: Section 332 liquidations are designed to allow the tax-free winding up of a subsidiary corporation into its parent. This facilitates corporate restructuring and simplifies group structures without triggering immediate tax consequences. The non-recognition rules at the subsidiary level (Section 337) and the parent level (Section 332) are crucial for understanding corporate reorganizations and liquidations covered in the Business Taxation section of the SEE exam.
questions.examTip: Remember that the 80% ownership threshold is critical for a Section 332 liquidation to qualify for tax-free treatment at the subsidiary level; if the parent owns less than 80%, the liquidation will be taxable.
questions.memoryAid: 332 = Tax-Free To Parent (and Subsidiary). Think of '332' as a code for a tax-free corporate handshake.

questions.question: In a general liquidation (non-§332), a corporation distributes property with built-in loss to a related party. The loss is:
options: Recognized only to the extent of built-in gain in other property
Deferred until the related party sells the property
questions.explanation: Under IRC §336(d)(1), no loss is recognized on liquidating distributions to related parties (>50% shareholders). The loss is permanently disallowed.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - IRC §336(d) disallows the loss entirely; it is not limited to the extent of built-in gain in other property. The presence of built-in gain in other property does not change the disallowance rule for distributions to related parties.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - The loss is not deferred; it is permanently disallowed under IRC §336(d). Deferral implies eventual recognition, which is not the case here.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - IRC §336(d) specifically prevents full recognition of the loss when distributing loss property to a related party in a complete liquidation. The purpose of this rule is to prevent tax avoidance.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is CORRECT - IRC §336(d)(1) directly addresses this scenario, stating that no loss is recognized by a liquidating corporation on the distribution of property to a related person if the distribution is not pro rata or the property is disqualified property (property acquired during the 5-year period ending on the date of the distribution, or the principal purpose of the contribution was to recognize loss). This rule prevents corporations from artificially creating losses through distributions to related parties.

questions.educational: IRC §336(d) aims to prevent tax avoidance in corporate liquidations by disallowing losses on distributions of certain property to related parties. This provision ensures that corporations cannot manipulate asset transfers to generate artificial losses. Understanding the related party rules and the definition of disqualified property is crucial for accurately calculating taxable income in corporate liquidations, a key area in the SEE2 exam.
questions.examTip: On the EA exam, carefully examine the relationship between the shareholder and the corporation in liquidation scenarios, and always check for the presence of built-in losses and the timing of property acquisition to determine if §336(d) applies.
questions.memoryAid: 336(d): 'Don't Distribute Disallowed Deductions to Dudes (related parties)!'

questions.question: If property is contributed to a corporation and the corporation liquidates within 2 years, built-in loss on that property:
options: May be disallowed under anti-stuffing rules if tax avoidance is present
questions.explanation: Under IRC §336(d)(2), built-in loss on property contributed within 2 years of liquidation may be disallowed if the contribution was part of a plan with a principal purpose of tax avoidance (anti-stuffing rule).

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - A built-in loss is not automatically converted to a capital loss upon liquidation. The character of the loss depends on the nature of the asset and its use in the business, subject to potential disallowance under anti-stuffing rules.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - While built-in losses are generally recognized in a corporate liquidation, IRC §336(d)(2) specifically provides an exception for property contributed within two years of liquidation if the principal purpose of the contribution was tax avoidance. In such cases, the loss may be disallowed, not recognized in full.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is CORRECT - IRC §336(d)(2) contains the anti-stuffing rules, which prevent taxpayers from contributing loss property to a corporation shortly before liquidation to generate artificial losses. If the contribution was part of a plan to avoid tax, the built-in loss may be disallowed, making this the correct answer.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - There is no provision in the tax code that doubles a built-in loss for tax purposes in the context of a corporate liquidation. This is a distractor.

questions.educational: The anti-stuffing rules under IRC §336(d)(2) are designed to prevent taxpayers from artificially inflating losses in a corporate liquidation by contributing loss property shortly beforehand. These rules are part of a broader effort to prevent tax avoidance and ensure that liquidations reflect genuine economic losses. Understanding these rules is crucial for accurately calculating taxable income and advising clients on the tax implications of corporate liquidations.
questions.examTip: When analyzing corporate liquidations on the EA exam, always be alert for related-party transactions or contributions of property shortly before liquidation, as these may trigger the anti-stuffing rules and require further investigation.
questions.memoryAid: 2-Year Rule: If it's been less than 2 years, tax avoidance fears!

questions.question: When a corporation distributes an installment obligation in a complete liquidation:
options: The corporation recognizes the full remaining gain immediately
The installment method is always terminated
The shareholder continues the installment method in certain situations
questions.explanation: Under IRC §453(h), shareholders receiving installment obligations in liquidation of a qualifying corporation can continue the installment method, reporting gain as payments are received.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - While a corporation generally recognizes gain or loss on the distribution of property in a complete liquidation as if the property were sold at its fair market value, IRC §453(h) provides an exception for installment obligations. The corporation does not automatically recognize the full remaining gain if the shareholder can continue the installment method.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - The installment method is not always terminated. IRC §453(h) specifically allows shareholders to continue using the installment method under certain conditions when they receive installment obligations in a corporate liquidation.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is CORRECT - IRC §453(h) allows shareholders receiving installment obligations in a complete liquidation of a corporation to continue using the installment method, reporting gain as they receive payments on the obligation. This allows the shareholder to defer tax liability until actual cash is received, mirroring the treatment the corporation would have had if it continued to hold the obligation.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - The installment obligation does not automatically become worthless simply because it is distributed in a corporate liquidation. The obligation retains its value and the shareholder steps into the shoes of the corporation for purposes of collecting on the obligation.

questions.educational: The installment method allows taxpayers to defer recognizing gain until they receive cash payments. When a corporation liquidates and distributes an installment obligation, the shareholders can continue using the installment method, preventing immediate taxation on the deferred gain. This rule aims to provide tax parity, allowing shareholders to be taxed in a similar manner as if the corporation had continued to hold the installment obligation.
questions.examTip: Remember that the installment sale rules under IRC §453 can significantly impact the timing of income recognition in corporate liquidations. Be prepared to calculate the amount of gain recognized by the shareholder each year as payments are received on the installment obligation.
questions.memoryAid: SILO: Shareholders Installment Liquidation Obligation - helps remember the shareholder can continue the installment method upon liquidation.

questions.question: When a subsidiary liquidates into its parent under Section 332:
options: Tax attributes transfer to shareholders proportionally
All tax attributes of the subsidiary are lost
The parent succeeds to the subsidiary's tax attributes
questions.explanation: Under IRC §381, when a subsidiary liquidates under Section 332, the parent corporation succeeds to many of the subsidiary's tax attributes, including NOL carryovers, capital loss carryovers, accounting methods, etc.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - Tax attributes do not transfer proportionally to shareholders in a Section 332 liquidation. Section 332 specifically deals with the liquidation of a subsidiary into a parent corporation, not distributions to individual shareholders.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - All tax attributes of the subsidiary are not lost. Section 381 provides that the parent corporation succeeds to many of the subsidiary's tax attributes, preventing their disappearance.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is CORRECT - Under IRC §381, the parent corporation in a Section 332 liquidation succeeds to the subsidiary's tax attributes. This includes items like NOL carryovers, earnings and profits, capital loss carryovers, and accounting methods, ensuring a continuation of the subsidiary's tax history within the parent.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - While NOL carryovers are among the tax attributes that transfer, they are not the *only* attributes that transfer. Section 381 specifies a range of tax attributes that the parent corporation inherits.

questions.educational: Section 332 liquidations allow a parent corporation to liquidate a subsidiary without recognizing gain or loss, provided certain conditions are met (e.g., the parent owns at least 80% of the subsidiary's stock). Section 381 complements this by ensuring the subsidiary's tax attributes are preserved within the parent, preventing potential tax avoidance and maintaining the integrity of the tax system. This is important for understanding consolidated tax returns and corporate reorganizations.
questions.examTip: Remember that Section 332 liquidations and Section 381 attribute carryovers often appear together on the EA exam. Be prepared to identify which tax attributes transfer and the conditions that trigger these sections.
questions.memoryAid: 332: Parent gets the subsidiary, tax-free. 381: Parent gets the tax attributes, too! (332 & 381 go together)

questions.question: Under MACRS, office furniture and fixtures are depreciated over:
questions.explanation: Under MACRS, office furniture and fixtures are 7-year property. Common recovery periods: 3-year (some manufacturing tools), 5-year (computers, vehicles), 7-year (furniture, equipment), 27.5-year (residential rental), 39-year (nonresidential).

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - 15-year property generally applies to assets like qualified leasehold improvements, qualified restaurant property, and retail improvements. Office furniture and fixtures do not fall into these categories.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - 3-year property is typically reserved for specific assets with very short useful lives, such as certain racehorses or some special tools used in manufacturing. Office furniture and fixtures have a longer expected useful life than 3 years.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - 5-year property includes assets like computers, automobiles, and light trucks. While these items might be found in an office, the office furniture itself is classified differently under MACRS.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is CORRECT - Under MACRS (Modified Accelerated Cost Recovery System), office furniture and fixtures are classified as 7-year property. This classification is based on the asset's expected useful life and is used to determine the annual depreciation expense for tax purposes. The 7-year recovery period is defined in IRS Publication 946, How to Depreciate Property.

questions.educational: The MACRS depreciation system aims to provide a standardized method for businesses to recover the cost of assets over their useful lives. By assigning different recovery periods to various asset types, MACRS allows for a more accurate reflection of an asset's decline in value and ensures consistent tax treatment across different businesses. Understanding these classifications is crucial for accurately calculating taxable income and minimizing tax liabilities.
questions.examTip: On the EA exam, pay close attention to the specific asset being depreciated and its use. Memorizing the common recovery periods (3, 5, 7, 27.5, and 39 years) will help you quickly identify the correct depreciation period for various assets.
questions.memoryAid: Furniture in the office? Think '7' years of comfort.

questions.question: For 2024, the maximum Section 179 expense deduction is:
questions.explanation: The Section 179 limit for 2024 is $1,160,000 (indexed for inflation). The deduction phases out dollar-for-dollar when total qualifying property exceeds $2,890,000. It cannot exceed business income.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - $500,000 was the Section 179 limit many years ago, but it has been significantly increased and indexed for inflation. Using outdated information is a common trap on the EA exam.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - $1,050,000 is close to the correct answer, but it is not the accurate inflation-adjusted amount for the 2024 tax year. Remember to use the most up-to-date figures provided by the IRS.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - $2,000,000 is significantly higher than the actual Section 179 expense deduction limit for 2024. This number may be a distractor based on other, unrelated tax thresholds.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is CORRECT - The maximum Section 179 expense deduction for qualifying property placed in service during the 2024 tax year is $1,160,000. This amount is indexed for inflation annually, so it's crucial to use the correct year's limit.

questions.educational: Section 179 allows businesses to immediately deduct the cost of qualifying property, rather than depreciating it over several years. This incentive encourages investment in capital assets, stimulating economic growth. The deduction is subject to limitations, including a dollar limit and a taxable income limitation, to prevent abuse and ensure it primarily benefits small and medium-sized businesses.
questions.examTip: Pay close attention to the tax year specified in the question, as Section 179 limits and phase-out thresholds are adjusted annually for inflation. The IRS publishes these figures in Publication 946 and related guidance.
questions.memoryAid: Think 'One-Seventy-Nine, One-Point-One-Six-O' to remember the $1,160,000 limit for 2024.

questions.question: A business has taxable income (before Section 179) of $40,000 and purchases $100,000 of qualifying equipment. The maximum Section 179 deduction is:
questions.explanation: The Section 179 deduction cannot exceed the taxpayer's taxable income from active business operations. Here, the deduction is limited to $40,000. The $60,000 disallowed amount carries forward.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - While the business purchased $100,000 of qualifying equipment, the Section 179 deduction is limited to the taxable income of the business. The deduction cannot exceed this income, making $100,000 an incorrect answer.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - Section 179 allows a business to deduct the cost of qualifying property as an expense rather than depreciating it. However, the deduction is limited to the business's taxable income before the Section 179 deduction. In this case, the taxable income is $40,000, so the maximum Section 179 deduction is $40,000.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - The business has taxable income of $40,000 and purchased qualifying equipment. While there are limitations on the Section 179 deduction, it is not zero in this scenario. The business can deduct up to its taxable income, which is $40,000.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - This option incorrectly calculates the Section 179 deduction. While the business purchased $100,000 of equipment, the deduction is limited to the taxable income of $40,000. $60,000 represents the excess of the purchase over the taxable income, but it is not the deductible amount.

questions.educational: Section 179 is designed to incentivize small businesses to invest in themselves by allowing them to immediately deduct the cost of qualifying property. However, Congress limits the deduction to prevent abuse and ensure it primarily benefits active businesses. The taxable income limitation ensures that the Section 179 deduction doesn't create or increase a net operating loss (NOL) for the business.
questions.examTip: Remember to always check for both the maximum dollar limitation for Section 179 (which is much higher and not relevant in this question) and the taxable income limitation. On the EA exam, they often test both limitations to see if you understand the interplay.
questions.memoryAid: Think of Section 179 as 'Income First': Taxable Income limits the Section 179 deduction.

questions.question: For property placed in service in 2024, bonus depreciation under IRC §168(k) is:
questions.explanation: Bonus depreciation phases down: 80% for 2023, 60% for 2024, 40% for 2025, 20% for 2026, 0% for 2027+. It applies to new qualified property (and used property under TCJA rules).

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - The bonus depreciation percentage was 80% for property placed in service in 2023, not 2024. The bonus depreciation phases down over time, as mandated by tax law.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - 100% bonus depreciation was available for qualified property placed in service before September 28, 2017, and after September 27, 2017, and before January 1, 2023. It is not the correct percentage for 2024.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - The bonus depreciation percentage will be 40% for property placed in service in 2025, not 2024. Keep track of the phase-down schedule to avoid confusion.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is CORRECT - For property placed in service in 2024, the bonus depreciation percentage under IRC §168(k) is 60%. This is part of the scheduled phase-down of bonus depreciation enacted by tax legislation.

questions.educational: Bonus depreciation is a tax incentive allowing businesses to deduct a large percentage of an asset's cost in the first year it's placed in service. This encourages investment in new equipment and stimulates economic growth. The Tax Cuts and Jobs Act (TCJA) initially increased bonus depreciation to 100%, but it is now phasing down, requiring careful attention to the placed-in-service date.
questions.examTip: On the EA exam, pay close attention to the 'placed in service' date when calculating depreciation, as this determines the applicable bonus depreciation percentage and other depreciation methods. Remember to check for any updates or changes to depreciation rules that may affect your calculations.
questions.memoryAid: 60 in '24: Bonus Depreciation is 60% in 2024.

questions.question: A taxpayer places a $60,000 passenger automobile (100% business use) in service in 2024 and does not elect out of bonus depreciation. The first-year depreciation is approximately:
questions.explanation: For 2024, the luxury auto limit is $20,200 for year 1 if bonus depreciation applies ($12,200 without bonus). Despite 60% bonus depreciation in 2024, the deduction is capped at the luxury auto limits.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - $10,200 is a common depreciation amount, but it doesn't reflect the correct first-year luxury auto limit with bonus depreciation for 2024. It's significantly lower than the allowable deduction.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - For passenger vehicles placed in service in 2024, the first-year depreciation limit is $20,200 when bonus depreciation is claimed. Even though the calculated depreciation (including bonus) might be higher based on the asset's cost, the luxury auto limits restrict the deduction to this amount. This limit is in place to prevent excessive depreciation deductions on high-end vehicles.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - $3,160 is the standard depreciation amount for a passenger vehicle using MACRS without bonus depreciation or the luxury auto limits. It doesn't account for the bonus depreciation election or the applicable first-year limit.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - $12,200 is the first-year luxury auto limit *without* bonus depreciation. The question states the taxpayer *does not* elect out of bonus depreciation, making this option incorrect.

questions.educational: Depreciation is a method of deducting the cost of an asset over its useful life. The Modified Accelerated Cost Recovery System (MACRS) is the standard depreciation system, but for passenger vehicles, 'luxury auto' limits apply, restricting the annual depreciation deduction. Bonus depreciation allows for an additional first-year deduction, but even with bonus, the luxury auto limits still cap the total deduction.
questions.examTip: Remember to check for luxury auto limits when calculating depreciation for passenger vehicles on the EA exam. The limits change annually, so use the most current IRS guidance.
questions.memoryAid: Luxury Auto Limits: Think 'LAL' - Limits Always Lurk!

questions.question: Which of the following is considered listed property subject to special depreciation rules?
options: Passenger automobiles used primarily for business
questions.explanation: Under IRC §280F(d)(4), listed property includes passenger autos, property used for entertainment, recreation, or amusement, and computers (pre-2018). Heavy SUVs/trucks over 6,000 lbs GVW are exempt from most listed property limitations.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - Office buildings are considered real property and are depreciated under MACRS using a 39-year recovery period for nonresidential real property. They are not subject to the listed property rules under IRC §280F.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - Passenger automobiles used primarily for business are specifically identified as listed property under IRC §280F(d)(4). This means their depreciation is subject to limitations if business use is not greater than 50%, and specific depreciation caps apply.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - Manufacturing equipment is generally considered tangible personal property used in a trade or business and is depreciated under MACRS using a shorter recovery period (e.g., 5 or 7 years). It does not fall under the definition of listed property.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - Commercial trucks with a gross vehicle weight (GVW) over 14,000 pounds are exempt from the listed property rules. Due to their size and intended use, they are not subject to the same depreciation limitations as passenger vehicles under IRC §280F.

questions.educational: Listed property rules under IRC §280F were enacted to prevent taxpayers from excessively deducting expenses related to assets that could be used for both business and personal purposes. These rules primarily target assets like passenger vehicles and entertainment-related property. Understanding these limitations is crucial for accurately calculating depreciation deductions and avoiding penalties.
questions.examTip: On the EA exam, pay close attention to the business use percentage of listed property. If business use falls to 50% or below in a subsequent year, you'll need to recapture excess depreciation previously taken.
questions.memoryAid: PALE: Passenger Autos, Listed Entertainment property. Remember these are the primary targets of listed property rules.

questions.question: A business placed a $120,000 asset in service in October 2024 (the only asset placed in service that year) with a 7-year MACRS recovery period. No Section 179 or bonus depreciation is elected. Which depreciation convention must be used?
options: Half-year convention since more than 40% of aggregate basis was NOT placed in service in the last quarter
Mid-quarter convention because the asset was placed in service in Q4
Full-year convention for the first year
questions.explanation: The mid-quarter convention is required only when more than 40% of the aggregate depreciable basis of all property placed in service during the year is placed in service in the last quarter (Q4). Here, 100% was placed in service in Q4, which exceeds 40%. However, when only one asset is placed in service, and it's the sole asset, the mid-quarter convention would technically apply. But the half-year convention is the default for MACRS personal property. Since all property was placed in Q4 and 100% > 40%, the mid-quarter convention actually applies.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is CORRECT - The half-year convention is the default depreciation convention for MACRS property. The mid-quarter convention is only required when more than 40% of the aggregate basis of all property placed in service during the year is placed in service in the fourth quarter. Since 100% of the basis was placed in service in Q4, the mid-quarter convention is triggered, making the half-year convention incorrect.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - While the asset was placed in service in Q4, the mid-quarter convention is not automatically triggered simply because an asset is placed in service in the last quarter. The mid-quarter convention is only required if more than 40% of the total depreciable basis of assets placed in service during the year are placed in service in the fourth quarter.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - The full-year convention is not applicable to personal property under MACRS. The applicable conventions are the half-year or mid-quarter convention.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - The mid-month convention is used for real property, not personal property like the asset in this question. The half-year or mid-quarter convention applies to personal property under MACRS.

questions.educational: Depreciation conventions are crucial for calculating the correct depreciation expense, impacting taxable income and ultimately tax liability. The half-year convention assumes assets are placed in service mid-year, while the mid-quarter convention is triggered when a significant portion of assets are placed in service late in the year to prevent taxpayers from disproportionately claiming depreciation in the first year. Understanding these conventions is vital for accurate tax planning and compliance.
questions.examTip: On the EA exam, pay close attention to the type of property (personal vs. real) and the timing of when assets are placed in service to determine the correct depreciation convention. Remember the 40% rule for the mid-quarter convention.
questions.memoryAid: 40% in Q4? Mid-Quarter Convention is at the door!

questions.question: Goodwill acquired in a business acquisition is amortized over:
questions.explanation: Under IRC §197, goodwill and most purchased intangibles (covenants not to compete, customer lists, franchises) are amortized ratably over 15 years beginning in the month of acquisition.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - Goodwill is not tested for impairment for tax purposes. While financial accounting (GAAP) requires impairment testing, tax law mandates amortization under IRC §197.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - A 5-year amortization period is not applicable to goodwill. Certain start-up and organizational costs can be amortized over 180 months (15 years) but can be immediately expensed up to $5,000 (subject to phase-out).
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - A 10-year amortization period is not the correct period for goodwill. While some assets might have a 10-year life, goodwill falls under the specific 15-year amortization rule of IRC §197.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is CORRECT - Under IRC §197, purchased goodwill is amortized ratably over a 15-year period (180 months), beginning with the month of acquisition. This applies regardless of the actual useful life of the goodwill.

questions.educational: IRC §197 was enacted to simplify the treatment of intangible assets, providing a uniform 15-year amortization period for a wide range of acquired intangibles, including goodwill, going concern value, covenants not to compete, and customer lists. This standardization reduces complexity and potential disputes with the IRS regarding the useful life of these assets, which are often difficult to determine. Understanding §197 is crucial for calculating taxable income and deductions for businesses, especially during acquisitions and reorganizations.
questions.examTip: On the EA exam, pay close attention to the difference between tax and financial accounting treatment of assets. Goodwill is amortized for tax purposes but tested for impairment under GAAP.
questions.memoryAid: Goodwill? Think 'G' for good, '15' for fifteen years of amortization.

questions.question: A new business incurs $60,000 in startup costs. In the first year, the business can deduct:
options: $5,000 deduction reduced by excess over $50,000; remainder amortized over 180 months
questions.explanation: Under IRC §195, $5,000 of startup costs can be deducted in year 1, REDUCED by the amount exceeding $50,000. Here, reduced by $10,000 to $0 immediate deduction. The full $60,000 is amortized over 180 months.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is CORRECT - IRC §195 allows for an immediate deduction of up to $5,000 in startup costs, but this deduction is reduced dollar-for-dollar by the amount exceeding $50,000; the remaining startup costs are amortized over 180 months. In this case, the $5,000 deduction is reduced by $10,000 ($60,000 - $50,000), resulting in a $0 immediate deduction. The remaining $60,000 is then amortized.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - IRC §195 does not allow for the full deduction of $60,000 in the first year; startup costs are subject to a limitation on the immediate deduction and subsequent amortization.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - While the amortization aspect is correct, the immediate deduction is incorrect; the $5,000 deduction is reduced by the excess over $50,000, which in this case eliminates the immediate deduction entirely.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - IRC §195 allows for an immediate deduction (subject to limitations) and amortization of startup costs; it does not require complete deferral of the deduction to amortization only.

questions.educational: The startup cost rules under IRC §195 are designed to provide some immediate tax relief for new businesses while also recognizing that these costs provide long-term benefits. By allowing a limited immediate deduction and amortizing the remainder, the tax code attempts to balance the need for immediate cash flow for new ventures with the principle of matching expenses to the revenue they generate over time. This is a common theme in business taxation, particularly when dealing with capital expenditures and intangible assets.
questions.examTip: Remember the $5,000 immediate deduction limit and the $50,000 phase-out threshold for startup costs; a common trick on the EA exam is to present scenarios where the costs exceed $50,000, requiring you to calculate the reduced immediate deduction and the amortizable amount.
questions.memoryAid: Think of '5 and 50' - $5,000 immediate deduction, phased out above $50,000.

questions.question: Equipment with an adjusted basis of $10,000 and accumulated depreciation of $15,000 is sold for $30,000. How much is Section 1245 recapture?
questions.explanation: Section 1245 recapture is the lesser of: (1) gain realized ($30,000 - $10,000 = $20,000) or (2) depreciation taken ($15,000). The $15,000 is ordinary income; remaining $5,000 is Section 1231 gain.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - This option incorrectly calculates the Section 1245 recapture by only considering the gain realized ($20,000) and not comparing it to the accumulated depreciation. Section 1245 recapture is limited to the lesser of the gain realized or the accumulated depreciation.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - This option is incorrect because it appears to be calculating the gain based on the adjusted basis ($10,000) but doesn't correctly apply the Section 1245 rules. The adjusted basis is relevant for determining the overall gain, but the recapture is based on depreciation.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - This option is incorrect and doesn't represent a logical calculation based on the information provided. It doesn't relate to either the gain realized or the accumulated depreciation.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is CORRECT - Section 1245 recapture treats the gain from the sale of depreciated property as ordinary income to the extent of depreciation previously taken. In this case, the accumulated depreciation is $15,000, and the gain realized is $20,000 ($30,000 selling price - $10,000 adjusted basis). Therefore, the Section 1245 recapture is the lesser of the two, which is $15,000.

questions.educational: Section 1245 exists to prevent taxpayers from converting ordinary income (depreciation deductions) into capital gains upon the sale of an asset. By recapturing depreciation as ordinary income, the tax code ensures that the tax benefits received during the asset's life are offset when it's sold at a gain. This concept is crucial for understanding the taxation of business assets and is frequently tested on the Business Taxation section of the EA exam.
questions.examTip: On the EA exam, always remember to compare the gain realized to the accumulated depreciation when calculating Section 1245 recapture. The recapture amount is the *lesser* of the two, and the remaining gain (if any) is generally Section 1231 gain.
questions.memoryAid: 1245: 'Recapture 'til you're Depleted, then it's 1231!' (Recapture up to the amount of depreciation, then any remaining gain is Section 1231).

questions.question: To qualify for exemption under IRC §501(c)(3), an organization must be organized and operated exclusively for:
options: Religious, charitable, scientific, educational, or certain other exempt purposes
Commercial activities that further exempt purposes
questions.explanation: Under IRC §501(c)(3), organizations must be organized and operated exclusively for religious, charitable, scientific, testing for public safety, literary, educational, or prevention of cruelty purposes.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is CORRECT - IRC §501(c)(3) explicitly states that an organization must be organized and operated exclusively for religious, charitable, scientific, testing for public safety, literary, or educational purposes, or to foster national or international amateur sports competition (but only if no part of its activities involve the provision of athletic facilities or equipment), or for the prevention of cruelty to children or animals. This option accurately reflects the core requirements for 501(c)(3) status.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - While a 501(c)(3) organization can engage in activities that generate income, it cannot be primarily operated for commercial purposes, even if those activities further its exempt goals. The organization's primary purpose must be charitable, religious, or educational, not commercial.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - An organization cannot qualify for 501(c)(3) status if it is operated for 'any lawful purpose'. The purpose must fall within the specific categories outlined in IRC §501(c)(3), such as religious, charitable, scientific, or educational purposes.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - Organizations that primarily benefit their members are generally not eligible for 501(c)(3) status. While some benefit to members may be incidental, the organization's primary purpose must be to benefit the public or a broad class of individuals, not its own members.

questions.educational: Tax-exempt organizations under IRC §501(c)(3) play a crucial role in society by providing public benefits. The strict requirements for qualification, including the organizational and operational tests, ensure that these organizations genuinely serve charitable purposes rather than private interests. Understanding these rules is essential for EAs advising clients on forming and maintaining tax-exempt status, a frequent topic on the SEE exam.
questions.examTip: On the EA exam, pay close attention to the 'exclusively' requirement for 501(c)(3) organizations; even a small amount of non-exempt activity can jeopardize their tax-exempt status.
questions.memoryAid: Remember 'ROCSLE' - Religious, Other charitable, Scientific, Literary, Educational - the core purposes for 501(c)(3) status.

questions.question: A private foundation is subject to excise tax on:
questions.explanation: Under IRC §4940, private foundations pay a 1.39% excise tax on net investment income (interest, dividends, rents, royalties, and capital gains less applicable expenses). This is reported on Form 990-PF.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - While private foundations must adhere to strict rules regarding distributions, including avoiding private benefit, distributions to individuals are not directly subject to excise tax. The excise tax focuses on the foundation's investment activities.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - The excise tax isn't levied on *all* investment income. It's specifically applied to *net* investment income, which is investment income less certain allowable expenses directly connected with generating that income. This distinction is crucial.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is CORRECT - IRC §4940 imposes a tax on a private foundation's net investment income (interest, dividends, rents, royalties, and capital gains) at a rate of 1.39%. This tax is designed to ensure that private foundations are using their resources for charitable purposes and not accumulating excessive wealth without benefiting the public.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - Gross receipts exceeding $1 million is a threshold that affects other aspects of a tax-exempt organization's reporting requirements (e.g., Schedule O), but it doesn't directly trigger an excise tax. The excise tax is specifically tied to net investment income.

questions.educational: The excise tax on private foundations' net investment income serves as a mechanism to encourage charitable giving and prevent the accumulation of wealth without public benefit. Understanding the nuances of this tax, including what constitutes net investment income and the applicable rate, is essential for Enrolled Agents advising these organizations. This also connects to the broader topic of tax-exempt organizations and their compliance requirements, a key area on the SEE exam.
questions.examTip: Remember the 1.39% excise tax rate on net investment income for private foundations; it's a frequently tested detail on the Enrolled Agent exam. Also, be prepared to differentiate between gross receipts and net investment income.
questions.memoryAid: NII = Net Investment Income; 1.39% excise tax. Think: NII-ce rate for the IRS.

questions.question: A 501(c)(3) organization avoids private foundation status if it:
options: Receives more than one-third of support from the general public or governmental units
Was formed within the last 5 years
questions.explanation: Under IRC §509(a), an organization is not a private foundation if it passes a public support test (more than 1/3 support from public/government) or is a supporting organization, church, school, hospital, or government unit.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - There is no blanket exception to private foundation status based solely on having annual revenues under $500,000. While revenue size can impact reporting requirements, it doesn't automatically exclude an organization from being classified as a private foundation.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - The number of employees an organization has is not a determining factor in whether it is classified as a private foundation. The key factor is the source of its financial support and its activities.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is CORRECT - Under IRC §509(a)(1), an organization is not a private foundation if it is a publicly supported organization, meaning it receives a substantial part of its support from governmental units or the general public. Specifically, it must normally receive more than one-third of its support from a combination of gifts, grants, contributions, membership fees, and gross receipts from permitted sources.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - The age of an organization is not a determining factor in whether it is classified as a private foundation. The source of its financial support and its activities are the key factors.

questions.educational: The distinction between public charities and private foundations is crucial because private foundations are subject to stricter regulations and excise taxes under Chapter 42 of the IRC. Public charities, which receive significant support from the public, are generally considered more accountable to the public and are therefore subject to less stringent rules. Understanding these classifications is essential for advising tax-exempt organizations on compliance and tax planning.
questions.examTip: Remember the 'one-third support test' for public charities under IRC §509(a)(1). On the exam, carefully analyze the sources of an organization's revenue to determine if it meets this threshold and avoids private foundation status.
questions.memoryAid: Think 'Public Support = Public Charity = Less Tax'. Publicly supported charities face less excise tax than private foundations.

questions.question: Unrelated business income tax (UBIT) applies when an exempt organization:
options: Conducts a trade or business regularly that is not substantially related to its exempt purpose
questions.explanation: Under IRC §511-514, UBIT applies to income from a trade or business that is regularly carried on and not substantially related to the organization's exempt purpose. Many passive income items are excluded.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - Investment income, such as dividends, interest, and rents from real property, is generally excluded from UBIT unless it's derived from debt-financed property or controlled organizations. The mere receipt of investment income doesn't trigger UBIT.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - While an exempt organization's gross receipts are relevant for determining its overall tax-exempt status and required filings (e.g., Form 990), there isn't a specific gross receipts threshold that automatically triggers UBIT. UBIT is determined by the nature of the income-generating activity, not the overall size of the organization.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is CORRECT - Unrelated Business Income Tax (UBIT) is imposed on income derived from a trade or business that is regularly carried on by an exempt organization and is not substantially related to the organization's exempt purpose. This ensures that tax-exempt organizations don't gain an unfair competitive advantage over taxable businesses by engaging in activities unrelated to their mission.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - The amount an exempt organization pays its employees is not a factor in determining whether UBIT applies. UBIT focuses on the nature of the income-generating activity, not the organization's expenses or compensation practices.

questions.educational: UBIT exists to prevent unfair competition between tax-exempt organizations and for-profit businesses. If a non-profit engages in activities that are not substantially related to its exempt purpose and do so regularly, the income from those activities is subject to tax, just like a for-profit business. This ensures a level playing field and prevents non-profits from using their tax-exempt status to gain an unfair advantage in unrelated commercial ventures. This is a key area tested in the SEE exam under the Business Taxation section.
questions.examTip: When calculating UBIT, remember to consider the specific exclusions and modifications allowed under IRC Sections 512-514, such as the specific deduction for expenses directly connected with the unrelated trade or business.
questions.memoryAid: UBIT = Unrelated, Business, Income, Tax. Think: Is the income UNRELATED to the exempt purpose, from a BUSINESS, and therefore TAXABLE?

questions.question: Which of the following is generally excluded from unrelated business taxable income?
options: Income from advertising in the organization's magazine
Fees from providing services to other organizations
Income from operating a parking lot open to the public
questions.explanation: Under IRC §512(b), passive income such as dividends, interest, royalties, and rents from real property (with exceptions) is generally excluded from UBIT, even if unrelated to exempt purposes.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is CORRECT - IRC §512(b) specifically excludes dividends, interest, royalties, and rents from real property (with certain exceptions not applicable here) from the definition of unrelated business taxable income (UBTI), recognizing that these are generally passive investments and not active business operations.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - Income from advertising in an organization's magazine is generally considered unrelated business income because it's an activity separate from the organization's exempt purpose and is conducted regularly for profit.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - Fees from providing services to other organizations are generally considered unrelated business income, especially if the services are not substantially related to the organization's exempt purpose and are provided on a regular basis.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - Income from operating a parking lot open to the public is generally considered unrelated business income if the operation is not substantially related to the organization's exempt purpose; operating a parking lot is a commercial activity.

questions.educational: The unrelated business income tax (UBIT) exists to prevent tax-exempt organizations from gaining an unfair competitive advantage over for-profit businesses. It taxes income from activities that are not substantially related to the organization's exempt purpose and are regularly carried on. Passive income, like dividends and interest, is generally excluded because it's not considered an active trade or business.
questions.examTip: Remember the acronym 'DIRR' (Dividends, Interest, Royalties, Rents) as a quick way to recall the major categories of passive income generally excluded from UBIT on the EA exam.
questions.memoryAid: DIRR - Think 'Don't Ignore Really Relaxing' income when considering UBIT exclusions.

questions.question: A tax-exempt organization with gross receipts of $200,000 must file:
questions.explanation: Form 990 is required for organizations with gross receipts ≥ $200,000 or total assets ≥ $500,000. Form 990-EZ for receipts < $200,000 and assets < $500,000. Form 990-N for receipts ≤ $50,000.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - Form 990-EZ is used by tax-exempt organizations with gross receipts less than $200,000 and total assets less than $500,000. Since the organization in the question has gross receipts of $200,000, it exceeds the gross receipts threshold for filing Form 990-EZ.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - Form 990-N (e-Postcard) is used by tax-exempt organizations with gross receipts normally not more than $50,000. The organization's gross receipts of $200,000 far exceed this threshold, making Form 990-N inappropriate.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - Most tax-exempt organizations are required to file an annual information return with the IRS. An organization with gross receipts of $200,000 is well above the threshold for mandatory filing.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is CORRECT - Tax-exempt organizations with gross receipts of $200,000 or more, or total assets of $500,000 or more, are required to file Form 990. The purpose of Form 990 is to provide the IRS and the public with information about the organization's mission, programs, and finances.

questions.educational: Tax-exempt organizations must file annual information returns to maintain their tax-exempt status. The specific form required depends on the organization's size and activities, with larger organizations required to provide more detailed information. This reporting requirement ensures transparency and accountability, allowing the IRS to monitor compliance with tax laws and regulations and the public to assess the organization's operations.
questions.examTip: Pay close attention to the gross receipts and total asset thresholds for each type of Form 990 (990, 990-EZ, and 990-N) on the EA exam, as questions often test your knowledge of these filing requirements.
questions.memoryAid: Remember the filing thresholds: N<EZ<Full. 990-N is the smallest, then 990-EZ, and the full 990 is for the largest organizations.

questions.question: A private foundation is generally required to distribute for charitable purposes annually at least:
questions.explanation: Under IRC §4942, private foundations must distribute approximately 5% of the fair market value of their net investment assets annually as qualifying distributions to avoid excise tax on undistributed income.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - While private foundations must distribute funds for charitable purposes, the required distribution is not based on a percentage of annual income. Instead, it's tied to the value of their investment assets.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - IRC §4942 mandates that private foundations distribute approximately 5% of the fair market value of their net investment assets annually as qualifying distributions. This requirement is designed to ensure that foundations actively use their resources for charitable purposes rather than accumulating wealth indefinitely.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - 1% of net investment assets is too low. The IRS requires a significantly higher distribution rate to prevent foundations from simply holding onto their assets.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - 10% of net investment assets is too high. While a foundation *could* distribute this much, the minimum required distribution is 5%.

questions.educational: Private foundations are subject to stricter regulations than public charities because they often derive their funding from a single source (e.g., a family or corporation). The 5% minimum distribution requirement ensures that these foundations actively engage in charitable activities and do not simply serve as tax shelters. This requirement is part of a broader set of rules under Chapter 42 of the IRC, which aims to prevent abuse by private foundations.
questions.examTip: Remember the 5% rule for private foundations' minimum distributions, and be aware that failure to meet this requirement can result in excise taxes under IRC §4942, a key area for the SEE exam.
questions.memoryAid: Think of 'FIVE Alive' - 5% keeps the foundation's mission alive.

questions.question: Self-dealing transactions between a private foundation and a disqualified person result in:
options: No penalty if disclosed on Form 990-PF
Excise taxes on the disqualified person and foundation managers
Ordinary income treatment of the transaction
questions.explanation: Under IRC §4941, self-dealing between private foundations and disqualified persons (substantial contributors, foundation managers, their families) results in excise taxes of 10% on the disqualified person (initial) and potential foundation manager taxes.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - Disclosure on Form 990-PF does not negate the excise taxes on self-dealing transactions. While disclosure is required, it doesn't excuse the violation of IRC §4941.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - IRC §4941 imposes excise taxes on disqualified persons who engage in self-dealing with a private foundation. Foundation managers may also be subject to excise taxes if they knowingly participate in the self-dealing act. The initial tax on the disqualified person is 10% of the amount involved, and 5% on the foundation manager (up to $20,000).
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - While egregious and repeated acts of self-dealing can ultimately lead to revocation of tax-exempt status, the *initial* consequence is the imposition of excise taxes under IRC §4941, not immediate loss of exempt status. Revocation is a more extreme measure taken after repeated violations.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - Self-dealing transactions do not result in ordinary income treatment. The tax consequences are excise taxes levied under IRC §4941 on the disqualified person and potentially the foundation managers involved.

questions.educational: The self-dealing rules under IRC §4941 are designed to prevent individuals with close ties to a private foundation from using the foundation's assets for their personal benefit. These rules are crucial for maintaining the integrity of private foundations and ensuring they operate for charitable purposes. Understanding these rules is essential for those advising private foundations, as violations can result in significant penalties.
questions.examTip: Remember the excise tax rates for self-dealing: 10% on the disqualified person and 5% on the foundation manager. Be prepared to calculate these taxes based on a given transaction amount on the exam.
questions.memoryAid: Self-Dealing = Excise Taxes (not ordinary income or just disclosure).

questions.question: A 501(c)(3) organization that participates in a political campaign for a candidate:
options: Is permitted if expenditures are under $5,000
Must report it as an unrelated business activity
Must pay UBIT on campaign expenditures
questions.explanation: Under IRC §501(c)(3), organizations are absolutely prohibited from participating in any political campaign for or against any candidate for public office. Violation can result in loss of exempt status and excise taxes.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - IRC §501(c)(3) prohibits *any* participation or intervention in political campaigns, regardless of the amount spent. There is no de minimis exception for expenditures under $5,000.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - IRC §501(c)(3) organizations are strictly prohibited from participating in, or intervening in, any political campaign on behalf of (or in opposition to) any candidate for public office. Engaging in such activities can result in the revocation of the organization's tax-exempt status by the IRS.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - While unrelated business income (UBI) generally arises from activities unrelated to the organization's exempt purpose, direct participation in a political campaign is a violation of the organization's exempt status itself, not merely an unrelated business activity. Loss of exempt status is the primary consequence.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - While unrelated business income tax (UBIT) applies to income from activities not substantially related to the organization's exempt purpose, direct involvement in political campaigns is a violation of the exempt purpose itself. The primary consequence is loss of exempt status, although excise taxes may also apply.

questions.educational: Section 501(c)(3) organizations are granted tax-exempt status to serve the public good through charitable, religious, scientific, or educational purposes. Allowing these organizations to engage in political campaigns would undermine their public benefit mission and potentially create unfair advantages in the political process. This restriction ensures that tax-exempt resources are used for their intended purpose, aligning with the broader principles of tax law and the integrity of the non-profit sector.
questions.examTip: Remember that the prohibition on political campaign activity for 501(c)(3) organizations is absolute. Even seemingly minor or indirect support can jeopardize their tax-exempt status, so advise your clients to avoid any involvement whatsoever.
questions.memoryAid: 501(c)(3) = No Politics, See?

questions.question: A 501(c)(3) public charity may engage in lobbying activities:
options: To an insubstantial extent or under a §501(h) expenditure election
Never - lobbying is prohibited for all 501(c)(3) organizations
Only if directly related to its exempt purpose
questions.explanation: Public charities may engage in limited lobbying (an insubstantial part of activities). Under IRC §501(h), eligible organizations can elect to measure lobbying limits as dollar expenditure limits rather than the vague "substantial part" test.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is CORRECT - Public charities, while primarily focused on their exempt purpose, are permitted to engage in a limited amount of lobbying. This limitation is defined either as an 'insubstantial part' of their overall activities or, for eligible organizations, through a specific expenditure test under IRC §501(h), providing a more concrete measure of permissible lobbying activities.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - While 501(c)(3) organizations face restrictions on lobbying, a complete prohibition is incorrect. They can engage in lobbying activities to an insubstantial extent or by making the §501(h) election.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - 501(c)(3) organizations are not permitted to engage in unlimited lobbying. Such unrestricted activity could jeopardize their tax-exempt status, as it would indicate a primary purpose beyond their stated charitable mission.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - While lobbying should ideally align with the organization's exempt purpose, the option is too restrictive. The key is the *extent* of lobbying, not solely its direct relation to the exempt purpose. An insubstantial amount of lobbying, even if indirectly related, is permissible.

questions.educational: The rules surrounding lobbying by 501(c)(3) organizations are designed to balance the public benefit they provide with the potential for political influence. The 'insubstantial part' test is subjective, leading many eligible organizations to elect the expenditure test under §501(h), which provides clearer, dollar-based limits. Understanding these limitations is crucial for maintaining a charity's tax-exempt status and avoiding penalties.
questions.examTip: On the EA exam, pay close attention to the wording of questions about lobbying activities. Distinguish between 'insubstantial part' and the specific expenditure limits under §501(h). Remember that excessive lobbying can result in loss of tax-exempt status.
questions.memoryAid: Think 'I(nsubstantial) or H(undreds of dollars)' to remember the two ways a public charity can lobby.

questions.question: Under the cash method of accounting, income is generally recognized when:
options: The customer's payment is deposited in the bank
questions.explanation: Under the cash method, income is recognized when actually received or constructively received (made available without restriction). Expenses are deducted when paid.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - Income recognition under the cash method isn't based on when services are performed, but rather when payment is received. The accrual method uses the 'earned' concept, not the cash method.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - While depositing a payment is strong evidence of receipt, the key is *constructive* receipt. Income is recognized when the taxpayer has access to the funds, even if they haven't physically deposited them. For example, a check received on December 31st but not deposited until January is still taxable in the year received.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is CORRECT - The cash method of accounting, a permissible method for many smaller businesses, dictates that income is recognized when actually or constructively received. Constructive receipt means the taxpayer has unrestricted access to the funds; they are available without substantial limitation or restriction. This aligns with the fundamental principle of cash basis accounting, focusing on the movement of cash.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - Billing a customer creates an account receivable, which is relevant under the accrual method of accounting. The cash method ignores the billing and focuses solely on when the cash changes hands.

questions.educational: The cash method of accounting is simpler than the accrual method, making it attractive to smaller businesses. However, it can lead to distortions in income reporting if not managed carefully. Understanding the difference between actual and constructive receipt is crucial for accurate tax reporting under the cash method, and is a common area tested on the EA exam.
questions.examTip: Remember that the IRS may challenge the use of the cash method if it does not clearly reflect income. Be prepared to justify its use, especially if your client has significant inventory or accounts receivable.
questions.memoryAid: Cash in Hand = Income Recognized (Cash Method)

questions.question: After TCJA, a corporation can generally use the cash method of accounting if average annual gross receipts for the 3 prior years do not exceed:
questions.explanation: Under IRC §448, the $25 million gross receipts test (indexed to ~$29-30 million for 2024) allows most businesses, including C corporations, to use the cash method if they meet this threshold.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - $5 million is significantly lower than the indexed gross receipts test amount for corporations using the cash method of accounting. This amount is not relevant to the gross receipts test under IRC §448.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - $50 million is higher than the indexed gross receipts test amount for corporations using the cash method of accounting. While a larger number, it doesn't reflect the actual threshold allowed by the IRS.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - $10 million is lower than the indexed gross receipts test amount for corporations using the cash method of accounting. This amount is not the threshold for the gross receipts test under IRC §448.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is CORRECT - IRC §448 allows C corporations (and partnerships with corporate partners) to use the cash method of accounting if their average annual gross receipts for the three prior tax years do not exceed $25 million, adjusted annually for inflation. This indexed amount is approximately $29 million for 2024, making this the correct answer.

questions.educational: The gross receipts test under IRC §448 is designed to simplify accounting for smaller businesses. Allowing smaller corporations to use the cash method reduces the complexity of tracking inventory and accruals, easing their tax compliance burden. This exception recognizes that the accrual method's benefits may not outweigh the costs for smaller entities.
questions.examTip: Remember that the gross receipts test is indexed annually, so check for the most current threshold amount when preparing for the EA exam. The IRS publishes this information, and it's crucial for accurate tax advice.
questions.memoryAid: CASH for Corps: Considerably Adjusted Since Historical (amount).

questions.question: Under the accrual method, income is generally recognized when:
options: All events have occurred to fix the right to receive and the amount can be determined with reasonable accuracy
questions.explanation: Under the accrual method, income is recognized when all events have occurred that fix the right to receive it (all-events test) and the amount can be determined with reasonable accuracy.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - While issuing an invoice is often a step towards fulfilling the all-events test, it's not sufficient on its own. The services or goods must also be provided or delivered for income recognition under the accrual method.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - Cash receipt is the trigger for income recognition under the cash method of accounting, not the accrual method. The accrual method focuses on when the right to receive income is earned, regardless of when cash changes hands.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is CORRECT - The accrual method of accounting, as defined by the all-events test, recognizes income when (1) all events have occurred that fix the right to receive the income, and (2) the amount of income can be determined with reasonable accuracy. This ensures income is matched to the period in which it is earned, regardless of when cash is received.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - While substantial completion of services is a factor considered, the 'all events' test requires that *all* events necessary to earn the income have occurred. Substantial completion may not fulfill this requirement if further actions are still needed.

questions.educational: The accrual method aims to provide a more accurate picture of a business's financial performance by matching revenues with the expenses incurred to generate those revenues, regardless of the timing of cash flows. This contrasts with the cash method, which recognizes income when cash is received and expenses when cash is paid. Understanding the nuances of accrual accounting is crucial for preparing accurate financial statements and tax returns for businesses, a key skill for Enrolled Agents.
questions.examTip: On the EA exam, carefully consider whether a question is asking about the cash or accrual method, as the timing of income and expense recognition differs significantly between the two.
questions.memoryAid: Accrual = All Events + Reasonable Accuracy (AERA)

questions.question: An accrual method taxpayer incurs a $50,000 liability for services in December 2024, but the services are performed in January 2025. The expense is deductible in:
options: 2024 if paid within 8.5 months of year-end
Either year at the taxpayer's election
questions.explanation: Under IRC §461(h), for liabilities for services, economic performance occurs when the services are provided. The expense is deductible in 2025, even though the liability was fixed in 2024 (with limited recurring item exceptions).

whyWrong.0: Why option A is CORRECT - Under the accrual method, an expense is deductible when all events have occurred that establish the liability, the amount can be determined with reasonable accuracy, and economic performance has occurred. For services, economic performance occurs when the services are actually performed, which in this case is in January 2025.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - While the all-events test requires the liability to be fixed, economic performance must also occur for the expense to be deductible. The liability being fixed in 2024 is only one part of the test; economic performance did not occur until 2025.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - The 8.5-month rule applies to certain recurring items, allowing deduction even if economic performance hasn't occurred by year-end, provided it occurs within a reasonable period (8.5 months). However, this is an exception, not the general rule, and without more information indicating it qualifies as a recurring item, it's incorrect to assume it applies here.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - Taxpayers generally do not have an election to choose the year in which to deduct an expense under the accrual method. The timing is dictated by the all-events test and economic performance rules under IRC §461(h).

questions.educational: The accrual method aims to match revenues and expenses in the period they are earned and incurred, respectively. Economic performance rules prevent taxpayers from prematurely deducting expenses before the related goods or services are actually received. This ensures a more accurate reflection of a business's financial performance and prevents tax avoidance.
questions.examTip: Remember the all-events test and economic performance requirements are crucial for accrual method taxpayers. Pay close attention to when services are performed, as this dictates the timing of the deduction.
questions.memoryAid: A-E-E: Accrual = All-Events test + Economic Performance

questions.question: A taxpayer receives a check on December 30, 2024, but does not deposit it until January 3, 2025. The income is taxable in:
options: Either year at the taxpayer's election
questions.explanation: Under the constructive receipt doctrine, income is taxable when it is made available to the taxpayer without substantial limitation. Receipt of a valid check constitutes constructive receipt in 2024.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is CORRECT - The constructive receipt doctrine, as outlined in Treasury Regulation §1.451-2(a), dictates that income is taxable when it is credited to a taxpayer's account, set apart for them, or otherwise made available so they can draw upon it at any time. Because the taxpayer received the check on December 30, 2024, and could have cashed or deposited it then, the income is constructively received in 2024.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - The income was constructively received in 2024, even though the taxpayer physically deposited the check in 2025. The key is the availability of the funds, not the date of deposit.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - Taxpayers do not have the option to choose the year in which to recognize income when the constructive receipt doctrine applies. The timing is determined by when the income was made available to them without substantial limitations.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - The date the check clears is irrelevant for determining the tax year in which the income is recognized. The constructive receipt doctrine focuses on when the taxpayer had access to the funds, not the bank's internal processing date.

questions.educational: The constructive receipt doctrine prevents taxpayers from manipulating the timing of income recognition by delaying access to funds that are already available to them. This rule is crucial for maintaining fairness and accuracy in the tax system and is a key concept within accounting methods, a heavily tested area on the SEE exam.
