A PHC is a corporation where: (1) more than 50% owned by 5 or fewer individuals, and (2) 60% or more of adjusted ordinary gross income is PHC income (dividends, interest, rents, royalties, certain personal service income).

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - The personal holding company (PHC) tax is not applied to all PHC income. It's specifically levied on the undistributed portion, meaning income not paid out as dividends.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - A personal holding company (PHC) is subject to a 20% tax on its undistributed personal holding company income. This tax is designed to discourage companies from being used to shelter investment income of high-income individuals. Undistributed PHC income is essentially the adjusted taxable income less the dividends paid.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - While 21% is the standard corporate income tax rate, the PHC tax is a separate, additional tax. It's not applied to all income, but only to undistributed PHC income.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - The PHC tax rate is 20%, not 15%. The 15% rate is often associated with qualified dividends and long-term capital gains for individuals, but it's not relevant to the PHC tax.

questions.educational: The personal holding company (PHC) tax exists to prevent individuals from using corporations to avoid paying individual income tax on investment income. By accumulating income within a corporation and not distributing it as dividends, individuals could potentially defer or avoid higher individual tax rates. The PHC tax is a penalty designed to discourage this practice, aligning with the broader goal of tax fairness and preventing tax avoidance schemes.
questions.examTip: Remember that the PHC rules are complex and require careful calculation of adjusted ordinary gross income and undistributed PHC income. On the exam, pay close attention to the details provided in the fact pattern to determine if a corporation meets the PHC definition and if the tax applies.
questions.memoryAid: PHC = 20% Undistributed (Think: PHC - Pay Hidden Cash, or else 20% tax!)

questions.question: A C corporation receiving dividends from a domestic corporation in which it owns 25% of the stock may deduct:
questions.explanation: The dividends received deduction (DRD) is: 50% for ownership < 20%, 65% for ownership of 20-79%, and 100% for ownership of 80% or more (affiliated group). Since 25% falls in the 20-79% range, the DRD is 65%.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is CORRECT - The dividends received deduction (DRD) for a C corporation depends on its ownership percentage in the dividend-paying corporation. Because the C corporation owns 25% of the stock, which falls between 20% and 80%, it is entitled to a 65% DRD.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - A C corporation can deduct 80% of dividends received only if it owns 80% or more of the dividend-paying corporation's stock. Since the corporation in this question owns only 25%, the 80% deduction is not applicable.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - A 100% dividends received deduction is available only to members of an affiliated group. An affiliated group requires 80% or more common ownership, which is not the case here with only 25% ownership.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - A 50% dividends received deduction applies when the recipient corporation owns less than 20% of the dividend-paying corporation's stock. The corporation in this question owns 25%, making the 50% deduction incorrect.

questions.educational: The dividends received deduction (DRD) mitigates triple taxation. Without it, corporate earnings would be taxed at the corporate level, again when distributed as dividends to another corporation, and yet again when the second corporation distributes those dividends to individual shareholders. The DRD rules are designed to alleviate this burden, encouraging inter-corporate investment.
questions.examTip: On the EA exam, pay close attention to the ownership percentage when calculating the dividends received deduction. A quick review of the DRD tiers (less than 20%, 20-79%, 80% or more) before the exam can save you time and ensure accuracy.
questions.memoryAid: DRD: 'Less than 20, get 50. 20 to 80, get 65. 80 or more, get the rest (100).'

questions.question: Davenport Inc., a C corporation, has modified taxable income of $520,000 before considering charitable contributions. During the year, Davenport donated appreciated stock with FMV of $75,000 (basis $30,000) held for 2 years to a public charity. What is the allowable charitable deduction for the current year?
questions.explanation: C corporation charitable deductions are limited to 10% of modified taxable income. The calculation is 10% × $520,000 = $52,000. Long-term capital gain property donated to a public charity is generally deductible at fair market value ($75,000), but the 10% limitation caps the current year deduction at $52,000. The excess $23,000 carries forward for up to 5 years.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is CORRECT - C corporations can deduct charitable contributions up to 10% of their modified taxable income. In this case, 10% of $520,000 is $52,000, which is the maximum deductible amount, even though the FMV of the donation was higher.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - While the fair market value of the donated stock is $75,000, the corporate charitable contribution deduction is limited to 10% of modified taxable income. The full $75,000 cannot be deducted in the current year.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - $30,000 represents the basis of the donated stock, not the deductible amount. When donating appreciated property held long-term to a public charity, corporations generally deduct the fair market value, subject to the 10% limitation.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - There is no calculation that results in $45,000. The allowable deduction is the lesser of the fair market value of the donated property ($75,000) or 10% of modified taxable income ($52,000).

questions.educational: Corporate charitable contribution rules are designed to encourage philanthropy while preventing excessive tax avoidance. The 10% limitation on deductions ensures that charitable giving doesn't unduly reduce a corporation's tax liability. Excess contributions can be carried forward for five years, providing some relief for larger donations.
questions.examTip: Remember that while individuals can deduct charitable contributions up to 60% of AGI (for cash) or 30% of AGI (for appreciated property), C corporations are limited to 10% of modified taxable income. Pay close attention to the entity type in charitable contribution questions.
questions.memoryAid: C Corp Charity: 10% MTI (Modified Taxable Income)

questions.question: When a C corporation distributes property to shareholders, the amount treated as a dividend is limited to:
options: The lesser of FMV or basis of distributed property
The corporation's accumulated and current earnings and profits (E&P)
The fair market value of the property
questions.explanation: Distributions are dividends to the extent of current and accumulated E&P. Amounts exceeding E&P are return of capital (reducing stock basis). Amounts exceeding stock basis are capital gain. Order: first current E&P, then accumulated E&P.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - The fair market value (FMV) of the distributed property is relevant in determining the amount of the distribution to the shareholder, but the dividend amount is limited by the corporation's E&P, not the property's basis. The shareholder recognizes income equal to the FMV of the property received, to the extent of E&P.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - A corporate distribution to a shareholder is treated as a dividend to the extent of the corporation's current and accumulated earnings and profits (E&P) under IRC Section 316. E&P represents the corporation's economic capacity to pay dividends. Distributions exceeding E&P are treated as a return of capital (reducing the shareholder's stock basis) and then as capital gain.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - The shareholder's basis in their stock becomes relevant *after* the distribution exceeds the corporation's E&P. Distributions exceeding E&P reduce the shareholder's stock basis, but the dividend amount itself is capped by E&P, not the stock basis.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - While the fair market value (FMV) of the distributed property determines the amount of the distribution the shareholder receives, the portion of that distribution treated as a dividend is limited by the corporation's E&P. The entire FMV is not necessarily a dividend; it's only a dividend to the extent of E&P.

questions.educational: Earnings and Profits (E&P) is a tax concept, not an accounting concept, and it measures a corporation's economic capacity to pay dividends. Understanding E&P is crucial for determining the taxability of corporate distributions to shareholders. Distributions are taxed as dividends to the extent of E&P, reflecting the idea that shareholders are receiving a return on their investment from the corporation's profits.
questions.examTip: Remember the 'layering' of corporate distributions: first, dividends to the extent of E&P; second, return of capital (reducing basis); and third, capital gain. Focus on calculating E&P and understanding its priority in determining the tax treatment of distributions on the EA exam.
questions.memoryAid: E&P is the 'Earning Power' that determines how much of a distribution is a taxable dividend. Think: E&P = Earnings Power = Extent of Dividend.

questions.question: When a corporation completely liquidates, shareholders recognize:
options: Dividend income equal to the distribution
No gain or loss until property is sold
Gain or loss (generally capital) based on FMV of property received minus stock basis
Ordinary income on all amounts received
questions.explanation: In a complete liquidation under §331, shareholders treat the liquidating distribution as full payment for stock. Gain or loss equals FMV of property received minus adjusted basis of stock surrendered. The gain/loss is generally capital in nature.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - Liquidating distributions are treated as payments in exchange for stock, not as dividends. Dividends are distributions from earnings and profits, while liquidations represent a return of capital and any associated gain.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - Shareholders recognize gain or loss upon receiving property in a complete liquidation. The liquidation is a taxable event for the shareholder, triggering immediate recognition of gain or loss, not deferral until the property is sold.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is CORRECT - In a complete liquidation under §331, shareholders are treated as if they sold their stock back to the corporation. The shareholder recognizes a capital gain or loss equal to the difference between the fair market value of the assets received and the adjusted basis of the stock surrendered. This mirrors the treatment of a sale of stock.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - While some distributions might be taxed as ordinary income (e.g., recapture of depreciation), the entire amount received in a liquidation is not automatically treated as ordinary income. The default treatment is capital gain or loss, with exceptions for specific situations.

questions.educational: Corporate liquidations represent the final distribution of assets to shareholders, effectively terminating the corporation's existence. The tax treatment is designed to reflect the shareholder's economic gain or loss on their investment in the corporation. Understanding the difference between liquidating distributions and regular dividends is crucial for the SEE exam, as the tax consequences differ significantly.
questions.examTip: Remember that the shareholder's basis in the assets received in liquidation is the fair market value on the date of distribution, which will be their starting point for future sales or dispositions.
questions.memoryAid: Liquidate = Like a Sale (of stock back to the company)

questions.question: A liquidating corporation recognizes gain or loss:
options: On all property distributed as if sold at FMV
Only if the shareholder recognizes gain
Only on property with built-in gains
questions.explanation: Under §336, a liquidating corporation recognizes gain or loss on all property distributed as if it were sold at FMV. This is double taxation - the corporation pays tax on the gain, and shareholders also recognize gain/loss. Exception: §332 subsidiary liquidation.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is CORRECT - Under IRC §336, a liquidating corporation is treated as if it sold its assets at fair market value (FMV) when distributing them in liquidation. This means the corporation recognizes gain or loss as if it had actually sold the assets, regardless of whether the shareholders recognize gain.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - The corporation's recognition of gain or loss in a liquidation is independent of whether the shareholder recognizes gain. IRC §336 dictates corporate-level recognition, while IRC §331 governs shareholder-level recognition. They are separate events.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - IRC §336 requires the liquidating corporation to recognize gain or loss on *all* property distributed, not just property with built-in gains. This includes property that may have a built-in loss.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - Liquidation is generally *not* a tax-free event for the corporation. IRC §336 specifically mandates the recognition of gain or loss as if the property were sold at FMV. The exception is a §332 subsidiary liquidation, which is not the general rule.

questions.educational: Corporate liquidations are generally taxable events at both the corporate and shareholder levels, reflecting the principle that economic gains should be taxed. The corporation recognizes gain or loss on the distribution of its assets as if they were sold, and the shareholders recognize gain or loss based on the difference between the value of assets received and their stock basis. This double taxation is a key characteristic of C corporations.
questions.examTip: Remember that the general rule for corporate liquidations under IRC §336 is that the corporation recognizes gain or loss as if it sold the assets at FMV. Be on the lookout for the exception for subsidiary liquidations under §332, which is often tested.
questions.memoryAid: FMV = For Many Valuations (corporation recognizes gain/loss as if sold at FMV)

questions.question: A C corporation's net operating loss (NOL) may:
options: Be carried back 2 years and forward 20 years
Be carried forward indefinitely with no limitation
Be carried forward indefinitely but limited to 80% of taxable income
questions.explanation: Under post-TCJA rules (for NOLs arising after 2020), NOLs carry forward indefinitely but are limited to 80% of taxable income. NOL carrybacks are generally not allowed (with limited exceptions). The 80% limitation ensures some tax is always paid.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - NOL carryback is generally not allowed for NOLs arising in tax years beginning after December 31, 2020. While there are limited exceptions, this statement is not generally true.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - While NOLs arising in tax years beginning after December 31, 2020 can be carried forward indefinitely, they are subject to a limitation. The deduction is limited to 80% of taxable income, computed without regard to the NOL deduction itself.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is CORRECT - For NOLs arising in tax years beginning after December 31, 2020, the NOL can be carried forward indefinitely to future tax years. However, the deduction is limited to 80% of taxable income (computed without regard to the NOL deduction). This limitation ensures that the corporation pays at least some tax in the carryforward year.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - For NOLs arising in tax years beginning after December 31, 2020, the carryforward period is indefinite, not limited to 5 years. The 5-year carryforward period was applicable under prior law.

questions.educational: Net Operating Losses (NOLs) provide tax relief to businesses experiencing losses, allowing them to offset profits in other years. The rules surrounding NOLs have changed significantly over time, particularly with the Tax Cuts and Jobs Act (TCJA) of 2017. Understanding the current rules regarding carryforward periods and limitations is crucial for accurate tax planning and compliance, a key skill for Enrolled Agents.
questions.examTip: Pay close attention to the year the NOL arose, as the carryback and carryforward rules have changed over time; the EA exam will test your knowledge of the different rules and their applicability.
questions.memoryAid: NOLs after 2020: No Back, 80% Cap, Carry Forward Forever (NBCF).

questions.question: A stock redemption is treated as a sale (capital gain) rather than a dividend if it meets which test?
options: Only if authorized by the board of directors
Only if the shareholder owns less than 50%
Substantially disproportionate, complete termination, or not essentially equivalent to a dividend
Any redemption is treated as a sale
questions.explanation: Under §302, a redemption is treated as a sale if it is: (1) substantially disproportionate (after redemption, shareholder owns < 50% voting and < 80% of prior percentage), (2) complete termination of interest, or (3) not essentially equivalent to a dividend (meaningful reduction). Otherwise, it is a §301 distribution.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - While board authorization is generally required for a stock redemption, it doesn't determine whether the redemption is treated as a sale or a dividend. The tax treatment hinges on the shareholder's ownership position before and after the redemption, as defined by §302.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - Owning less than 50% of the company after the redemption is a factor in determining if the redemption is 'substantially disproportionate,' but it is not the only factor. The shareholder must also own less than 80% of their prior percentage of voting stock and common stock to meet the substantially disproportionate test under §302(b)(2).
whyWrong.2: Why option C is CORRECT - Section 302 of the Internal Revenue Code dictates that a stock redemption is treated as a sale (resulting in capital gain or loss) if it falls under one of these three exceptions: substantially disproportionate redemption, complete termination of the shareholder's interest, or a redemption not essentially equivalent to a dividend. These exceptions ensure that redemptions that significantly alter the shareholder's relationship with the corporation are taxed as sales rather than dividends.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - Not all redemptions are treated as sales. If a redemption does not meet one of the exceptions outlined in §302 (substantially disproportionate, complete termination, or not essentially equivalent to a dividend), it will be treated as a dividend distribution under §301.

questions.educational: Stock redemptions can be treated as either a sale of stock (capital gain/loss) or as a dividend distribution. The distinction is important because dividends are taxed as ordinary income (potentially at a lower rate than capital gains, but without the benefit of basis offset), while a sale allows for the recovery of basis. The rules under §302 are designed to prevent shareholders from disguising dividend distributions as capital gains through stock redemptions.
questions.examTip: On the EA exam, pay close attention to the shareholder's ownership percentage before and after the redemption, as well as any family attribution rules that may apply when determining if a redemption qualifies as a sale under §302.
questions.memoryAid: Think 'STD' for Stock redemption Treatment as a sale: Substantially disproportionate, Termination of interest, or not Essentially equivalent to a dividend.

questions.question: A C corporation must make estimated tax payments if its expected tax liability is:
questions.explanation: C corporations must make estimated tax payments if the expected tax for the year is $500 or more. Payments are due the 15th of the 4th, 6th, 9th, and 12th months. Each payment is generally 25% of required annual payment.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - $10,000 is not the threshold for mandatory estimated tax payments for C corporations. While a corporation with a large tax liability will certainly be required to make estimated payments, the trigger is much lower.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - $5,000 is not the threshold for mandatory estimated tax payments for C corporations. The actual threshold is significantly lower, requiring more corporations to comply with estimated tax rules.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is CORRECT - C corporations are required to make estimated tax payments if their expected tax liability for the year is $500 or more. This requirement ensures that the IRS receives tax revenue throughout the year, rather than waiting until the corporation's tax return is filed.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - $1,000 is not the threshold for mandatory estimated tax payments for C corporations. The threshold is lower, at $500, meaning more corporations are subject to these rules.

questions.educational: The requirement for C corporations to make estimated tax payments stems from the pay-as-you-go system of taxation. This system, also applied to individuals through withholding and estimated taxes, ensures a steady stream of revenue for the government throughout the year. Failure to make timely and sufficient estimated tax payments can result in penalties under IRC Section 6655, making it crucial for corporations to accurately estimate their tax liability.
questions.examTip: Remember the $500 threshold for C corporation estimated taxes. On the exam, carefully read the facts to determine if the corporation's estimated tax liability exceeds this amount, triggering the estimated tax payment requirement and potential penalties for underpayment.
questions.memoryAid: C-Corps estimate at $500.

questions.question: C corporation tax returns (Form 1120) are due:
options: April 15 (15th day of the 4th month after year-end)
The last day of the 3rd month after year-end
questions.explanation: Form 1120 for C corporations is due the 15th day of the 4th month after the close of the tax year (April 15 for calendar year). A 6-month extension is available via Form 7004.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is CORRECT - C corporations generally file Form 1120, and the due date is the 15th day of the 4th month following the close of the corporation's tax year. For a calendar-year corporation, this date is April 15th.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - September 15 is not the standard due date for C corporation tax returns. While extensions can push the filing deadline into September, the original due date is earlier.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - The last day of the 3rd month after year-end is the due date for partnership tax returns (Form 1065), not C corporation returns (Form 1120).
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - March 15 is the due date for S corporation tax returns (Form 1120-S), not C corporation returns (Form 1120).

questions.educational: Understanding the different due dates for various business entities is crucial for tax compliance. C corporations have a later initial due date than S corporations and partnerships, reflecting the complexity of their tax structure and potential for estimated tax payments. This knowledge is essential for advising clients on timely filing and avoiding penalties.
questions.examTip: Remember the different filing deadlines for various business entities: partnerships and S-corps are due earlier than C-corps. Pay close attention to the entity type in the question stem to avoid easy mistakes.
questions.memoryAid: C-Corps Come Later (than S-Corps and Partnerships).

questions.question: C corporation capital losses:
options: May offset any type of income
May offset up to $3,000 of ordinary income
questions.explanation: Unlike individuals, C corporations may ONLY deduct capital losses against capital gains. There is no $3,000 deduction against ordinary income. Excess capital losses carry back 3 years and forward 5 years (all as short-term).

whyWrong.0: Why option A is CORRECT - C corporations can only deduct capital losses to the extent of capital gains. This is a key difference between the tax treatment of individuals and corporations, as individuals can deduct up to $3,000 of capital losses against ordinary income.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - C corporations are specifically prohibited from offsetting capital losses against ordinary income. This strict limitation is a core rule in corporate tax law.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - Capital losses are deductible by C corporations, but only to the extent of capital gains. They are not completely disallowed, just limited in their application.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - The $3,000 offset against ordinary income is a provision for individual taxpayers, not C corporations. C corporations have no such allowance.

questions.educational: The differing treatment of capital losses between individuals and C corporations reflects the distinct nature of these entities. Individual tax law often aims to provide some relief for investment losses, while corporate tax law tends to be more stringent, focusing on the entity's business operations and investment activities within that context. Understanding these distinctions is crucial for navigating the complexities of business taxation covered in the SEE2 exam.
questions.examTip: Remember that the capital loss rules for C corporations are stricter than those for individuals; focus on the 'capital gain offset only' rule and the carryback/carryforward provisions.
questions.memoryAid: C Corp: Capital losses = Capital gains ONLY. No ordinary income offset.

questions.question: Which of the following business entities does NOT require a formal filing with the state to be created?
questions.explanation: A sole proprietorship does not require any formal state filing to be created. It comes into existence when an individual begins conducting business. LLCs, corporations, and limited partnerships all require articles of organization or incorporation filed with the state.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - A limited liability company (LLC) requires formal filing with the state, typically articles of organization, to be legally recognized and established. Without this filing, the liability protection afforded by the LLC structure is not in effect.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - A C corporation, like an LLC, is a creature of statute and requires filing articles of incorporation with the state. This filing creates the legal entity separate from its owners (shareholders).
whyWrong.2: Why option C is CORRECT - A sole proprietorship is the simplest business structure and arises automatically when an individual begins conducting business activity. No formal filing or registration with the state is generally required to establish a sole proprietorship, although local licenses or permits may be needed.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - A limited partnership (LP) requires a formal filing with the state, typically a certificate of limited partnership, to be properly formed. This filing establishes the LP and defines the roles and liabilities of the general and limited partners.

questions.educational: Entity selection is a crucial decision with significant tax implications. Sole proprietorships offer simplicity but expose the owner to personal liability, while more complex entities like LLCs and corporations provide liability protection but require adherence to state regulations and may involve more complex tax reporting. Understanding these differences is essential for advising clients on the optimal business structure for their specific needs and circumstances.
questions.examTip: On the EA exam, pay close attention to the question's wording regarding 'formal filing with the state' versus local licenses or permits, as many businesses require the latter regardless of their entity type.
questions.memoryAid: SIMPLE: Sole proprietorship = Informal; LLC/Corp/LP = Filed.

questions.question: A newly formed S corporation must generally adopt which tax year?
options: Any fiscal year elected by the shareholders
questions.explanation: S corporations are generally required to adopt a calendar year (ending December 31) as their tax year under IRC §1378. An S corporation may use a fiscal year only if it can establish a business purpose or makes a Section 444 election.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - While shareholders' preferences are considered, IRC §1378 dictates that S corporations generally must adopt a permitted tax year, which is typically a calendar year. Electing a fiscal year solely based on shareholder preference is not sufficient justification.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - IRC §1378 generally requires S corporations to use a calendar year ending December 31 as their tax year. This aligns the S corporation's tax year with that of its shareholders, simplifying tax reporting and preventing income deferral.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - A fiscal year ending September 30 is not a permitted tax year for an S corporation unless a valid business purpose for the fiscal year is established and approved by the IRS, or a Section 444 election is made. Without meeting these conditions, a September 30 year-end is not allowed.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - While a 52-53 week tax year is permissible under certain circumstances, it must still end on a date that aligns with a permitted tax year (typically December 31 for S corporations). A 52-53 week year is not a default option for a newly formed S corporation.

questions.educational: The requirement for S corporations to use a calendar year is primarily designed to prevent income deferral by shareholders. If an S corporation could freely choose a fiscal year, it could potentially delay the reporting of income to its shareholders, resulting in a tax advantage. This rule connects to the broader concept of tax compliance and ensuring fair tax treatment across different business entities.
questions.examTip: Remember that S corporations generally default to a calendar year. On the EA exam, pay close attention to whether the question provides any information about a valid business purpose or a Section 444 election, as these are the only exceptions to the calendar year requirement.
questions.memoryAid: S Corp = Standard Calendar (default)

questions.question: An eligible entity files Form 8832 to elect to be classified as a corporation. When does the election become effective if no effective date is specified on the form?
options: The first day of the next tax year
The date the form is filed
The date the entity was organized
questions.explanation: Under the check-the-box regulations, if no effective date is specified on Form 8832, the election is effective on the date the form is filed. The entity may specify an effective date up to 75 days before or 12 months after the filing date.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - The election does not automatically default to the first day of the next tax year if no effective date is specified. The default is the filing date, offering more immediate flexibility.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - According to Treasury Regulation §301.7701-3(c)(1)(iii), if Form 8832 does not specify an effective date, the election will take effect on the date the form is filed. This provides a clear and immediate default rule for entity classification elections.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - While an entity *can* elect an effective date up to 75 days *before* the filing date, this is only if an effective date is *specified* on Form 8832. If no date is specified, the election is effective on the filing date, not retroactively.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - The date the entity was organized is irrelevant to the effective date of the entity classification election. The election process via Form 8832 determines the entity's classification for tax purposes, independent of its organizational date.

questions.educational: The 'check-the-box' regulations (Treas. Reg. §301.7701-1 through -3) simplify entity classification by allowing eligible entities to choose their tax status. This election is made using Form 8832, and the effective date is crucial for determining when the chosen classification applies. Understanding these regulations is vital for advising clients on the tax implications of their business structure.
questions.examTip: On the EA exam, pay close attention to the wording of entity classification questions. Note whether an effective date is specified on Form 8832, as this determines the default effective date of the election.
questions.memoryAid: No Date, Filing Date!

questions.question: A sole proprietor earned $100,000 in net self-employment income for 2025. What is the self-employment tax rate applied to the taxable self-employment earnings?
questions.explanation: The self-employment tax rate is 15.3%, consisting of 12.4% for Social Security and 2.9% for Medicare. This is applied to 92.35% of net self-employment earnings. The employer-equivalent portion (half) is deductible on Form 1040.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - 12.4% represents only the Social Security portion of the self-employment tax. The total self-employment tax also includes Medicare tax, making this option incomplete.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - The self-employment tax rate is 15.3%, which is the combined rate for Social Security (12.4%) and Medicare (2.9%). This combined rate is applied to the self-employment income after it is reduced by the deduction to arrive at the taxable base.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - 7.65% represents the employee's portion of Social Security and Medicare taxes if they were an employee. Self-employed individuals are responsible for both the employer and employee portions, making this option incorrect.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - 2.9% represents only the Medicare portion of the self-employment tax. The total self-employment tax also includes Social Security tax, making this option incomplete.

questions.educational: Self-employment tax is designed to ensure that self-employed individuals contribute to Social Security and Medicare, similar to how employers and employees contribute through payroll taxes. Because self-employed individuals are both the employer and employee, they pay both portions. Understanding self-employment tax is crucial for accurately calculating a client's tax liability and advising them on strategies to minimize their tax burden.
questions.examTip: Remember to calculate the self-employment tax *before* calculating the self-employment tax deduction on Form 1040, Schedule SE. The deduction is one-half of the self-employment tax.
questions.memoryAid: SE Tax = Social Security + Medicare (12.4% + 2.9% = 15.3%)

questions.question: Which of the following would disqualify a corporation from making an S election?
options: Having 80 shareholders who are U.S. citizens
Having a single class of stock
Having a nonresident alien as a shareholder
questions.explanation: S corporations cannot have nonresident alien shareholders under IRC §1361(b). Other requirements include having no more than 100 shareholders, only one class of stock, being a domestic corporation, and only individuals, estates, and certain trusts as shareholders.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - S corporations can have up to 100 shareholders. Having 80 shareholders who are U.S. citizens does not violate this requirement and would not disqualify the corporation from making an S election.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - S corporations are permitted to have only one class of stock. Having a single class of stock is a requirement for S corporation status, not a disqualification.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is CORRECT - S corporations are prohibited from having nonresident alien shareholders under IRC §1361(b)(1)(C). This restriction ensures that all shareholders are subject to U.S. tax laws and reporting requirements, simplifying tax administration.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - To be eligible for S corporation status, a corporation must be a domestic corporation, meaning it is created or organized in the United States or under the laws of the United States or any state. Being a domestic corporation is a requirement, not a disqualification.

questions.educational: S corporation eligibility requirements are designed to simplify tax administration and ensure compliance with U.S. tax laws. The restrictions on the types of shareholders and the number of shareholders prevent complex ownership structures that would make it difficult to track income and allocate it to shareholders. This aligns with the pass-through nature of S corporations, where income is taxed at the individual shareholder level.
questions.examTip: Remember the specific eligibility requirements for S corporations, particularly the restrictions on shareholder types and the number of shareholders, as these are frequently tested on the EA exam.
questions.memoryAid: Think 'DRONES' to remember S-Corp requirements: Domestic, Resident (no nonresident aliens), One class of stock, Number (no more than 100), Eligible shareholders (individuals, estates, certain trusts), Social Security Number (required for all shareholders).

questions.question: What is the minimum number of members required to form a partnership for federal tax purposes?
questions.explanation: A partnership for federal tax purposes requires at least two members. Under IRC §761, a partnership includes a syndicate, group, pool, joint venture, or other unincorporated organization through which any business is carried on and that is not classified as a corporation or trust.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - A single individual operating a business is considered a sole proprietorship, not a partnership. A partnership, by definition, requires more than one person.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - A partnership, as defined by IRC §761, requires at least two members to carry on a business for profit. The essence of a partnership lies in the agreement and collaboration between two or more individuals or entities.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - While some partnerships may have three or more members, the minimum requirement for forming a partnership is two. Three members are not required to meet the basic definition of a partnership.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - Five members are not required to form a partnership. The minimum requirement is two, and partnerships can exist with any number of members greater than or equal to two.

questions.educational: The partnership structure allows for the pooling of resources, expertise, and capital, which is why it requires at least two members. This structure contrasts with sole proprietorships (one owner) and corporations (separate legal entities). Understanding entity formation is crucial for advising clients on the most suitable business structure for their needs, a key aspect of the Enrolled Agent's role.
questions.examTip: Remember that the IRS looks to the substance of the relationship, not just the label. Even if parties don't explicitly call themselves a partnership, their actions can create one for tax purposes, triggering partnership tax rules.
questions.memoryAid: Partnership = Paired (at least two)

questions.question: Which schedule does a sole proprietor use to report business income and expenses on their Form 1040?
questions.explanation: Sole proprietors report business income and expenses on Schedule C (Profit or Loss from Business) of Form 1040. Schedule A is for itemized deductions, Schedule B for interest and dividends, and Schedule E for rental and partnership income.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - Schedule A is used to report itemized deductions, such as medical expenses, state and local taxes (SALT), and charitable contributions. These are personal expenses, not business income or expenses related to a sole proprietorship.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - Schedule B is used to report interest and dividend income. This type of income is generally considered investment income, not income generated from the operation of a sole proprietorship.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is CORRECT - Schedule C, Profit or Loss From Business (Sole Proprietorship), is specifically designed for sole proprietors to report their business income and expenses. This schedule calculates the net profit or loss from the business, which is then transferred to Form 1040.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - Schedule E is used to report supplemental income and loss from activities such as rental real estate, royalties, partnerships, S corporations, and estates and trusts. While a sole proprietor might have rental income or partnership income, the direct income and expenses from their sole proprietorship business are reported on Schedule C.

questions.educational: Sole proprietorships are the simplest business structure, where the business is not separate from its owner for tax purposes. This means the business's profits are taxed at the individual level. Schedule C allows for the direct reporting of business income and expenses, simplifying the tax filing process for these businesses and ensuring that all business-related financial activity is accounted for when calculating the individual's overall tax liability.
questions.examTip: Remember that the net profit or loss calculated on Schedule C is subject to both income tax and self-employment tax. Be prepared to calculate self-employment tax on the exam, which is reported on Schedule SE.
questions.memoryAid: C for Company (even though it's a sole proprietorship), C for reporting business income and expenses.

questions.question: ABC Corp, a C corporation, earns $200,000 of taxable income taxed at 21%. It distributes the remaining $158,000 as dividends to its sole shareholder who is in the 15% qualified dividend tax bracket. What is the total combined tax on this income?
questions.explanation: The corporation pays $42,000 in tax ($200,000 × 21%). The remaining $158,000 distributed as qualified dividends is taxed at 15%, resulting in $23,700 of individual tax. The total combined tax is $42,000 + $23,700 = $65,700, illustrating double taxation of C corporation earnings.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - This option only calculates the corporate tax and omits the individual tax on the qualified dividends. Remember to consider both the corporate and shareholder-level taxes when analyzing C corporation distributions.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - This option incorrectly calculates either the corporate tax or the individual tax on the qualified dividends. Double-check your calculations for both the corporate tax (21% of $200,000) and the individual tax (15% of $158,000).
whyWrong.2: Why option C is CORRECT - The C corporation pays corporate income tax on its taxable income. In this case, the corporate tax is $200,000 * 21% = $42,000. The remaining income is distributed as dividends, which are then taxed at the shareholder level. The dividend tax is $158,000 * 15% = $23,700. The total combined tax is $42,000 + $23,700 = $65,700.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - This option overstates the total combined tax. It likely involves an error in calculating either the corporate tax or the individual tax on the qualified dividends, or both. Ensure you are using the correct tax rates.

questions.educational: C corporations are subject to double taxation: once at the corporate level when income is earned and again at the shareholder level when dividends are distributed. This contrasts with pass-through entities like S corporations and partnerships, where income is generally taxed only at the owner level. Understanding the tax implications of different entity structures is crucial for advising clients on the most advantageous business form.
questions.examTip: On the EA exam, carefully identify the entity type and its tax implications before calculating the total tax liability. Pay close attention to whether the income is subject to single or double taxation.
questions.memoryAid: C Corp = Double Tax (Corporate and then individual)

questions.question: When a C corporation converts to an S corporation, what tax applies to built-in gains recognized during the recognition period?
options: No tax applies because S corporations are pass-through entities
The built-in gains tax at the highest corporate rate of 21%
Capital gains tax at the individual shareholder level only
Alternative minimum tax on the corporation
questions.explanation: Under IRC §1374, when a C corporation converts to an S corporation, a built-in gains (BIG) tax applies at the highest corporate rate (21%) on net recognized built-in gains during the 5-year recognition period. This prevents C corporations from converting to S status to avoid corporate-level tax on appreciated assets.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - While S corporations are generally pass-through entities, the built-in gains tax is an exception. IRC §1374 specifically imposes a corporate-level tax on certain gains recognized within a specified period after conversion from a C corporation.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - IRC §1374 imposes a built-in gains (BIG) tax on an S corporation that was formerly a C corporation. This tax is calculated by applying the highest corporate tax rate (currently 21%) to the S corporation's net recognized built-in gains during the 5-year recognition period following the conversion from C to S status. The purpose is to prevent corporations from avoiding corporate-level tax on appreciated assets by electing S corporation status.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - While S corporation income generally flows through to the shareholders and is taxed at the individual level, the built-in gains tax is initially assessed at the corporate level before any income passes through to the shareholders. The capital gains tax at the individual level would apply to the shareholder's share of the *remaining* income after the BIG tax is paid.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - The alternative minimum tax (AMT) has been repealed for C corporations for tax years beginning after 2017. Furthermore, the built-in gains tax is a separate tax from the AMT, specifically designed to address the conversion from C to S corporation status.

questions.educational: The built-in gains tax under IRC §1374 is a crucial concept for the EA exam, particularly when advising clients on entity selection and conversions. It prevents C corporations from avoiding corporate-level tax on appreciated assets by electing S corporation status shortly before selling those assets. Understanding the 5-year recognition period and the calculation of the tax is essential.
questions.examTip: Remember that the built-in gains tax applies only if the S corporation was previously a C corporation and recognizes gains on assets held at the time of conversion within the 5-year recognition period. Pay close attention to the dates in exam questions to determine if the BIG tax applies.
questions.memoryAid: BIG tax = Formerly a C-corp, now an S-corp, with Built-In Gains during the 5-year recognition period.

questions.question: Which form is used to apply for an Employer Identification Number (EIN)?
questions.explanation: Form SS-4, Application for Employer Identification Number, is used to obtain an EIN from the IRS. An EIN is required for partnerships, corporations, and most LLCs. Sole proprietors may need an EIN if they have employees or certain other requirements.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - Form W-9, Request for Taxpayer Identification Number and Certification, is used by businesses to collect taxpayer identification information (TIN) from independent contractors or vendors they pay. It's not used to apply for an EIN.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - Form SS-4, Application for Employer Identification Number (EIN), is the correct form to use when applying for an EIN from the IRS. An EIN is a unique nine-digit number assigned by the IRS to business entities operating in the United States. This form gathers information about the entity, its responsible party, and the reason for needing an EIN.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - Form W-4, Employee's Withholding Certificate, is completed by employees and given to their employers. It's used to determine the amount of federal income tax to withhold from the employee's wages, not to apply for an EIN.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - Form 8832, Entity Classification Election, is used by eligible entities to elect how they will be classified for federal tax purposes (e.g., corporation, partnership, or disregarded entity). While related to entity formation, it's not the form used to obtain an EIN.

questions.educational: The Employer Identification Number (EIN) is crucial for business entities as it serves as their taxpayer identification number with the IRS. It's analogous to a Social Security number for individuals. Understanding when an EIN is required (e.g., for corporations, partnerships, LLCs with multiple members, or sole proprietorships with employees) and how to obtain one is fundamental for tax compliance and is frequently tested on the SEE exam.
questions.examTip: On the EA exam, pay close attention to the context of the question. If the question asks about *applying* for an EIN, Form SS-4 is almost always the correct answer. Don't confuse it with forms used for withholding or entity classification.
questions.memoryAid: SS-4: 'SS' sounds like 'Employer,' and 4 looks like 'E' in EIN. SS-4 = Employer Identification Number Application.

questions.question: Form 2553 to elect S corporation status must be filed by the 15th day of the _____ month of the tax year the election is to take effect.
questions.explanation: Form 2553 must be filed no later than 2 months and 15 days (the 15th day of the 3rd month) after the beginning of the tax year the election is to take effect. It may also be filed at any time during the preceding tax year.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - The election to be treated as an S corporation using Form 2553 is not due by the 15th day of the first month of the tax year. This is too early in the tax year.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - The election to be treated as an S corporation using Form 2553 is not due by the 15th day of the second month of the tax year. The deadline extends further into the tax year.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is CORRECT - To be effective for the current tax year, Form 2553 must be filed no later than 2 months and 15 days after the beginning of the tax year, which falls on the 15th day of the 3rd month. This allows the IRS sufficient time to process the election and ensures the corporation is taxed appropriately from the start of the year.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - The election to be treated as an S corporation using Form 2553 is not due by the 15th day of the sixth month of the tax year. Filing this late would likely result in the S-election being effective for the *following* tax year, not the current one.

questions.educational: The S corporation election allows a qualifying corporation to pass its income, losses, deductions, and credits through to its shareholders, avoiding corporate income tax. This election is governed by IRC Section 1362, and timely filing of Form 2553 is crucial for the election to be valid for the desired tax year. Understanding entity selection and its implications on taxation is a core concept tested in the Business Taxation section.
questions.examTip: Pay close attention to deadlines for various elections, especially the S corporation election. The IRS is strict about these deadlines, and missing them can have significant tax consequences for your clients.
questions.memoryAid: Think 'March 15th' - the 3rd month, 15th day, is the key date for S-corp election filing (when the tax year aligns with the calendar year).

questions.question: A sole proprietor uses a room in their home exclusively and regularly as their principal place of business. The room is 200 square feet. Using the simplified method, what is the maximum home office deduction?
questions.explanation: The simplified method allows a deduction of $5 per square foot, up to a maximum of 300 square feet ($1,500 maximum). For a 200-square-foot office, the deduction is 200 × $5 = $1,000. The simplified method was introduced as an alternative to the regular method of calculating actual expenses.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - This option represents the deduction for only 100 square feet of office space using the simplified method ($5/sq ft * 100 sq ft = $500). The problem states the office is 200 square feet, making this calculation incorrect.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - The simplified method for calculating the home office deduction allows $5 per square foot, up to a maximum of 300 square feet. With a 200-square-foot office, the deduction is calculated as 200 sq ft * $5/sq ft = $1,000. This amount is within the maximum limit and therefore the correct deduction.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - This option represents the maximum deduction allowed under the simplified method (300 sq ft * $5/sq ft = $1,500). While this is the maximum *possible* deduction, it's only applicable if the home office is 300 square feet or more. The office in this question is only 200 square feet.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - This option is significantly higher than the maximum deduction allowed under the simplified method. The simplified method is capped at 300 square feet, resulting in a maximum deduction of $1,500 (300 sq ft * $5/sq ft). $5,000 is not a valid deduction amount under the simplified method.

questions.educational: The home office deduction allows taxpayers to deduct expenses related to using a portion of their home for business. The simplified method offers an easier calculation, using a prescribed rate per square foot, but it's crucial to remember the 300 square foot limit. Understanding both the simplified and regular methods, including eligibility requirements and expense limitations, is essential for the SEE exam.
questions.examTip: On the EA exam, pay close attention to whether the question specifies the simplified or regular method for the home office deduction. If the regular method is required, remember to consider direct and indirect expenses and their limitations.
questions.memoryAid: Think of '5 and 3' - $5 per square foot, up to 300 square feet for the simplified home office deduction.

questions.question: If a partnership agreement is silent on allocations of income, how are profits and losses allocated among partners?
options: Equally among all partners regardless of capital contributions
Based on each partner's capital account balance
Based on each partner's services performed
questions.explanation: Under IRC §704(b), if the partnership agreement does not specify how to allocate income, losses, deductions, or credits, they are allocated according to the partners' interests in the partnership. In most states under the Uniform Partnership Act, if no agreement exists, profits and losses are shared equally.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is CORRECT - IRC §704(b) dictates that if the partnership agreement is silent on allocations, profits and losses are allocated according to the partners' interests in the partnership. In the absence of a defined interest, the default under most state partnership laws (like the Uniform Partnership Act) is equal allocation, regardless of capital contributions or services performed.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - While capital account balances are important for determining a partner's basis and distributions, they do not dictate profit and loss allocation when the partnership agreement is silent. The default rule is equal allocation, not allocation based on capital.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - Allocation based on services performed is a valid method if specified in the partnership agreement, but it is not the default rule when the agreement is silent. The default is equal allocation, regardless of services performed.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - While capital contributions can influence allocations if explicitly stated in the partnership agreement, the default rule when the agreement is silent is equal allocation. Capital contributions only matter if the agreement specifies it.

questions.educational: Partnership taxation emphasizes flexibility, allowing partners to tailor allocations through their partnership agreement. However, the IRS requires these allocations to have 'substantial economic effect' to prevent tax avoidance. When the agreement is silent, the law provides a default mechanism to ensure fairness and prevent disputes, which is generally equal allocation.
questions.examTip: Remember that the partnership agreement is king, but silence defaults to equality. On the EA exam, carefully read the facts to determine if the partnership agreement is silent on allocations before applying the default rule.
questions.memoryAid: Silent Agreement = Equal Shares (SA=ES)

questions.question: A C corporation files its tax return on which form?
questions.explanation: C corporations file their federal income tax return on Form 1120, U.S. Corporation Income Tax Return. Form 1065 is for partnerships, Form 1120-S is for S corporations, and Form 1040 is for individuals.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - Form 1040 is used by individuals to report their personal income and calculate their individual income tax liability. C corporations are separate legal entities from their owners and therefore do not use Form 1040.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - Form 1065, U.S. Return of Partnership Income, is used by partnerships to report their income, deductions, and credits. Partnerships are pass-through entities and do not pay income tax at the entity level; instead, the income flows through to the partners who report it on their individual income tax returns.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is CORRECT - C corporations are required to file Form 1120, U.S. Corporation Income Tax Return, to report their income, deductions, and calculate their corporate income tax liability. C corporations are subject to double taxation: once at the corporate level and again when dividends are distributed to shareholders.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - Form 1120-S, U.S. Income Tax Return for an S Corporation, is used by S corporations to report their income, deductions, and credits. Like partnerships, S corporations are pass-through entities, but they have different rules regarding shareholder compensation and distributions.

questions.educational: The choice of entity (C corporation, S corporation, partnership, etc.) significantly impacts the tax implications for a business. C corporations are subject to corporate income tax, while S corporations and partnerships are pass-through entities. Understanding these differences is crucial for advising clients on the most advantageous entity structure for their specific circumstances, impacting both current and future tax liabilities.
questions.examTip: On the EA exam, pay close attention to the entity type specified in the question. A seemingly small detail like 'C corporation' versus 'S corporation' completely changes the correct form and tax treatment.
questions.memoryAid: 1120 = C Corp. Think 'C' for Corporation and '1120' for Corporate form.

questions.question: Which of the following trusts is eligible to be an S corporation shareholder?
options: A complex trust with corporate beneficiaries
questions.explanation: An electing small business trust (ESBT) is an eligible S corporation shareholder under IRC §1361(e). Foreign trusts, charitable remainder trusts, and most complex trusts with ineligible beneficiaries cannot hold S corporation stock. Qualified subchapter S trusts (QSSTs) and grantor trusts are also eligible.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - A foreign trust is not a permitted S corporation shareholder. IRC §1361(c)(2)(A) outlines the permissible shareholders, and foreign trusts are not included in this list due to concerns about tax enforcement and compliance with U.S. tax laws.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - A charitable remainder trust (CRT) is generally not a permitted S corporation shareholder. CRTs are tax-exempt entities under IRC §664, and allowing them to hold S corporation stock would create complexities in determining the taxable income passed through to the beneficiaries and potentially jeopardize the CRT's tax-exempt status.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is CORRECT - An electing small business trust (ESBT) is a specifically permitted S corporation shareholder under IRC §1361(c)(2)(A)(v) and §1361(e). The ESBT structure allows for more flexibility in trust terms and beneficiary types compared to other eligible trusts, making it a useful tool for estate planning while still complying with S corporation requirements.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - A complex trust with corporate beneficiaries is generally not a permitted S corporation shareholder. While individuals and certain other trusts are allowed, corporate beneficiaries can complicate the pass-through taxation of S corporation income and potentially circumvent the restrictions on the number and types of shareholders an S corporation can have.

questions.educational: S corporations have strict rules regarding who can be a shareholder to prevent tax avoidance and maintain simplicity in the pass-through taxation system. Permitted shareholders include individuals, estates, certain trusts (like ESBTs and QSSTs), and certain tax-exempt organizations. Understanding these restrictions is crucial for advising clients on entity selection and ensuring compliance with S corporation regulations.
questions.examTip: Pay close attention to the specific types of trusts that are eligible S corporation shareholders, as this is a frequently tested topic on the EA exam. Remember the acronyms QSST and ESBT and what they stand for.
questions.memoryAid: Think of 'ESBT' as 'Easy S-corp Beneficiary Trust' – it's designed to make S-corp ownership easier for trusts.

questions.question: A business owner wants to minimize self-employment tax while maintaining limited liability. Which entity type would BEST accomplish this goal?
questions.explanation: An S corporation allows the owner to receive a reasonable salary (subject to employment taxes) while taking additional profits as distributions not subject to self-employment tax. Sole proprietorships and single-member LLCs (default) subject all net income to self-employment tax. General partnerships do not provide limited liability.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - A sole proprietorship does not provide limited liability. Furthermore, all net income from the business is subject to self-employment tax, making it a poor choice for minimizing this tax.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - A general partnership does not provide limited liability to its partners. Each partner is jointly and severally liable for the partnership's debts and obligations. Also, a partner's distributive share of partnership income is subject to self-employment tax.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is CORRECT - An S corporation allows the owner to be an employee and receive a reasonable salary subject to employment taxes (Social Security and Medicare). The remaining profits can be distributed as dividends, which are not subject to self-employment tax. This structure provides both limited liability and the potential to minimize self-employment tax.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - A single-member LLC, by default, is treated as a disregarded entity for tax purposes, meaning its income is reported on Schedule C of the owner's Form 1040 and is subject to self-employment tax. While it offers limited liability, it doesn't help minimize self-employment tax compared to an S corporation.

questions.educational: The choice of business entity significantly impacts taxation and liability. S corporations are a hybrid, offering limited liability like a corporation but pass-through taxation similar to a partnership. However, S corporations require owners who materially participate to take a reasonable salary subject to payroll taxes, while distributions above that salary are not subject to self-employment tax, potentially reducing the overall tax burden. Understanding the nuances of entity selection is crucial for effective tax planning and compliance, a core competency for Enrolled Agents.
questions.examTip: On the EA exam, pay close attention to the question's objective. If the question asks about minimizing self-employment tax *and* maintaining limited liability, an S corporation is often the best answer. Remember the 'reasonable salary' requirement for S corporation shareholder-employees.
questions.memoryAid: S Corp = Salary + Savings (on self-employment tax)

questions.question: A sole proprietor expects to owe $2,000 in tax for the current year. Which of the following is true regarding estimated tax payments?
options: Estimated tax payments are not required because the amount is under $5,000
The sole proprietor must make estimated tax payments if they expect to owe $1,000 or more
Estimated tax payments are only required for C corporations
Estimated tax payments are due in two installments
questions.explanation: Generally, a taxpayer must make estimated tax payments if they expect to owe at least $1,000 in tax after subtracting withholding and refundable credits. Estimated tax is paid in four installments due April 15, June 15, September 15, and January 15 of the following year.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - The threshold for requiring estimated tax payments is generally $1,000, not $5,000. If a taxpayer expects to owe $1,000 or more in tax, they are generally required to make estimated tax payments to avoid penalties.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - Generally, an individual, including a sole proprietor, must make estimated tax payments if they expect to owe at least $1,000 in tax after subtracting their withholding and refundable credits. This ensures that income tax is paid throughout the year rather than all at once at the end of the tax year.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - Estimated tax payments are required for individuals, including sole proprietors, partners, and S corporation shareholders, in addition to C corporations. The requirement is based on the expected tax liability, not the entity type.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - Estimated tax payments are generally due in four installments, not two. The standard due dates are April 15, June 15, September 15, and January 15 of the following year (subject to adjustments for weekends and holidays).

questions.educational: The estimated tax payment system exists to ensure the government receives tax revenue throughout the year, rather than waiting until the tax filing deadline. This is particularly important for individuals with income not subject to withholding, such as self-employment income. Understanding estimated tax rules is crucial for Enrolled Agents to advise clients on avoiding underpayment penalties, which are a common area of IRS scrutiny.
questions.examTip: Pay close attention to the specific thresholds for estimated tax payments and the potential penalties for underpayment. Remember the $1,000 threshold and the four installment due dates, as these are frequently tested on the EA exam.
questions.memoryAid: Think of '1000' as 'a grand' you might owe, triggering estimated taxes.

questions.question: A domestic partnership files its information return on which form?
questions.explanation: Domestic partnerships file their annual information return on Form 1065, U.S. Return of Partnership Income. A partnership is a pass-through entity and generally does not pay income tax itself. Each partner receives a Schedule K-1 reporting their share of partnership items.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - Form 1120 is used by C corporations to report their income, deductions, and credits. Partnerships, as pass-through entities, do not use this form.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - Domestic partnerships are required to file Form 1065, U.S. Return of Partnership Income, annually to report their income, deductions, gains, losses, etc. This form provides information to the IRS and the partners regarding the partnership's financial activities. Each partner then receives a Schedule K-1 detailing their share of these items.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - Form 1040 is the individual income tax return used by individuals to report their personal income, deductions, and credits. While partners report their share of partnership income on Schedule E of Form 1040, the partnership itself does not file Form 1040.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - Form 990 is used by tax-exempt organizations, such as charities and non-profits, to report their financial information to the IRS. Partnerships are not tax-exempt organizations and therefore do not file Form 990.

questions.educational: Partnerships are pass-through entities, meaning the partnership itself generally does not pay income tax. Instead, the partnership's income and deductions are passed through to the partners, who report their share on their individual income tax returns. Form 1065 serves as an informational return, providing the IRS with details of the partnership's operations and the allocation of income and deductions to each partner, ensuring proper taxation at the individual level.
questions.examTip: Remember that Form 1065 is an information return, not a tax payment form, for partnerships; the partners pay the tax on their individual returns (Form 1040) based on their Schedule K-1.
questions.memoryAid: 1065: 'Partners GEt to FIVE' (Form 1065).

questions.question: For a calendar-year partnership, the due date for filing Form 1065 is:
questions.explanation: Calendar-year partnerships must file Form 1065 by March 15 of the following year. An automatic 6-month extension to September 15 is available by filing Form 7004. S corporations also share the March 15 due date, while C corporations file by April 15.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is CORRECT - Partnerships, like S corporations, are pass-through entities, meaning the income or loss flows through to the partners' individual tax returns. To allow partners sufficient time to prepare their individual returns (Form 1040) which are due April 15th, the partnership return (Form 1065) is due earlier, on March 15th, for calendar-year partnerships.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - April 15th is the due date for individual income tax returns (Form 1040) and C corporation income tax returns (Form 1120). Partnerships need to file earlier to allow partners time to prepare their individual returns.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - September 15th is the extended due date for partnership returns (Form 1065) if Form 7004, Application for Automatic Extension of Time To File Certain Business Income Tax, Information, and Other Returns, is filed by the original due date of March 15th. This is not the original due date.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - October 15th is the extended due date for individual income tax returns (Form 1040) if an extension was filed. It is not related to the filing of partnership returns.

questions.educational: Partnerships operate under a pass-through taxation system, where the partnership itself doesn't pay income tax. Instead, the partnership's income, losses, deductions, and credits are passed through to the partners, who then report these items on their individual income tax returns. The earlier filing deadline for partnerships ensures partners have the necessary information (Schedule K-1) to accurately prepare their individual returns by the April 15th deadline.
questions.examTip: Remember the March 15th due date for partnerships and S corporations. A common trick on the EA exam is to mix up entity filing deadlines, so create a table summarizing the due dates for different entity types.
questions.memoryAid: Think 'Partnerships Pass Through Early' - Partnership returns are due earlier (March 15th) to allow partners time to prepare their individual returns.

questions.question: What is the federal income tax rate for C corporations under current law?
questions.explanation: The Tax Cuts and Jobs Act of 2017 established a flat 21% corporate tax rate for C corporations, replacing the previous graduated rate structure that ranged from 15% to 35%. This rate applies to all taxable income regardless of amount.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - The 15% rate was a lower end of the graduated tax bracket system for C corporations before the Tax Cuts and Jobs Act (TCJA) of 2017. It is no longer the applicable rate.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - The Tax Cuts and Jobs Act (TCJA) of 2017 established a flat corporate income tax rate of 21% for C corporations. This rate applies to all levels of taxable income, simplifying the previous graduated rate structure.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - 28% is not the current federal income tax rate for C corporations. While some pass-through entities may be subject to rates in this range, it does not apply to C corporations.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - 35% was the highest marginal tax rate for C corporations under the graduated tax rate system that existed prior to the Tax Cuts and Jobs Act (TCJA) of 2017. It is no longer the applicable rate.

questions.educational: The flat 21% corporate tax rate was implemented to simplify the tax code and potentially incentivize business investment. Understanding the tax rates for different entity types is crucial for advising clients on the most advantageous business structure, a key aspect of the Enrolled Agent's role in tax planning and compliance.
questions.examTip: Remember that the 21% corporate tax rate applies to C corporations only; other business entities such as partnerships and S corporations are taxed differently at the owner level.
questions.memoryAid: C Corp = 21 (Think 'C' for Corporation and '21' for the tax rate)

questions.question: In a limited partnership, which partner has unlimited personal liability for partnership debts?
options: Only the partner with the largest capital contribution
questions.explanation: In a limited partnership, the general partner has unlimited personal liability for partnership debts and obligations. Limited partners have liability limited to their investment in the partnership as long as they do not participate in management.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - Limited partners, by definition, have limited liability, meaning their personal assets are generally protected from partnership debts as long as they do not actively participate in the management of the partnership. Their liability is typically limited to their capital contribution.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - In a limited partnership, the general partner(s) assume full personal liability for the debts and obligations of the partnership. This unlimited liability is a key characteristic distinguishing general partners from limited partners and reflects their active role in managing the business.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - This statement is incorrect because, in a limited partnership, at least one partner (the general partner) must have unlimited liability. The structure of a limited partnership is designed to offer limited liability to some partners (limited partners) while assigning unlimited liability to others (general partners).
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - A partner's capital contribution does not determine their liability in a limited partnership. Liability is determined by their role as either a general partner (unlimited liability) or a limited partner (limited liability), irrespective of the amount of capital contributed.

questions.educational: Limited partnerships are designed to attract investors who want to limit their personal risk. The general partner takes on the risk of unlimited liability in exchange for control over the business operations. This structure is often used in real estate and investment ventures, balancing the need for capital with the desire to protect personal assets. Understanding the liability differences between general and limited partners is crucial for advising clients on the appropriate business structure.
questions.examTip: Remember that the general partner's unlimited liability in a limited partnership is a fundamental concept tested on the EA exam. Pay close attention to questions that involve the roles and responsibilities of different partners.
questions.memoryAid: GULP: General partners = Unlimited Liability; Limited partners = Protected (liability limited to investment).

questions.question: A sole proprietor had gross receipts of $150,000, cost of goods sold of $60,000, and business expenses of $35,000. What is the net profit reported on Schedule C?
questions.explanation: Net profit on Schedule C is calculated as gross receipts ($150,000) minus cost of goods sold ($60,000) = gross profit of $90,000, minus business expenses ($35,000) = net profit of $55,000. This amount flows to Form 1040 and is also subject to self-employment tax.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - This answer only considers the business expenses and ignores the gross receipts and cost of goods sold, leading to a significant underestimation of the net profit.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - Schedule C net profit is calculated by subtracting the cost of goods sold and business expenses from gross receipts. In this case, $150,000 (gross receipts) - $60,000 (cost of goods sold) - $35,000 (business expenses) = $55,000 net profit.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - This answer only subtracts the cost of goods sold from the gross receipts, resulting in the gross profit ($90,000), but it fails to account for the business expenses, which must also be deducted to arrive at the net profit.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - This answer incorrectly adds the cost of goods sold to the gross receipts before subtracting the business expenses. The cost of goods sold should always be subtracted from gross receipts to arrive at gross profit.

questions.educational: Schedule C reports the profit or loss from a sole proprietorship. It's crucial to understand the distinction between gross receipts, cost of goods sold, gross profit, and net profit. The net profit from Schedule C is not only subject to income tax but also to self-employment tax, covering Social Security and Medicare taxes for the self-employed individual. This is a key difference from employees who have these taxes withheld by their employer.
questions.examTip: Remember to always deduct both the cost of goods sold AND business expenses from gross receipts when calculating net profit on Schedule C. Overlooking either deduction is a common mistake on the EA exam.
questions.memoryAid: GRACE: Gross Receipts - COGS = Adjusted Gross profit - Expenses = Net profit.

questions.question: The Section 199A qualified business income (QBI) deduction is available to owners of which type of entity?
options: Pass-through entities including sole proprietorships, S corporations, and partnerships
All business entities including C corporations
