questions.explanation: The Section 199A QBI deduction is available to owners of pass-through entities, including sole proprietorships, S corporations, partnerships, and certain trusts and estates. C corporation income is not eligible for the QBI deduction because it is taxed at the entity level.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - C corporations are taxed at the corporate level, and their income is not eligible for the Section 199A QBI deduction. The deduction is designed to provide tax relief to owners of pass-through businesses.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - While S corporations and partnerships are eligible for the Section 199A QBI deduction, this option incorrectly excludes sole proprietorships, which are also pass-through entities eligible for the deduction.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is CORRECT - The Section 199A qualified business income (QBI) deduction is specifically designed for owners of pass-through entities. These entities, including sole proprietorships, S corporations, partnerships, and certain trusts and estates, pass their income through to the owners, who then report it on their individual income tax returns and may be eligible for the deduction.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - C corporations are not pass-through entities and are taxed at the corporate level. Therefore, their income is not eligible for the Section 199A QBI deduction, making this option overly broad and incorrect.

questions.educational: The Section 199A QBI deduction was introduced as part of the Tax Cuts and Jobs Act of 2017 to provide a tax benefit to owners of pass-through businesses, aiming to create a more level playing field between these businesses and C corporations, which received a significant tax rate reduction. Understanding the nuances of this deduction is crucial for advising clients on business structure and tax planning strategies.
questions.examTip: On the EA exam, pay close attention to the specific entity type mentioned in the question stem when assessing eligibility for the QBI deduction. Remember that the deduction is calculated at the individual level, not the entity level.
questions.memoryAid: QBI = Pass-Through Benefit (QBI deduction is for pass-through entities)

questions.question: Which of the following events would cause an involuntary termination of an S corporation election?
options: Issuing a second class of stock with different voting rights but identical distribution and liquidation rights
A shareholder transferring stock to a partnership
questions.explanation: Transferring S corporation stock to a partnership causes an involuntary termination because partnerships are not eligible shareholders under IRC §1361(b)(1)(B). Having 99 shareholders is within the 100-shareholder limit. Different voting rights alone do not create a second class of stock. Late filing does not terminate the election.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - Filing Form 1120-S late results in penalties and potential IRS scrutiny, but it does *not* automatically terminate the S corporation election. The IRS may revoke the election in extreme cases of non-compliance, but a single late filing is unlikely to trigger this.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - An S corporation can have different voting rights among its shares without violating the single class of stock requirement, as long as all shares have identical rights to distribution and liquidation proceeds. IRC §1361(c)(4) specifically allows for differences in voting rights.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is CORRECT - Partnerships are ineligible shareholders in an S corporation. Transferring stock to a partnership violates IRC §1361(b)(1)(B), which specifies who can be shareholders. This transfer immediately terminates the S corporation election because the corporation no longer meets the eligibility requirements.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - An S corporation can have up to 100 shareholders. Having 99 shareholders is within the legal limit and does not cause an involuntary termination of the S corporation election. The limit is strictly enforced, but 99 is perfectly acceptable.

questions.educational: S corporation eligibility rules are strict to maintain the simplicity of pass-through taxation. The rules regarding eligible shareholders (individuals, estates, and certain trusts) and the single class of stock requirement are designed to prevent complex ownership structures that would complicate the allocation of income and losses. Understanding these rules is crucial for advising clients on entity selection and maintaining S corporation status.
questions.examTip: Pay close attention to the details of S corporation shareholder eligibility on the EA exam. Questions often test your knowledge of who can and cannot be a shareholder, including the types of trusts that are permitted.
questions.memoryAid: S Corp Shareholders: Think 'I Eat Certain Treats' - Individuals, Estates, Certain Trusts (eligible).

questions.question: Under the check-the-box regulations, what is the default classification of a newly formed domestic entity with two or more members that does not file Form 8832?
questions.explanation: Under Treas. Reg. §301.7701-3, a domestic eligible entity with two or more members is classified as a partnership by default. A single-member entity defaults to a disregarded entity. The entity can elect a different classification by filing Form 8832.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - A corporation is not the default classification for a domestic entity with two or more members under the check-the-box regulations. While an entity can elect to be taxed as a corporation by filing Form 8832, the default is partnership taxation.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - Under Treas. Reg. §301.7701-3(b)(1)(i), a domestic eligible entity with two or more members that does not elect its classification is classified as a partnership for federal tax purposes. This default classification allows for flow-through taxation, where the entity's income and losses are passed through to its members.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - A disregarded entity is the default classification only for a domestic eligible entity with a single member, per Treas. Reg. §301.7701-3(b)(1)(ii). Since the question specifies two or more members, this option is incorrect.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - S corporation status is not a default classification. An entity must first be a corporation (or elect to be treated as one) and then file Form 2553 to elect S corporation status. This election is separate from the check-the-box regulations.

questions.educational: The check-the-box regulations (Treas. Reg. §301.7701-1 through 301.7701-3) provide simplicity and flexibility in entity classification for federal tax purposes. These regulations allow eligible entities to choose their tax classification, avoiding the rigid, formalistic rules that previously governed entity classification. Understanding these rules is crucial for advising clients on the optimal entity structure for their business.
questions.examTip: Remember the default classifications: single-member LLCs are disregarded entities, and multi-member LLCs are partnerships, unless Form 8832 is filed. On the EA exam, pay close attention to the number of members in the entity when determining the default classification.
questions.memoryAid: Two or more? Partnership by default! (Think: 'Two' partners.)

questions.question: A partner contributes property with a fair market value of $50,000 and an adjusted basis of $30,000 to a partnership in exchange for a partnership interest. What is the partner's basis in the partnership interest?
questions.explanation: Under IRC §722, a partner's basis in a partnership interest received in exchange for a contribution of property is equal to the adjusted basis of the contributed property ($30,000). No gain or loss is generally recognized on the contribution under IRC §721.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - The fair market value of the contributed property ($50,000) is not used to determine the partner's basis in the partnership interest. The basis is determined by the adjusted basis of the property contributed.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - Under IRC §722, the contributing partner's basis in the partnership interest is equal to the adjusted basis of the property contributed. In this case, the adjusted basis of the property is $30,000, so the partner's basis in the partnership interest is also $30,000.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - The difference between the fair market value and the adjusted basis ($20,000) represents the built-in gain, which is not used to determine the partner's initial basis in the partnership interest. The partner's basis is based on the adjusted basis of the contributed property.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - A partner contributing property to a partnership in exchange for a partnership interest always receives a basis in the partnership interest. The basis is equal to the adjusted basis of the contributed property, which in this case is $30,000, not $0.

questions.educational: The general rule for partnership formation is that neither the partner nor the partnership recognizes gain or loss when property is contributed in exchange for a partnership interest (IRC §721). The partner's basis in the partnership interest is determined by the adjusted basis of the contributed property (IRC §722), ensuring that the built-in gain or loss is preserved for future taxation when the partnership disposes of the property or the partner disposes of the partnership interest. This aligns with the concept of pass-through taxation, where the character of income and losses is maintained as it flows through the partnership to the partners.
questions.examTip: Remember that the partner's basis in the partnership interest is generally the adjusted basis of the contributed property, but be aware of exceptions such as when the contribution is treated as a sale or exchange, or when the partnership assumes liabilities of the partner.
questions.memoryAid: Basis In = Basis Out (Partner's basis *in* the partnership = Adjusted basis of property contributed *out* of the partner's hands).

questions.question: When a partner contributes property with a fair market value of $80,000 and an adjusted basis of $45,000 to a partnership, what is the partnership's basis in the property?
questions.explanation: Under IRC §723, the partnership takes a carryover basis in contributed property equal to the contributing partner's adjusted basis ($45,000). The partnership does not step up the basis to fair market value.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - The partnership does not take the fair market value as its basis in contributed property. Under IRC §723, the partnership's basis is generally the same as the contributing partner's adjusted basis.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - IRC §723 states that the partnership's basis in property contributed by a partner is the adjusted basis of the property to the contributing partner at the time of the contribution. In this case, the partner's adjusted basis is $45,000, so the partnership's basis is also $45,000.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - $35,000 represents the built-in gain (fair market value less adjusted basis) of the contributed property. While built-in gain is important for other partnership tax calculations, it is not the partnership's basis in the property.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - $62,500 is an arbitrary number and does not represent any relevant calculation related to the partnership's basis in contributed property. The basis is determined solely by the partner's adjusted basis at the time of contribution.

questions.educational: The carryover basis rule for partnership contributions (IRC §723) reflects the aggregate theory of partnerships, where the partnership is seen as an extension of its partners. This ensures that the built-in gain or loss at the time of contribution is preserved and taxed to the contributing partner when the partnership eventually disposes of the property. Understanding basis rules is crucial for calculating partnership income, partner distributions, and eventual liquidation.
questions.examTip: Remember that the partnership generally takes a carryover basis from the contributing partner. Be alert for scenarios involving liabilities assumed by the partnership, as this can affect both the partner's basis in their partnership interest and potentially the partnership's basis in the contributed property.
questions.memoryAid: Partnership Basis: 'Carryover, not step-up!' (unless liabilities shift)

questions.question: A partner receives a 25% partnership interest valued at $40,000 solely in exchange for services rendered. What are the tax consequences to the partner?
options: No taxable income; the interest has a zero basis
$40,000 of ordinary income and a $40,000 basis in the partnership interest
$40,000 of capital gain and a $40,000 basis
$40,000 of ordinary income and a zero basis
questions.explanation: Under IRC §721, the nonrecognition rule does not apply when a partnership interest is received in exchange for services. The partner recognizes ordinary income equal to the fair market value of the interest received ($40,000) and takes a basis equal to that amount.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - IRC §721(a) provides for nonrecognition of gain or loss when property is contributed to a partnership in exchange for a partnership interest. However, this nonrecognition rule *does not* apply when a partnership interest is received in exchange for services. The partner recognizes income and takes a basis in the partnership interest.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - When a partnership interest is received in exchange for services, the partner recognizes ordinary income equal to the fair market value of the interest received, per established tax principles. The partner's basis in the partnership interest is equal to the amount of income recognized, which is $40,000 in this case.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - The receipt of a partnership interest in exchange for services results in ordinary income, not capital gain. Capital gain generally arises from the sale or exchange of a capital asset. The basis is correct, but the income type is not.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - While the income type is correctly identified as ordinary, the partner's basis in the partnership interest is *not* zero. The basis is equal to the amount of income recognized, which is $40,000.

questions.educational: The tax treatment of partnership interests received for services is an exception to the general rule of nonrecognition for contributions under IRC §721. This exception ensures that services are taxed as compensation, similar to wages or other forms of payment. Understanding this distinction is crucial for accurately determining a partner's income and basis, which impacts future tax liabilities and distributions.
questions.examTip: Remember that the receipt of a partnership interest for services creates taxable income to the partner, and the amount of that income becomes the partner's initial basis in the partnership interest. This is a common area tested on the SEE exam, so be sure to understand the difference between contributions of property versus services.
questions.memoryAid: Services = Salary = Ordinary Income = Basis

questions.question: Partner A contributes property with a basis of $60,000 and a fair market value of $100,000, subject to a $70,000 mortgage, to a partnership for a 50% interest. What is Partner A's basis in the partnership interest?
questions.explanation: Partner A's basis starts at $60,000 (basis of contributed property). The liability assumed by the partnership reduces A's basis by $70,000 (full amount). A's share of the partnership liability (50% × $70,000 = $35,000) increases basis. Result: $60,000 - $70,000 + $35,000 = $25,000. Basis cannot go below zero.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is CORRECT - When a partner contributes property subject to a liability to a partnership, the partner's basis in the partnership interest is the basis of the contributed property, decreased by the portion of the liability assumed by the other partners, and increased by the partner's share of partnership liabilities. In this case, $60,000 (basis) - $35,000 (liability shifted to other partners, 50% of $70,000) = $25,000.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - This option represents the initial basis of the contributed property before considering the liability assumed by the other partners. The liability reduces the contributing partner's basis, so $60,000 is incorrect.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - A partner's basis in a partnership interest can be reduced by liabilities assumed by other partners, but it cannot go below zero. While the initial calculation might seem to result in a negative number ($60,000 - $70,000 = -$10,000), the partner's share of the partnership liability ($35,000) increases the basis, preventing it from being zero.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - This option represents only the partner's share of the partnership liability (50% of $70,000). It fails to account for the basis of the contributed property before the liability adjustment. The initial basis is a critical component of the calculation.

questions.educational: When a partner contributes property to a partnership, the contribution is generally tax-free under Section 721. However, if the contributed property is subject to a liability, the contributing partner is treated as receiving a distribution to the extent the liability assumed by the other partners exceeds the partner's basis in the contributed property. This rule prevents partners from contributing over-encumbered property to avoid tax.
questions.examTip: Remember that a partner's basis in their partnership interest can never be less than zero. Always consider the impact of liabilities when calculating a partner's basis upon contribution of property.
questions.memoryAid: Basis UP: Partner's share of liabilities. Basis DOWN: Liabilities assumed by other partners.

questions.question: Partner B receives guaranteed payments of $60,000 from the partnership for services. How are these payments treated for tax purposes?
options: As a return of capital, not taxable
As ordinary income to Partner B and deductible by the partnership
As capital gain to Partner B
As a distribution reducing Partner B's basis
questions.explanation: Under IRC §707(c), guaranteed payments are treated as ordinary income to the receiving partner and are deductible by the partnership in computing its ordinary income or loss. They are similar to salary payments and are subject to self-employment tax.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - Guaranteed payments are not a return of capital. They represent compensation for services or the use of capital and are specifically designed to be taxable income to the partner.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - IRC §707(c) explicitly states that guaranteed payments are treated as ordinary income to the receiving partner, similar to a salary. The partnership can deduct these payments as a business expense, reducing its taxable income, provided they meet the ordinary and necessary business expense requirements under IRC §162.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - Guaranteed payments are not capital gains. Capital gains arise from the sale or exchange of capital assets, whereas guaranteed payments are compensation for services or the use of capital, taxed as ordinary income.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - While partnership distributions generally reduce a partner's basis, guaranteed payments are treated separately. They are included in the partner's income and do not automatically reduce the partner's basis. Other distributions, not classified as guaranteed payments, would reduce basis.

questions.educational: Guaranteed payments ensure partners receive compensation for their contributions (services or capital) regardless of the partnership's profitability. This treatment aligns with the broader tax principle that income should be taxed when earned, and expenses should be deductible when incurred. Understanding guaranteed payments is crucial for accurately calculating a partner's taxable income and the partnership's deductible expenses, which directly impacts the K-1 issued to each partner.
questions.examTip: On the EA exam, carefully distinguish between guaranteed payments and ordinary partnership distributions. Guaranteed payments are always ordinary income, while the taxability of ordinary distributions depends on the partner's basis in the partnership.
questions.memoryAid: GPS: Guaranteed Payment = Salary (ordinary income).

questions.question: A partner's beginning basis in a partnership is $40,000. During the year, the partnership allocated $15,000 of ordinary income and $5,000 of tax-exempt interest to the partner. The partner received a cash distribution of $8,000. What is the partner's ending basis?
questions.explanation: Beginning basis: $40,000 + Ordinary income: $15,000 + Tax-exempt interest: $5,000 - Distribution: $8,000 = $52,000. Both taxable and tax-exempt income increase basis. Distributions decrease basis. This ordering ensures basis is not reduced below zero by distributions.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - This answer only adds the ordinary income and subtracts the distribution, neglecting to include the tax-exempt interest which also increases basis. Remember that all income items, whether taxable or tax-exempt, increase a partner's basis.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - This answer incorrectly calculates the basis. It may have added the ordinary income and tax-exempt interest, but then subtracted a smaller distribution amount, or made an addition error. Always double-check your calculations when dealing with basis adjustments.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is CORRECT - A partner's basis is increased by their share of partnership income (both taxable and tax-exempt) and decreased by distributions. Therefore, the calculation is: $40,000 (beginning basis) + $15,000 (ordinary income) + $5,000 (tax-exempt interest) - $8,000 (distribution) = $52,000.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - This answer incorrectly calculates the basis. It may have added both income items but failed to subtract the distribution, or added it instead. Distributions *decrease* a partner's basis.

questions.educational: A partner's basis in a partnership is crucial for determining the tax consequences of distributions and the deductibility of losses. The basis is increased by contributions, the partner's share of partnership income (including tax-exempt income), and increased by the partner's share of any increase in partnership liabilities. It is decreased by distributions, the partner's share of partnership losses (but not below zero), and the partner's share of any decrease in partnership liabilities. This ensures that partners are taxed only on their economic gain from the partnership.
questions.examTip: On the EA exam, carefully consider all items that affect basis, including contributions, income (taxable and tax-exempt), losses, distributions, and changes in liabilities. Remember the ordering rules for basis adjustments, especially regarding distributions and losses.
questions.memoryAid: CILD: Contributions and Income increase basis, Liabilities decrease basis, Distributions decrease basis.

questions.question: Which of the following is an advantage of S corporation status compared to C corporation status?
options: S corporations can have an unlimited number of shareholders
S corporations can issue multiple classes of stock
S corporation income avoids double taxation
S corporations are not subject to estimated tax requirements
questions.explanation: The primary advantage of S corporation status is avoidance of double taxation. S corporation income passes through to shareholders and is taxed only at the individual level. C corporation income is taxed at the corporate level and again when distributed as dividends.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - S corporations are limited in the number of shareholders they can have. While the limit has increased over time, it is not unlimited. C corporations do not have this restriction.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - S corporations are restricted to having only one class of stock. This simplifies the allocation of income and losses among shareholders. C corporations can issue multiple classes of stock with varying rights and preferences.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is CORRECT - A key advantage of S corporations is the avoidance of double taxation. Unlike C corporations, S corporations are pass-through entities, meaning their income, losses, deductions, and credits are passed through to their shareholders' individual income tax returns. This avoids the corporate-level tax imposed on C corporations, followed by a second tax at the shareholder level when dividends are distributed.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - S corporations are generally subject to estimated tax requirements. Shareholders who receive pass-through income from an S corporation may be required to make estimated tax payments to avoid penalties for underpayment of taxes. The S corporation itself may also be subject to certain built-in gains taxes or passive income taxes that require estimated tax payments.

questions.educational: The choice of entity structure significantly impacts a business's tax liability. S corporations offer pass-through taxation, avoiding the double taxation of C corporations, but they have limitations on the number and type of shareholders and stock. Understanding these differences is crucial for advising clients on the most advantageous entity structure for their specific circumstances, a key area tested on the SEE exam.
questions.examTip: When comparing entity types on the EA exam, focus on the tax implications, shareholder restrictions, and administrative burdens associated with each. Remember that the 'best' entity choice depends on the client's specific situation and goals.
questions.memoryAid: S-Corp: Single Tax, Simple Stock.

questions.question: Schedule K-1 (Form 1065) is used to report:
options: The total income of the partnership
Each partner's share of partnership income, deductions, and credits
questions.explanation: Schedule K-1 (Form 1065) reports each partner's distributive share of partnership income, deductions, credits, and other items. Each partner uses this information to report their share on their individual tax return.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - The total income of the partnership is reported on Form 1065, not Schedule K-1. Schedule K-1 details the *allocation* of that income to each partner.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - Schedule K-1 (Form 1065) is specifically designed to report each partner's distributive share of the partnership's income, deductions, credits, and other items. This allows each partner to accurately report their share on their individual income tax return (Form 1040) and pay the appropriate taxes.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - The partnership's estimated tax payments, if any, are not reported on Schedule K-1. Partners are responsible for their own estimated tax payments based on their share of partnership income, and this is calculated separately.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - The partnership's balance sheet is reported on Form 1065, specifically Schedule L, not on Schedule K-1. Schedule K-1 focuses on the allocation of income and other items, not the overall financial position of the partnership.

questions.educational: Partnerships are pass-through entities, meaning the partnership itself doesn't pay income tax. Instead, the partnership's income, deductions, and credits are passed through to the partners, who report them on their individual tax returns. Schedule K-1 is the crucial document that facilitates this pass-through, ensuring each partner reports their correct share of the partnership's activities, which directly impacts their individual tax liability.
questions.examTip: On the EA exam, remember that Schedule K-1 is the key to understanding a partner's individual tax obligations arising from their partnership interest. Pay close attention to the various line items on the K-1, as they can affect different parts of the partner's Form 1040.
questions.memoryAid: K-1: 'K'ey to individual partner's income.

questions.question: A limited partner in a partnership generally:
options: May deduct all partnership losses against any type of income
Is treated as materially participating in the partnership activity
Has all partnership income and losses treated as passive activity
Is exempt from the at-risk rules
questions.explanation: Under IRC §469(h)(2), a limited partner's share of partnership income and losses is generally treated as passive activity. Limited partners are presumed not to materially participate unless they meet one of the narrow exceptions. Passive losses can only offset passive income.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - A limited partner cannot deduct all partnership losses against any type of income. Losses are generally limited to the partner's basis in the partnership and are further subject to the passive activity loss rules under IRC §469 and the at-risk rules under IRC §465.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - A limited partner is generally presumed *not* to materially participate in the partnership's business activities, according to IRC §469(h)(2). Material participation requires regular, continuous, and substantial involvement in the operations of the activity.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is CORRECT - Generally, a limited partner's share of partnership income and losses is treated as passive activity under IRC §469(h)(2). This means the income and losses can only offset passive losses and income, respectively, subject to certain limitations.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - Limited partners are *not* exempt from the at-risk rules under IRC §465. The at-risk rules limit a partner's deductible losses to the amount they have at risk in the partnership, which includes cash contributions and recourse debt.

questions.educational: The passive activity loss rules, under IRC §469, were enacted to prevent taxpayers from using losses from activities in which they don't materially participate to offset income from other sources, such as wages or active business income. This is particularly relevant for limited partners, who typically have a more passive role in the partnership's operations. Understanding these rules is crucial for accurately determining a taxpayer's taxable income and potential tax liability.
questions.examTip: On the EA exam, pay close attention to the taxpayer's level of involvement in an activity. The terms 'material participation,' 'active participation,' and 'passive activity' have specific meanings and different tax consequences.
questions.memoryAid: LP = Lazy Partner = Passive (Generally)

questions.question: The accumulated earnings tax under IRC §531 applies to which type of entity?
options: C corporations that accumulate earnings beyond reasonable business needs
questions.explanation: The accumulated earnings tax is a penalty tax that applies to C corporations formed or used to avoid income tax on shareholders by accumulating earnings and profits beyond the reasonable needs of the business. The tax rate is 20% on accumulated taxable income. S corporations and partnerships are not subject to this tax.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - S corporations are pass-through entities, meaning their income and losses are passed directly to the shareholders. Because the income is taxed at the shareholder level, the accumulated earnings tax does not apply to S corporations.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - Partnerships are also pass-through entities, similar to S corporations. Income and losses are reported on the partners' individual income tax returns, precluding the need for an accumulated earnings tax at the partnership level.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is CORRECT - The accumulated earnings tax, imposed under IRC §531, is specifically designed to prevent C corporations from accumulating earnings beyond reasonable business needs to avoid paying dividends to shareholders, thereby avoiding the double taxation inherent in C corporations (taxed at the corporate level and again at the shareholder level when distributed as dividends). The tax is levied on accumulated taxable income.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - The accumulated earnings tax does not apply to all corporations. It is specifically targeted at C corporations that accumulate earnings beyond reasonable business needs. Simply being a corporation does not automatically trigger the tax; there must be an intent to avoid shareholder-level tax.

questions.educational: The accumulated earnings tax exists to prevent C corporations from being used as tax shelters. By accumulating earnings instead of distributing them as dividends, shareholders could defer or avoid paying taxes on those earnings. The tax is a penalty designed to encourage distributions and ensure that corporate profits are eventually taxed at the shareholder level, a key consideration in entity selection and tax planning.
questions.examTip: When answering questions about the accumulated earnings tax, remember that it's a penalty tax on C corporations, and the IRS will scrutinize accumulations of earnings, especially if the corporation has a history of not paying dividends. Be prepared to advise clients on documenting reasonable business needs to justify accumulations.
questions.memoryAid: AET = Avoid Earnings Tax (by distributing dividends or demonstrating reasonable business needs).

questions.question: Which of the following items must be separately stated on a partnership return rather than included in ordinary business income?
questions.explanation: Items that could affect individual partners differently must be separately stated on the partnership return and Schedule K-1. Capital gains and losses are separately stated because they are subject to different tax rates and netting rules at the individual level. Cost of goods sold and depreciation are included in ordinary business income.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - Cost of goods sold (COGS) is directly related to the partnership's business operations and is used to calculate gross profit, which is a component of ordinary business income. It's not separately stated because it doesn't have a unique impact on individual partners' tax situations.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - Officers' compensation is treated as a deductible business expense that reduces the partnership's ordinary business income. It's not passed through separately to partners because it's already factored into the overall profit or loss allocation.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is CORRECT - Net long-term capital gain must be separately stated because it's subject to preferential tax rates at the partner level. Section 702(a) of the Internal Revenue Code requires that partners separately account for their distributive share of capital gains and losses, as these items retain their character when passed through to the partners and may be subject to different tax treatment depending on the partner's individual circumstances.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - Depreciation expense is generally included in the calculation of ordinary business income. While certain depreciation adjustments (e.g., Section 179 expense) may be separately stated, regular depreciation is not.

questions.educational: Partnerships operate under a pass-through taxation system, meaning the partnership itself doesn't pay income tax. Instead, profits and losses are passed through to the partners, who report them on their individual income tax returns. Certain items that could affect partners differently, such as capital gains, dividends, and charitable contributions, must be separately stated to ensure accurate individual tax calculations. This prevents the partnership from obscuring the true tax impact on each partner.
questions.examTip: Remember that Schedule K-1 is the key document for understanding a partner's share of partnership income, deductions, credits, and other items. Pay close attention to the separately stated items on Schedule K-1, as they directly impact the partner's individual tax liability.
questions.memoryAid: SILO - Separately stated items impact: Specific, Individual, Liability, Outcome.

questions.question: A sole proprietor drove 12,000 business miles during 2025 and uses the standard mileage rate of 70 cents per mile. What is the deductible vehicle expense?
questions.explanation: Using the standard mileage rate for 2025 of 70 cents per mile, the deductible vehicle expense is 12,000 miles × $0.70 = $8,400. The standard mileage rate is an alternative to calculating actual vehicle expenses and includes gas, maintenance, insurance, and depreciation.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - This option is incorrect because it uses a mileage rate of $0.65 per mile instead of the correct 2025 standard mileage rate of $0.70 per mile. 12,000 miles x $0.65 = $7,800.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - The standard mileage rate for business use in 2025 is $0.70 per mile. The deductible vehicle expense is calculated by multiplying the business miles driven by the standard mileage rate: 12,000 miles * $0.70/mile = $8,400. This is a direct application of the IRS's prescribed method for calculating deductible vehicle expenses.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - This option is incorrect as it doesn't represent the correct calculation using the standard mileage rate. There's no readily apparent calculation that arrives at $6,750 using the given information and the standard mileage rate.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - This option is incorrect because it uses a mileage rate of $0.60 per mile instead of the correct 2025 standard mileage rate of $0.70 per mile. 12,000 miles x $0.60 = $7,200.

questions.educational: The standard mileage rate is a simplified method for calculating deductible vehicle expenses, covering costs like gas, oil, insurance, and depreciation. It's an alternative to tracking actual expenses, offering ease of record-keeping. Choosing between the standard mileage rate and actual expenses often depends on which method yields a larger deduction, impacting taxable income and ultimately, the tax liability of the business. This is a key consideration in tax planning.
questions.examTip: On the EA exam, pay close attention to the tax year specified in the question, as the standard mileage rate can change annually. The IRS publishes these rates, and using the incorrect rate will lead to a wrong answer.
questions.memoryAid: Remember: 'Miles Matter Most' – when calculating vehicle expenses, accurately record the business miles driven and use the correct standard mileage rate for the tax year.

questions.question: An S corporation with no accumulated earnings and profits distributes $30,000 to a shareholder whose stock basis is $20,000. How is the distribution treated?
options: $20,000 tax-free return of capital and $10,000 capital gain
questions.explanation: For an S corporation with no accumulated earnings and profits, distributions are first treated as a tax-free return of capital to the extent of the shareholder's stock basis ($20,000). Any excess ($10,000) is treated as capital gain from the sale or exchange of property.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - S corporations without accumulated earnings and profits (E&P) do not generate ordinary dividend income from distributions to the extent of the shareholder's basis. Distributions are treated as a return of capital first.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - Distributions from an S corporation with no accumulated E&P are treated as a tax-free return of capital up to the shareholder's stock basis. Any distribution exceeding the basis is treated as a capital gain, as if the shareholder sold the stock.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - While the first $20,000 is a tax-free return of capital, the entire $30,000 cannot be treated as such because the shareholder's stock basis is only $20,000. The excess $10,000 is treated differently.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - While the first $20,000 is tax-free, the excess $10,000 is not treated as ordinary income. It is treated as a capital gain, similar to the sale of stock.

questions.educational: S corporations are pass-through entities, meaning income and losses are passed through to the shareholders. Distributions are generally tax-free to the extent of the shareholder's basis. This treatment avoids double taxation, which is a key difference between S corporations and C corporations, and is a crucial concept for the SEE exam.
questions.examTip: On the EA exam, carefully note whether an S corporation has accumulated E&P, as this significantly changes the tax treatment of distributions. If E&P exists, distributions are taxed as dividends to the extent of E&P before being treated as a return of capital.
questions.memoryAid: S Corp Distribution Hierarchy (No E&P): 1. Return of Capital (Tax-Free, up to basis), 2. Capital Gain.

questions.question: When a partner contributes a capital asset held for more than one year to a partnership, the partner's holding period for the partnership interest:
options: Begins on the date of contribution
Includes the holding period of the contributed property
Is always long-term regardless of the contributed property
Begins on the first day of the partnership's tax year
questions.explanation: Under IRC §1223(1), when property is contributed to a partnership in a tax-free exchange under IRC §721, the partner's holding period for the partnership interest includes the holding period of the contributed property (tacking). This applies when the contributed property was a capital asset or Section 1231 property.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - The partner's holding period doesn't simply begin on the date of contribution. IRC §1223(1) allows for the holding period of the contributed property to be included in the holding period of the partnership interest, assuming certain conditions are met.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - IRC §1223(1) explicitly states that if a partner contributes a capital asset or Section 1231 property to a partnership in a non-taxable exchange under IRC §721, the partner's holding period for the partnership interest includes the holding period of the contributed property. This is known as 'tacking' the holding period.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - The holding period is not *always* long-term. The holding period of the partnership interest depends on the holding period of the contributed property. If the contributed property was held for one year or less, the partnership interest's holding period will also be short-term initially.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - The holding period is determined by the holding period of the contributed property, not by the partnership's tax year. The partnership's tax year is relevant for other aspects of partnership taxation, but not for determining the partner's holding period in this specific scenario.

questions.educational: The 'tacking' rule in partnership taxation allows partners to include the holding period of contributed capital assets or Section 1231 property when determining the holding period of their partnership interest. This rule prevents taxpayers from artificially creating short-term gains or losses by contributing property to a partnership and then immediately selling their partnership interest. Understanding holding periods is crucial for determining whether gains or losses are treated as short-term or long-term, which impacts the applicable tax rates.
questions.examTip: Remember the 'tacking' rule for partnership interests when a partner contributes capital assets or Section 1231 property. This is a common area tested on the EA exam, especially in scenarios involving the sale of a partnership interest shortly after formation.
questions.memoryAid: Contributed Capital = Continued Holding Period (Tacking)

questions.question: An S corporation shareholder-employee takes a salary of only $10,000 annually while the business generates $200,000 in net income. What is the primary IRS concern?
options: The salary may be too high, causing excessive deductions
The salary may be unreasonably low to avoid employment taxes
The S corporation election is invalid with such low compensation
The excess income must be reclassified as passive income
questions.explanation: The IRS scrutinizes S corporation shareholder-employees who pay themselves unreasonably low salaries to avoid FICA/Medicare taxes. The IRS can reclassify distributions as wages subject to employment taxes if compensation is not reasonable for the services provided. Reasonable compensation should reflect what would be paid to a non-owner for similar services.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - The IRS concern is not that the salary is too high. With $200,000 in net income, a $10,000 salary is likely far too low, not too high, for a shareholder-employee actively involved in the business.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - The primary IRS concern is that the shareholder-employee is attempting to avoid employment taxes (FICA and Medicare) by taking a salary that is unreasonably low relative to the services they provide to the S corporation. The IRS can reclassify distributions as wages subject to these taxes under IRC Section 3121(d).
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - While unreasonably low compensation can raise red flags, it doesn't automatically invalidate the S corporation election. The IRS's remedy is typically to reclassify distributions as wages, not to revoke the S election.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - The issue is not about reclassifying excess income as passive income. The concern is that the shareholder-employee is receiving distributions that should be classified as wages subject to employment taxes, not that the business income itself is being mischaracterized.

questions.educational: S corporations allow profits to pass through to shareholders, avoiding corporate-level income tax. However, shareholder-employees must receive reasonable compensation for services rendered to the corporation. This rule prevents shareholders from avoiding employment taxes by taking distributions instead of wages, which are subject to FICA and Medicare taxes. Understanding reasonable compensation is crucial for S corporation compliance and is tested throughout the SEE exam.
questions.examTip: On the EA exam, always consider the 'reasonable compensation' issue when you see an S corporation shareholder-employee receiving a very low salary relative to the company's profits. The IRS will likely reclassify distributions as wages.
questions.memoryAid: S-Corp Low Salary = IRS FICA Worry

questions.question: A partner's share of partnership losses exceeds their basis in the partnership. The excess losses are:
options: Lost permanently and cannot be deducted
Deductible against the partner's other income
Suspended and carried forward until the partner has sufficient basis
questions.explanation: Under IRC §704(d), a partner's share of partnership losses is deductible only to the extent of the partner's adjusted basis in the partnership interest at the end of the partnership year. Any excess loss is suspended and carried forward indefinitely until the partner has sufficient basis to absorb the loss.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - Partnership losses exceeding basis are not permanently lost. IRC §704(d) specifically allows for the carryforward of these losses, meaning they can be used in future years when the partner has sufficient basis.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - While partners generally report their share of partnership income and losses on their individual tax returns, losses exceeding basis cannot be immediately deducted against other income. They are suspended until basis is restored.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is CORRECT - IRC §704(d) dictates that partnership losses exceeding a partner's basis are suspended and carried forward indefinitely. These losses can be deducted in future years when the partner's basis increases, either through contributions, partnership income, or other means.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - Partnership losses are generally treated as ordinary losses, not capital losses, unless they arise from the sale of a capital asset by the partnership. The limitation on deductibility due to insufficient basis does not convert an ordinary loss into a capital loss.

questions.educational: The limitation on deducting partnership losses to the extent of a partner's basis is designed to prevent partners from deducting losses exceeding their economic investment in the partnership. This rule ensures that partners are only able to deduct losses up to the amount they have at risk. Understanding basis is crucial for the EA exam, as it impacts not only loss deductibility but also the taxability of distributions and the calculation of gain or loss on the sale of a partnership interest.
questions.examTip: Remember that increases to a partner's basis can come from various sources, including contributions, the partner's share of partnership income, and the partner's share of partnership liabilities. Be prepared to calculate a partner's basis and determine the deductibility of losses on the exam.
questions.memoryAid: Basis Before Benefit: Losses exceeding basis are suspended until basis is restored.

questions.question: A personal holding company (PHC) is subject to an additional tax at what rate on undistributed PHC income?
questions.explanation: A personal holding company is subject to a 20% tax on its undistributed personal holding company income under IRC §541. A corporation is a PHC if (1) at least 60% of its adjusted ordinary gross income is PHC income (passive investment income) and (2) more than 50% of its stock is owned by 5 or fewer individuals.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - The personal holding company tax rate is not 10%. This rate is not associated with any specific tax penalty related to corporate income or retained earnings.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - The personal holding company tax rate is not 15%. While this rate might be relevant in other tax contexts, it does not apply to undistributed PHC income.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is CORRECT - IRC §541 imposes a tax of 20% on the undistributed personal holding company income (UPHC income) of a personal holding company (PHC). This tax is in addition to the regular corporate income tax.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - The personal holding company tax rate is not 35%. While 35% was a previous top individual income tax rate, it is not the current rate for PHC income.

questions.educational: The personal holding company (PHC) tax exists to prevent individuals from using corporations to shelter passive income from higher individual tax rates. By accumulating earnings instead of distributing them as dividends, shareholders could potentially defer or avoid taxes. The PHC tax discourages this practice by imposing a penalty on undistributed earnings, forcing distribution and taxation at the shareholder level.
questions.examTip: Remember the two prongs of the PHC test: the income test (60% of adjusted ordinary gross income is PHC income) and the stock ownership test (more than 50% owned by 5 or fewer individuals). Both must be met for the PHC tax to apply, and this is a common area tested on the SEE exam.
questions.memoryAid: PHC = Passive Haven Corporation (20% tax on undistributed income).

questions.question: Which of the following expenses is deductible on Schedule C by a sole proprietor?
questions.explanation: Business liability insurance premiums are an ordinary and necessary business expense deductible on Schedule C. Personal life insurance, federal income taxes, and personal clothing are not deductible business expenses.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - Personal life insurance premiums are generally not deductible unless the business is the beneficiary and the premiums are treated as compensation to the employee (sole proprietor in this case). Since the question doesn't state this condition, it's assumed to be a non-deductible personal expense.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - Federal income taxes, including income tax, self-employment tax, and payroll taxes on the sole proprietor's own income, are not deductible on Schedule C. These taxes are considered personal expenses. Self-employment tax is partially deductible on Schedule 1 (Form 1040).
whyWrong.2: Why option C is CORRECT - Business liability insurance premiums are an ordinary and necessary expense directly related to the sole proprietor's business. As such, they are fully deductible on Schedule C (Profit or Loss From Business) as a business expense under IRC Section 162.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - Personal clothing purchases are generally not deductible unless the clothing is required for the job, is not suitable for everyday wear, and is a condition of employment. Ordinary street clothing, even if worn exclusively at work, is not deductible.

questions.educational: Schedule C deductions are governed by the principle of 'ordinary and necessary' business expenses. This means the expense must be common and accepted in the taxpayer's trade or business and helpful and appropriate for the business. Understanding the distinction between personal and business expenses is crucial for accurate tax reporting and minimizing tax liabilities for self-employed individuals, a key area of focus for Enrolled Agents.
questions.examTip: When analyzing Schedule C deductions, always consider whether the expense directly benefits the business and if it has a personal element. If there's a mixed-use component, allocate the expense accordingly and only deduct the business portion.
questions.memoryAid: OIL: Ordinary, Insurance, Liability - remember these are common Schedule C deductions.

questions.question: Which type of business entity may file a consolidated tax return?
options: An S corporation and its subsidiaries
An affiliated group of C corporations
A partnership and its corporate partners
questions.explanation: Under IRC §1501, an affiliated group of C corporations connected through stock ownership may elect to file a consolidated tax return. An affiliated group requires a common parent that directly owns at least 80% of the voting power and value of at least one includible corporation. S corporations, partnerships, and sole proprietorships cannot file consolidated returns.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - S corporations are pass-through entities and generally cannot be included in a consolidated return. While an S corporation can own subsidiaries, the S corporation itself is not eligible to be part of a consolidated group under IRC §1504.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - An affiliated group of C corporations, as defined under IRC §1504, has the option to file a consolidated tax return. This allows the group to offset losses of one member against the profits of another, potentially reducing the overall tax liability. The key requirement is 80% ownership of each member by the common parent.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - Partnerships are pass-through entities and do not pay income tax at the entity level. Therefore, a partnership cannot be included in a consolidated return with its corporate partners. The partnership's income and deductions flow through to the partners, who report them on their individual or corporate tax returns.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - Sole proprietorships are not separate legal entities from their owners. The income and expenses of a sole proprietorship are reported on Schedule C of the owner's individual income tax return (Form 1040). Therefore, related sole proprietorships cannot file a consolidated tax return.

questions.educational: Consolidated returns are designed to treat a group of affiliated C corporations as a single economic unit for tax purposes. This simplifies tax compliance and can result in tax savings by allowing losses in one corporation to offset profits in another. Understanding consolidated return rules is crucial for the SEE exam, as it involves complex calculations and eligibility requirements under IRC §1501-§1505.
questions.examTip: Remember that the 80% ownership test for affiliated groups applies to both voting power and value of the stock. A failure to meet either test disqualifies the group from filing a consolidated return, a common trick on the EA exam.
questions.memoryAid: C-CORP CONTROL = CONSOLIDATED (C corporations with sufficient control can consolidate)

questions.question: A partnership without a natural business year wishes to use a fiscal year ending September 30. Under Section 444, what is required?
options: No additional requirements; any fiscal year may be used
The partnership must make a required payment under IRC §7519
All partners must individually elect the same fiscal year
The partnership must receive IRS approval through a private letter ruling
questions.explanation: Under Section 444, a partnership may elect a fiscal year with a deferral period of no more than 3 months. However, the partnership must make a required payment under IRC §7519 to approximate the tax deferral benefit. This payment ensures partners do not obtain significant tax deferral by using a different year-end.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - Section 444 provides specific rules when a partnership wants to use a fiscal year that doesn't align with its partners' tax years. It doesn't allow for unrestricted fiscal year elections.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - IRC §444 allows a partnership to elect a fiscal year other than what is required under IRC §706, but IRC §7519 mandates a 'required payment' designed to offset the tax deferral benefit partners receive from the partnership's fiscal year. This payment effectively approximates the tax the partners would have paid had the partnership used a required tax year.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - While partner tax years are relevant in determining the partnership's required tax year under IRC §706, Section 444 doesn't require individual partners to elect the same fiscal year. The partnership makes the election, not the individual partners.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - Section 444 provides a statutory election process, not a discretionary approval process requiring a private letter ruling from the IRS. Partnerships meeting the criteria can elect a fiscal year by following the procedures outlined in the regulations.

questions.educational: Section 444 exists to prevent undue tax deferral benefits for partners when a partnership uses a fiscal year different from its partners' tax years. Without this provision, partners could significantly delay paying taxes on partnership income. This ties into the broader concept of tax year conformity and the IRS's efforts to ensure timely tax collection.
questions.examTip: Remember that Section 444 elections are made at the partnership level, and the required payment under Section 7519 is calculated based on the partnership's net base year income and the applicable deferral ratio. Be prepared to identify situations where a Section 444 election is permissible and the consequences of making that election.
questions.memoryAid: 444 = Fiscal Year Flexibility (but with a fee!). 7519 = The fee you pay (Required Payment).

questions.question: How are nonrecourse liabilities of a partnership allocated among partners?
options: Based on each partner's share of partnership profits
Equally among all partners regardless of profit-sharing ratios
questions.explanation: Under Treas. Reg. §1.752-3, nonrecourse liabilities are generally allocated among partners based on their share of partnership profits. This is because no partner bears the economic risk of loss for nonrecourse debt. The allocation increases each partner's basis accordingly.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - Nonrecourse liabilities are not allocated entirely to the general partner. While general partners may have more management responsibilities, the allocation of nonrecourse liabilities is tied to profit sharing, not solely to their general partner status. Treas. Reg. §1.752-3 governs the allocation.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - Nonrecourse liabilities are allocated among partners based on their share of partnership profits. This is because no partner bears the economic risk of loss if the debt is not repaid. Treas. Reg. §1.752-3 outlines a three-tiered allocation approach, with the third tier being based on profit sharing ratios.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - Nonrecourse liabilities are not allocated *only* to limited partners. The allocation is based on profit-sharing ratios, which apply to all partners, regardless of their status as general or limited partners. The allocation rules under IRC Section 752 apply to all partners.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - Nonrecourse liabilities are not allocated equally among all partners regardless of profit-sharing ratios. The allocation is directly tied to the partnership agreement's provisions for profit sharing, ensuring that partners with a larger stake in profits also bear a larger share of the nonrecourse debt. An equal allocation would disregard the economic realities of the partnership agreement.

questions.educational: The allocation of partnership liabilities, including nonrecourse debt, is crucial for determining a partner's basis in their partnership interest. This basis is important because it limits the amount of losses a partner can deduct and affects the taxability of distributions. Understanding IRC Section 752 and its related regulations is essential for accurately calculating a partner's basis and ensuring compliance with tax laws.
questions.examTip: Remember the three-tiered approach to allocating partnership liabilities under IRC Section 752: first to reflect each partner's share of partnership minimum gain, second to account for any IRC Section 704(c) built-in gain, and third according to the partners' share of partnership profits. This is a frequently tested area on the SEE exam.
questions.memoryAid: Think 'PNP' - Partnership liabilities are allocated based on Partnership minimum gain, 704(c) gain, and then Profits.

questions.question: Which type of entity allows more-than-2% shareholder-employees to deduct health insurance premiums as an above-the-line deduction rather than receiving them tax-free?
questions.explanation: More-than-2% S corporation shareholder-employees cannot receive tax-free employer-provided health insurance. Instead, the premiums are included in the shareholder's W-2 wages and the shareholder takes an above-the-line deduction for self-employed health insurance. C corporation shareholder-employees can receive tax-free health insurance.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - C corporations can provide health insurance to shareholder-employees as a tax-free fringe benefit. The premiums are deductible by the corporation and are not included in the shareholder-employee's income, which is the opposite of what the question asks.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - More-than-2% S corporation shareholder-employees are treated as partners for fringe benefit purposes. This means they cannot receive tax-free employer-provided health insurance. Instead, the S corporation includes the health insurance premiums in the shareholder's W-2 wages, and the shareholder then deducts the premiums as an above-the-line deduction for self-employed health insurance (subject to certain limitations).
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - In a general partnership, partners are considered self-employed. They pay for their own health insurance and deduct the premiums as an above-the-line deduction for self-employed health insurance. The health insurance is not included in their income as wages, so this doesn't fit the scenario described in the question.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - A sole proprietor is also considered self-employed. Like partners, they pay for their own health insurance and deduct the premiums as an above-the-line deduction for self-employed health insurance. The health insurance is not included in their income as wages, so this doesn't fit the scenario described in the question.

questions.educational: The tax treatment of fringe benefits, like health insurance, differs significantly between entity types. C corporations generally offer the most favorable tax treatment for fringe benefits to shareholder-employees. S corporations, on the other hand, treat more-than-2% shareholders more like partners for these purposes, leading to different reporting and deduction rules. Understanding these distinctions is crucial for advising clients on the optimal entity structure for their business.
questions.examTip: On the EA exam, pay close attention to the ownership percentage in S corporations when dealing with fringe benefits. The 'more-than-2%' rule triggers special treatment for health insurance and other benefits.
questions.memoryAid: S-Corp: 'Shareholder-employee health insurance is Separate' – meaning it's treated differently than in a C-Corp.

questions.question: A partner with a basis of $25,000 receives a current cash distribution of $20,000 from the partnership. What is the tax result?
options: The partner recognizes $20,000 of ordinary income
The partner has no taxable gain; basis is reduced to $5,000
The partner recognizes $20,000 of capital gain
questions.explanation: Under IRC §731(a), a partner generally does not recognize gain on a current (non-liquidating) distribution unless the cash distributed exceeds the partner's basis. Here, $20,000 does not exceed the $25,000 basis, so no gain is recognized. The partner's basis is reduced from $25,000 to $5,000.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - A partner recognizes ordinary income from a partnership distribution only when it represents a guaranteed payment for services or the distribution of unrealized receivables or substantially appreciated inventory (hot assets) under IRC §751. This scenario involves a simple cash distribution, not a guaranteed payment or hot assets.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - Under IRC §731(a)(1), a partner recognizes gain from a current distribution only to the extent that the cash distributed exceeds the partner's adjusted basis in the partnership interest immediately before the distribution. Since the $20,000 cash distribution is less than the partner's $25,000 basis, no gain is recognized, and the partner's basis is reduced to $5,000 ($25,000 - $20,000).
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - Capital gain is recognized from a partnership distribution only when the cash distributed exceeds the partner's basis in their partnership interest. While a distribution exceeding basis would result in capital gain, in this case, the cash distribution is less than the partner's basis, so no gain is recognized.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - The partnership does not recognize a loss on a current distribution to a partner. Loss recognition is generally only permitted in a liquidating distribution where the partner receives only cash, unrealized receivables, and inventory, and the basis of those assets is less than the partner's basis in the partnership interest (IRC §731(a)(2)).

questions.educational: Partnership taxation operates under a pass-through system, meaning the partnership itself generally doesn't pay income tax. Distributions to partners are generally tax-free to the extent of their basis, preventing double taxation. The rules surrounding partnership distributions are designed to maintain the partners' capital accounts and prevent distortions in future tax liabilities, reflecting the economic reality of the partnership's operations and the partners' investments.
questions.examTip: Remember that cash distributions reduce a partner's basis, but a distribution of property other than cash first reduces the basis by the adjusted basis of the property to the partnership, not its fair market value. This is a common trick on the EA exam.
questions.memoryAid: CASH OUT, BASIS DOWN (to extent of cash). If cash exceeds basis, then GAIN!

questions.question: Which of the following is an eligible shareholder of an S corporation?
questions.explanation: Tax-exempt organizations described in IRC §501(c)(3) are eligible S corporation shareholders under IRC §1361(b)(1)(B). However, income allocated to such organizations may be treated as unrelated business taxable income (UBTI). C corporations, partnerships, and nonresident aliens are not eligible shareholders.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - C corporations are specifically prohibited from being S corporation shareholders. Allowing C corporations as shareholders would complicate the pass-through taxation rules of S corporations and potentially allow for tax avoidance.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - Tax-exempt organizations described in IRC §501(c)(3) are permitted to be S corporation shareholders under IRC §1361(c)(6). While the income allocated to the tax-exempt organization is generally UBTI (Unrelated Business Taxable Income), this does not disqualify them as shareholders.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - Partnerships are not eligible S corporation shareholders. The S corporation rules aim for simplicity in ownership structure, and allowing partnerships as shareholders would introduce complexities in tracking income and losses.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - Nonresident aliens are not permitted to be S corporation shareholders under IRC §1361(b)(1)(C). This restriction is in place to prevent potential complications with international tax treaties and enforcement.

questions.educational: S corporations are designed to allow small businesses to be taxed at the individual level, avoiding double taxation. The eligibility rules for S corporation shareholders are strict to maintain the integrity of this pass-through taxation system. Understanding these restrictions is crucial for advising clients on the optimal business structure and ensuring compliance with IRS regulations.
questions.examTip: Remember the acronym 'ES BIRD' - Eligible S-Corp shareholders can be Beneficiaries, Individuals, Resident estates, and certain types of trusts and organizations, but not C-corps, partnerships, or nonresident aliens.
questions.memoryAid: No C-P-N for S-Corps: No C corporations, Partnerships, or Non-resident aliens.

questions.question: A sole proprietor with average annual gross receipts of $20 million or less may use which accounting method?
options: Either the cash or accrual method
questions.explanation: Under IRC §448(c), as amended by the Tax Cuts and Jobs Act, taxpayers with average annual gross receipts of $29 million or less (adjusted for inflation, $30 million for 2024/2025) for the prior 3-year period may use the cash method of accounting. Sole proprietors meeting this threshold can choose either cash or accrual.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - While the accrual method is permissible, it's not the *only* option. Sole proprietors meeting the gross receipts test can also use the cash method.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - IRC §448(c) allows taxpayers, including sole proprietors, with average annual gross receipts below a certain threshold ($30 million for 2024/2025) to use either the cash or accrual method of accounting. This provides flexibility for smaller businesses.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - The cash method is allowed, but it's not the *only* permissible method. The accrual method is also an option for qualifying sole proprietors.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - The installment method is a method of reporting gains from the sale of property where at least one payment is received after the year of sale. It's unrelated to the general accounting method election under IRC §448(c).

questions.educational: The ability to use the cash method simplifies accounting for smaller businesses as income is recognized when received and expenses when paid. This contrasts with the accrual method, which recognizes income when earned and expenses when incurred, regardless of cash flow. Understanding the gross receipts test and permissible accounting methods is crucial for advising sole proprietors on tax compliance and planning.
questions.examTip: Remember the gross receipts test threshold for the cash method election is adjusted annually for inflation; check the latest IRS guidance for the applicable amount when preparing for the EA exam.
questions.memoryAid: CASH is KING (or at least an option) for small businesses.

questions.question: Three individuals transfer property to a newly formed corporation. Together they receive 90% of the corporation's stock. One individual also receives $10,000 cash. Which statement is correct regarding IRC §351?
options: The transaction does not qualify because cash was received
The transaction qualifies; the individual receiving cash recognizes gain up to $10,000
All three individuals must recognize gain on their transfers
The transaction qualifies only if each individual transfers identical property
questions.explanation: Under IRC §351, no gain or loss is recognized when property is transferred to a corporation solely in exchange for stock, if the transferors control (80%+) the corporation immediately after. If boot (cash or other property) is also received, gain is recognized to the extent of boot received, but the overall transaction still qualifies.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - The receipt of cash (boot) does not automatically disqualify a transaction under IRC §351. While it triggers gain recognition up to the amount of the boot, the overall transaction can still qualify for nonrecognition of gain on the stock received.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - IRC §351 allows for the tax-free transfer of property to a corporation in exchange for stock if the transferors control the corporation immediately after the exchange. When boot (like cash) is received in addition to stock, the transferor recognizes gain, but only to the extent of the boot received. In this case, the individual recognizes a gain of $10,000.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - Only the individual who received the cash (boot) recognizes gain. The other two individuals, who only received stock, do not recognize any gain, assuming their contributed property's basis is less than the fair market value of the stock they received.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - IRC §351 does not require that each individual transfer identical property. The type of property transferred is irrelevant as long as property is transferred and the control requirement (80%+) is met.

questions.educational: IRC §351 facilitates business formation by allowing individuals to transfer property to a corporation without immediate tax consequences, encouraging investment and economic growth. The control requirement (80%+) ensures that the transferors maintain a significant ownership stake in the corporation. Boot is taxed because it represents a partial liquidation of the investment.
questions.examTip: Remember that the receipt of boot in a §351 transaction only triggers gain recognition to the extent of the boot received; it does not necessarily disqualify the entire transaction. Pay close attention to the definition of 'property' under §351, which is very broad.
questions.memoryAid: §351: Stock for Property, Control, Boot is Gain.

questions.question: A C corporation with $50,000 of current earnings and profits (E&P) and $30,000 of accumulated E&P distributes $90,000 to its sole shareholder who has a stock basis of $15,000. How is the distribution characterized?
options: $80,000 dividend, $5,000 return of capital, $5,000 capital gain
questions.explanation: Corporate distributions are dividends to the extent of current E&P ($50,000) plus accumulated E&P ($30,000) = $80,000 total dividend. The remaining $10,000 exceeds the shareholder's $15,000 stock basis... wait, $10,000 does not exceed basis. Actually: $90,000 - $80,000 E&P = $10,000 excess. This $10,000 reduces basis from $15,000 to $5,000 (return of capital). No capital gain since basis is not fully reduced.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - This option incorrectly assumes the entire distribution is a dividend. Distributions are dividends only to the extent of current and accumulated E&P.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - This option correctly calculates the dividend portion but incorrectly characterizes the remaining distribution as a return of capital. The remaining distribution is first applied against the shareholder's stock basis, and only the amount exceeding the basis is treated as a capital gain.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is CORRECT - Corporate distributions are treated as dividends to the extent of current and accumulated E&P. Here, the total E&P is $80,000 ($50,000 current + $30,000 accumulated), making $80,000 a dividend. The remaining $10,000 ($90,000 distribution - $80,000 E&P) exceeds the shareholder's basis of $15,000, resulting in a $10,000 capital gain.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - This option incorrectly assumes that the distribution exceeding E&P is split between return of capital and capital gain without first considering the shareholder's stock basis. The excess distribution first reduces basis to zero before any capital gain is recognized.

questions.educational: Corporate distributions follow a specific order: first, they are treated as dividends to the extent of current and accumulated earnings and profits (E&P). Then, any remaining distribution reduces the shareholder's stock basis (return of capital). Finally, any distribution exceeding the shareholder's basis is treated as a capital gain. Understanding this hierarchy is crucial for accurately determining the tax consequences of corporate distributions, a frequent topic on the SEE exam.
questions.examTip: On the EA exam, always calculate current and accumulated E&P before determining the character of a corporate distribution. Remember the order: dividend (to extent of E&P), return of capital (to extent of basis), then capital gain.
questions.memoryAid: Use the acronym 'DERC' to remember the order of distribution treatment: Dividend, Excess reduces basis (Return of capital), Capital gain.

questions.question: A C corporation owns 25% of the stock of another domestic C corporation and receives $100,000 in dividends. What is the dividends received deduction (DRD)?
questions.explanation: A corporation that owns 20% or more (but less than 80%) of the distributing corporation's stock is entitled to a 65% dividends received deduction. Thus, the DRD is $100,000 × 65% = $65,000. Ownership below 20% qualifies for 50%, and 80%+ ownership qualifies for 100%.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - This option represents a 50% dividends received deduction (DRD), which applies when the recipient corporation owns less than 20% of the distributing corporation's stock. In this case, the recipient owns 25%, making 50% incorrect.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - A corporation owning between 20% and 80% of another domestic corporation is entitled to a 65% dividends received deduction (DRD) under IRC Section 243. Since the corporation owns 25% of the distributing corporation, the DRD is $100,000 * 65% = $65,000.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - This option suggests a 100% dividends received deduction (DRD). A 100% DRD is only applicable when the recipient corporation owns 80% or more of the distributing corporation's stock, which is not the case here.
