whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - Form 1120, the U.S. Corporation Income Tax Return for C corporations, is due on the 15th day of the 4th month following the close of the corporation's tax year. For a calendar-year corporation, whose tax year ends December 31st, this falls on April 15th.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - June 15 is not the standard due date for Form 1120 for calendar-year corporations. While it might be relevant for certain estimated tax payments, it doesn't apply to the annual return filing deadline.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - September 15 is not the standard due date for Form 1120 for calendar-year corporations. It may be relevant as a deadline related to extended returns for partnerships and S-Corps, but not for the original due date of a C-Corp return.

questions.educational: The due dates for various business entities are designed to allow the IRS to efficiently process tax returns and collect revenue. C corporations have a later filing deadline than pass-through entities (partnerships and S corporations) because their tax liability is generally more complex and requires more time for preparation. Understanding these deadlines is crucial for compliance and avoiding penalties.
questions.examTip: On the EA exam, pay close attention to the entity type specified in the question. A common trick is to provide information relevant to an S corporation or partnership when the question is about a C corporation, or vice versa. Always double-check!
questions.memoryAid: C-Corps file in April (C-A). S-Corps and Partnerships file in March (S&P-M).

questions.question: When an eligible entity files Form 8832 to change its classification (e.g., from partnership to corporation), the effective date cannot be more than how many days prior to the filing date?
questions.explanation: Under Treas. Reg. §301.7701-3(c)(1)(iii), the effective date of a classification election cannot be more than 75 days prior to the filing date of Form 8832 or more than 12 months after the filing date. If no effective date is specified, the election is effective on the filing date.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - While 30 days might seem like a reasonable timeframe, the regulations specifically allow for a longer retroactive period. The permissible retroactive period is 75 days, not 30.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - 60 days is closer to the correct answer than 30, but it's still incorrect. The IRS allows for a maximum retroactive period of 75 days when electing a change in entity classification using Form 8832.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is CORRECT - Treas. Reg. §301.7701-3(c)(1)(iii) explicitly states that the effective date of an entity classification election on Form 8832 can be up to 75 days prior to the filing date. This allows businesses some flexibility in aligning their tax classification with their operational changes.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - 90 days exceeds the maximum retroactive period allowed by the IRS for entity classification elections. The regulation limits the retroactive effect to 75 days to prevent excessive manipulation of tax liabilities.

questions.educational: The entity classification rules under Treas. Reg. §301.7701 provide businesses with flexibility in choosing their tax structure. The 75-day retroactive election period allows businesses to align their tax classification with operational changes that may have occurred before the formal election is filed, while the 12-month prospective election period allows for planning future tax strategies. Understanding these rules is crucial for advising clients on the optimal entity structure and timing of elections to minimize their tax burden.
questions.examTip: Remember the 75-day and 12-month rules for Form 8832 elections. The EA exam often tests your knowledge of specific timeframes related to tax elections and filings.
questions.memoryAid: Think of '75 days back, 12 months ahead' for Form 8832 effective dates.

questions.question: When a partner's interest in a partnership changes during the year, the partner's share of partnership income may be determined using:
options: The interim closing or proration method, as elected by the partnership
Only the percentage held on December 31
A simple average of beginning and ending percentages
questions.explanation: Under IRC §706(d), when a partner's interest varies during the year (e.g., due to a sale or new partner admission), the partnership may use either the interim closing of the books method (closing the books on the date of change) or the proration method (prorating items daily). The partnership agreement or the partners may agree on which method to use.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - While the closing-of-the-books method is permissible under IRC §706(d), it is not the *only* method allowed. The partnership has the option to use either the interim closing or the proration method.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - IRC §706(d) explicitly allows a partnership to choose between the interim closing of the books method (treating the year as two separate periods) or the proration method (allocating income/loss based on the portion of the year the partner held the interest) when a partner's interest changes. The partnership agreement or the partners may agree on which method to use.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - Using only the percentage held on December 31st ignores any changes in the partner's interest that occurred during the year. This method would not accurately reflect the partner's economic stake in the partnership's income or loss for the entire tax year, violating the principles of proper allocation under partnership tax law.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - A simple average of beginning and ending percentages is not a method sanctioned by the IRS or partnership tax law. While it might seem like a simplified approach, it fails to accurately reflect the time-weighted interest of the partner and is not an acceptable allocation method under IRC §706(d).

questions.educational: The rules governing partnership allocations, particularly when a partner's interest changes, are designed to ensure that income and losses are attributed to partners based on their actual economic stake during the year. This prevents shifting income or losses unfairly and aligns with the conduit nature of partnerships, where income flows through to the partners' individual tax returns. Understanding these rules is crucial for accurate tax reporting and compliance.
questions.examTip: On the EA exam, pay close attention to the dates of any changes in partnership interests. This will help you determine whether the interim closing or proration method is more appropriate and ensure accurate allocation of income and losses to the partners.
questions.memoryAid: CIP: Changes In Partnership interest? Choose between Closing or Proration.

questions.question: Under IRC §1245, when depreciable personal property is sold at a gain, the depreciation previously taken is recaptured as:
options: Capital gain up to the amount of depreciation
Ordinary income up to the amount of depreciation
Passive income subject to the 3.8% NIIT
questions.explanation: Under IRC §1245, gain on the sale of depreciable personal property (Section 1245 property) is recaptured as ordinary income to the extent of depreciation previously taken. Any gain in excess of the depreciation amount is treated as Section 1231 gain (potentially long-term capital gain). This prevents taxpayers from converting ordinary deductions into capital gains.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - While a portion of the gain *could* be capital gain, the recaptured depreciation under §1245 is explicitly treated as ordinary income, not capital gain. This option only addresses the potential for capital gain and ignores the ordinary income component.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - IRC §1245 dictates that when depreciable personal property (Section 1245 property) is sold at a gain, the portion of the gain equal to the depreciation previously taken is recaptured as ordinary income. This prevents taxpayers from using depreciation deductions to offset ordinary income and then selling the asset at a capital gain rate.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - Gains from the sale of depreciable property are generally taxable. Section 1245 specifically addresses how that gain is characterized, not whether it is taxable. Tax-exempt income is a separate category and does not apply here.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - While the sale of a business asset *could* potentially generate passive income in certain circumstances (e.g., if the taxpayer is a passive investor in the business), §1245 specifically addresses the character of the gain as ordinary income due to depreciation recapture, not passive income. The 3.8% NIIT is also not directly related to §1245, but rather to the taxpayer's overall net investment income.

questions.educational: Section 1245 exists to prevent taxpayers from converting ordinary income into capital gains through depreciation deductions. Depreciation is an ordinary deduction that reduces taxable income. When an asset is sold for more than its adjusted basis (original cost less accumulated depreciation), the gain attributable to the depreciation taken is 'recaptured' as ordinary income. This recapture rule ensures that the tax benefit received from depreciation is offset when the asset is sold at a gain, aligning with the broader tax principle of matching income and expenses.
questions.examTip: On the EA exam, carefully distinguish between §1245 and §1250 recapture. §1245 applies to personal property, while §1250 applies to real property, and the recapture rules differ slightly.
questions.memoryAid: 1245: Personal Property = Ordinary Income Recapture (think 'P' for Personal and Ordinary).

questions.question: An S corporation reports each shareholder's share of income, deductions, and credits on:
questions.explanation: An S corporation issues Schedule K-1 (Form 1120-S) to each shareholder showing their pro rata share of the corporation's items of income, deductions, credits, and other tax items. The shareholder uses this information to prepare their individual return. Note: shareholder-employees also receive Form W-2 for their wages.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - Form W-2 is used to report wages paid to employees, including shareholder-employees, but it does not report the shareholder's share of the S corporation's income, deductions, and credits. This form only reflects compensation for services rendered.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - Schedule K-1 (Form 1120-S) is the correct form used by S corporations to report each shareholder's pro rata share of the corporation's income, deductions, credits, and other items. This schedule provides the necessary information for shareholders to accurately report their share of the S corporation's activities on their individual income tax returns (Form 1040).
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - Form 1099-DIV reports dividends paid to shareholders, which are distributions of earnings and profits. While S corporations can make distributions, the shareholder's overall share of the S corporation's income, deductions, and credits is reported on Schedule K-1, not Form 1099-DIV.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - Form 1099-MISC reports payments made to independent contractors for services rendered. This form is not used to report a shareholder's share of an S corporation's income, deductions, and credits. It is used for payments to non-employees.

questions.educational: S corporations are pass-through entities, meaning the corporation's income, deductions, and credits are passed through to the shareholders and reported on their individual income tax returns. This avoids double taxation, as the corporation itself does not pay income tax. Understanding the flow of income and deductions from the S corporation to the shareholder is crucial for accurate tax compliance and planning, a key area for Enrolled Agents.
questions.examTip: On the EA exam, remember that Schedule K-1 (Form 1120-S) is the primary source document for shareholders to determine their share of S corporation items. Pay close attention to the various boxes on the K-1, as each represents a different type of income, deduction, or credit that needs to be reported on the shareholder's individual return.
questions.memoryAid: K-1 = Key Information for shareholders of pass-through entities.

questions.question: When a corporation is thinly capitalized (excess debt relative to equity), the IRS may recharacterize debt as:
options: Equity, resulting in interest payments being treated as nondeductible dividends
Accumulated earnings subject to penalty tax
questions.explanation: When a corporation has an unusually high debt-to-equity ratio (thin capitalization), the IRS may recharacterize the purported debt as equity under IRC §385. The consequence is that interest payments become nondeductible (treated as dividend distributions), and repayments are treated as returns of capital rather than debt repayment. Factors considered include the debt-to-equity ratio, whether the debt is held proportionate to stock ownership, and whether there is a fixed repayment schedule.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - Tax-exempt bonds are a type of debt instrument, but the IRS recharacterizing debt as equity has nothing to do with the tax status of bonds. The issue is the nature of the instrument itself, not its tax treatment if it were truly debt.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - IRC §385 gives the IRS the authority to reclassify debt as equity when a corporation is thinly capitalized. This means that what the corporation treats as deductible interest payments are instead considered nondeductible dividend distributions to shareholders. The repayments of principal are then treated as returns of capital, not debt repayment.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - The accumulated earnings tax is a penalty imposed under IRC §531 on corporations that accumulate earnings beyond the reasonable needs of the business to avoid shareholder-level taxes. While thin capitalization can be a *symptom* of a strategy to avoid shareholder taxes, it doesn't directly trigger the accumulated earnings tax. The IRS would need to prove the intent to avoid shareholder taxes.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - Cancellation of indebtedness (COD) income arises when a debt is forgiven or discharged for less than its face value. Recharacterizing debt as equity doesn't involve the cancellation of any debt; it simply changes the classification of the financial instrument.

questions.educational: Thin capitalization rules exist to prevent corporations from excessively leveraging debt to reduce their tax burden through interest deductions. By reclassifying debt as equity, the IRS ensures that distributions to owners are taxed appropriately (or not deducted at the corporate level), preventing tax avoidance. This is related to the broader concept of substance over form, where the IRS looks at the economic reality of a transaction rather than its legal form.
questions.examTip: On the EA exam, remember that the IRS's main concern with thin capitalization is the potential for corporations to disguise dividend distributions as deductible interest payments. Focus on the tax consequences of reclassification.
questions.memoryAid: Thin Cap = Debt Trap: Too much debt can trap you with reclassification.

questions.question: A small business with average gross receipts of $5 million or less may elect to apply the research credit (IRC §41) against which tax?
options: Against the employer's share of Social Security (payroll) tax
Against the alternative minimum tax only
questions.explanation: Under IRC §41(h), a qualified small business (average gross receipts of $5 million or less in the prior 3 years and no gross receipts for any of the preceding 5 years) may elect to apply up to $500,000 of its research credit against its employer share of Social Security (payroll) tax liability. This benefits startups that may not yet have taxable income.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - While the research credit can generally offset income tax, IRC §41(h) specifically allows qualified small businesses to elect to apply the credit against payroll tax, making this option incomplete.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - IRC §41(h) provides a special election for qualified small businesses (those with average gross receipts of $5 million or less in the prior 3 years and no gross receipts for any of the preceding 5 years) to apply up to $50,000 (indexed for inflation, currently $500,000) of the research credit against the employer's share of Social Security (payroll) tax. This is intended to benefit startups and small businesses that may not yet have significant income tax liability.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - The research credit can offset alternative minimum tax (AMT) in certain situations, but the specific provision for small businesses in IRC §41(h) focuses on the payroll tax liability, not the AMT.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - State income tax is a separate tax system from the federal tax system. The federal research credit under IRC §41 cannot be applied against state income tax liabilities.

questions.educational: The research credit is designed to incentivize businesses to invest in innovation and development. The small business exception allowing the credit to offset payroll taxes is particularly helpful for startups that may not yet be profitable, as it provides immediate cash flow benefits. This provision acknowledges the importance of supporting early-stage companies in fostering economic growth and technological advancement.
questions.examTip: Remember the specific gross receipts test ($5 million or less) and the 'no gross receipts for any of the preceding 5 years' requirement for the payroll tax election of the research credit under IRC §41(h). This is a common area tested on the SEE exam.
questions.memoryAid: Think 'Small Biz = Social Security' to remember that small businesses can use the research credit against Social Security (payroll) taxes.

questions.question: When a business changes its accounting method with IRS consent, the Section 481(a) adjustment:
options: Is always recognized in full in the year of change
Prevents the business from duplicating or omitting income or deductions due to the change
questions.explanation: The IRC §481(a) adjustment is a cumulative catch-up amount that prevents items of income or deduction from being duplicated or omitted as a result of the accounting method change. A positive adjustment (income) is generally spread over 4 years; a negative adjustment (deduction) is taken entirely in the year of change. The adjustment ensures the lifetime income is correct.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - The Section 481(a) adjustment is not always recognized in full in the year of change. While a negative adjustment (resulting in a deduction) is generally taken in the year of change, a positive adjustment (resulting in income) is often spread out over multiple years, typically four, to mitigate the tax impact.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - The primary purpose of the Section 481(a) adjustment is to prevent the duplication or omission of income or deductions when a business changes its accounting method. This adjustment ensures that the taxpayer's lifetime income is accurately reflected, regardless of the accounting method used in any particular year.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - The Section 481(a) adjustment is not limited to cash-basis taxpayers. It applies to any taxpayer, regardless of their accounting method (cash or accrual), who changes their accounting method with IRS consent and where that change results in a duplication or omission of income or deductions.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - There is no general limit of $50,000 per year on the Section 481(a) adjustment. The amount of the adjustment depends on the specific items affected by the accounting method change and the cumulative impact of those changes on the taxpayer's income.

questions.educational: Section 481(a) adjustments are crucial for maintaining tax neutrality when a business changes its accounting method. Without this adjustment, income could be either taxed twice or escape taxation altogether. Understanding these adjustments is essential for Enrolled Agents when advising clients on accounting method changes, as it directly impacts their tax liability and requires careful planning and compliance with IRS regulations.
questions.examTip: On the EA exam, remember that a positive Section 481(a) adjustment (increasing income) is generally spread over multiple years, while a negative adjustment (decreasing income) is usually taken in the year of change, unless a specific exception applies.
questions.memoryAid: 481(a): 'Adjust' to avoid 'Omit' or 'Repeat' income.

questions.question: In a partnership, deductions attributable to nonrecourse liabilities are generally allocated to:
options: Partners in accordance with their share of partnership profits
The partner who guarantees the debt
questions.explanation: Under Treas. Reg. §1.704-2, nonrecourse deductions are deductions funded by nonrecourse liabilities (where no partner bears the economic risk of loss). These deductions must be allocated in accordance with the partners' interests in partnership profits (or any other manner consistent with the regulations). Since no partner bears the loss risk, the allocation follows profit-sharing ratios.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - Nonrecourse deductions are not allocated solely to general partners. The allocation is based on profit-sharing ratios, regardless of whether a partner is a general or limited partner. Treas. Reg. §1.704-2 dictates the allocation rules, and partner status is not the determining factor.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - Nonrecourse deductions, as defined under Treas. Reg. §1.704-2, are allocated according to the partners' interests in partnership profits. This is because no partner bears the economic risk of loss for these deductions, so the allocation follows the profit-sharing arrangement.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - The partner who guarantees the debt is responsible for recourse debt, not nonrecourse debt. If a partner guarantees the debt, it becomes recourse debt to that partner, and the allocation rules for nonrecourse debt no longer apply to that portion of the debt.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - Nonrecourse deductions are not allocated solely to limited partners. The allocation is based on profit-sharing ratios, regardless of whether a partner is a general or limited partner. The key is the absence of economic risk of loss for any partner.

questions.educational: Nonrecourse debt is debt for which no partner has personal liability. Because no partner is personally at risk, the associated deductions are allocated based on how the partners share profits. This allocation method ensures that tax benefits are distributed equitably among partners who are not bearing the economic burden of the debt, aligning with the overall principle of partnership taxation where income and deductions flow through to the partners.
questions.examTip: On the EA exam, carefully distinguish between recourse and nonrecourse debt when determining deduction allocations in a partnership. Remember that recourse debt is allocated based on economic risk of loss, while nonrecourse debt is allocated based on profit-sharing ratios.
questions.memoryAid: Non-Recourse = No Risk = Profit Share

questions.question: A sole proprietor may deduct what percentage of self-employment tax as an above-the-line deduction?
questions.explanation: Under IRC §164(f), self-employed individuals may deduct 50% of self-employment tax (the "employer equivalent" portion) as an above-the-line deduction on Schedule 1 of Form 1040. This deduction reduces adjusted gross income but does not reduce net earnings from self-employment for purposes of calculating the SE tax itself.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - The deductible portion of self-employment tax is not 25%. This percentage is not related to any specific deduction or calculation regarding self-employment tax.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - IRC §164(f) allows self-employed individuals to deduct one-half (50%) of their self-employment tax as an above-the-line deduction. This deduction represents the employer's equivalent portion of Social Security and Medicare taxes, which would be deductible by an employer.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - 75% is not the correct percentage for the self-employment tax deduction. The law specifically allows for a 50% deduction, not 75%.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - While a sole proprietor pays both the employee and employer portions of Social Security and Medicare taxes, they are only allowed to deduct the employer equivalent portion, which is 50%, not 100%. Allowing a 100% deduction would effectively eliminate the tax burden, which is not the intent of the law.

questions.educational: The deduction for one-half of self-employment tax is designed to provide self-employed individuals with similar tax treatment as employees. Employees do not pay income tax on the employer's share of Social Security and Medicare taxes, so allowing self-employed individuals to deduct the equivalent amount helps to level the playing field. This deduction is an adjustment to gross income, meaning it reduces AGI and can impact other deductions and credits.
questions.examTip: Remember that the deduction for one-half of self-employment tax is an above-the-line deduction, meaning it is taken before adjusted gross income (AGI) is calculated. This is important for determining eligibility for various deductions and credits that are limited by AGI.
questions.memoryAid: Think of 'SE tax' as 'See Fifty' - you can deduct 50% of your self-employment tax.

questions.question: Under IRC §267, losses on sales between related parties are:
options: Deferred until the buyer sells to an unrelated party
questions.explanation: Under IRC §267(a)(1), no deduction is allowed for losses on sales or exchanges of property between related parties. Related parties include family members (spouses, siblings, ancestors, lineal descendants), an individual and a corporation of which they own more than 50%, and two corporations in the same controlled group. The buyer may use the disallowed loss to reduce gain on a subsequent sale to an unrelated party.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - IRC §267 specifically disallows the deduction of losses between related parties; it does not allow for full deduction.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - IRC §267(a)(1) explicitly states that no deduction shall be allowed for losses from the sale or exchange of property between related persons. This rule prevents taxpayers from artificially generating tax losses by selling property to related parties at a loss.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - The $3,000 limitation applies to capital losses for individual taxpayers under IRC §1211, not to the disallowance of losses between related parties under IRC §267.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - While the disallowed loss can be used to reduce gain on a subsequent sale to an unrelated party by the *buyer*, the initial loss is not deferred; it is disallowed to the seller. The buyer's basis is their cost, not the seller's original basis.

questions.educational: IRC §267 aims to prevent tax avoidance by disallowing losses on transactions between related parties. This is because related parties may not be dealing at arm's length, and the transaction could be structured solely to generate a tax benefit. While the loss is disallowed to the seller, the related buyer gets to use the disallowed loss to offset any gain when they sell the property to an unrelated party.
questions.examTip: Remember that the related party rules under IRC §267 are broad and include various relationships, so carefully analyze the relationship between the buyer and seller when determining the deductibility of a loss on a sale.
questions.memoryAid: Think 'Related = Rejected' when it comes to deducting losses on sales to related parties.

questions.question: A partnership must generally adopt the same tax year as its:
options: Partners owning a majority interest (majority interest tax year)
questions.explanation: Under IRC §706(b), a partnership must generally use the tax year of its majority-interest partners (partners owning more than 50% of profits and capital). If there is no majority interest tax year, the partnership must use the tax year of all principal partners. If neither rule determines a year, the partnership must use the year that produces the least aggregate deferral. A Section 444 election may allow a different year.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - A partnership is not required to adopt the tax year of its founding partner. While the tax year of the partners is considered, it's the majority interest that dictates the partnership's tax year, not just one partner.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - IRC §706(b)(1)(B) mandates that a partnership adopt the same tax year as the partners owning a majority interest (more than 50% of profits and capital). This ensures that the partnership's income is reported in the same tax year as the majority of its owners, minimizing deferral.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - The state of formation has no bearing on the partnership's tax year. Federal tax law, specifically the Internal Revenue Code, governs the selection of a partnership's tax year.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - A partnership's primary bank is irrelevant in determining its tax year. The tax year is determined by the tax years of its partners, not its banking relationships.

questions.educational: The rules for partnership tax year selection are designed to prevent tax deferral. By aligning the partnership's tax year with that of its majority owners, the IRS ensures that partnership income is taxed to the partners in a timely manner. This is a key concept in partnership taxation, as it directly impacts when partners must report and pay taxes on their share of the partnership's profits.
questions.examTip: On the EA exam, pay close attention to the ownership percentages when determining a partnership's required tax year. Remember the hierarchy: majority interest, principal partners, least aggregate deferral, and then consider a Section 444 election.
questions.memoryAid: Majority Rules! The majority interest partners determine the partnership's tax year.

questions.question: Which of the following factors most strongly indicates that a worker is an employee rather than an independent contractor?
options: The worker uses their own tools and equipment
The business controls when, where, and how the work is performed
The worker sets their own schedule
The worker provides services to multiple clients
questions.explanation: The IRS uses common-law rules to determine worker classification, focusing on the degree of control and independence. Behavioral control (when, where, and how work is performed) is a primary factor. Other factors include financial control (how the worker is paid, expense reimbursement) and the type of relationship (written contracts, benefits). Workers who control their own methods are more likely independent contractors.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - Independent contractors often use their own tools and equipment. This indicates a level of autonomy and control over their work, which is characteristic of independent contractor status, not employee status.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - The degree of control a business has over a worker is a primary factor in determining worker classification. If the business dictates when, where, and how the work is performed, this strongly suggests an employer-employee relationship. This aligns with the IRS's common-law rules for worker classification, emphasizing behavioral control.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - Setting one's own schedule is a characteristic of an independent contractor. Employees typically have their work schedules determined by their employer. This independence is a key differentiator.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - Providing services to multiple clients is a strong indicator of independent contractor status. Employees generally work exclusively for one employer at a time. Serving multiple clients demonstrates business independence.

questions.educational: The distinction between an employee and an independent contractor is crucial for employment tax purposes. Employers are responsible for withholding income tax, Social Security, and Medicare taxes from employee wages, as well as paying employer-side payroll taxes. Misclassifying employees as independent contractors can result in significant penalties from the IRS, including back taxes, interest, and fines. Understanding the IRS's three categories of control (behavioral, financial, and relationship) is essential for accurate classification.
questions.examTip: On the EA exam, when evaluating worker classification, prioritize the 'behavioral control' factor. Look for indicators of how much control the business exerts over the worker's actions and methods.
questions.memoryAid: Remember 'ABC' - Autonomy = Better Contractor. If the worker has Autonomy, they are likely a Better (independent) Contractor.

questions.question: The employer's share of FICA tax for Social Security in 2024 is:
questions.explanation: The FICA tax for Social Security is 12.4% total, split equally between employer and employee at 6.2% each, on wages up to the Social Security wage base ($168,600 for 2024). The Medicare tax is an additional 1.45% each (2.9% total) with no wage base limit. High-earning employees pay an additional 0.9% Medicare tax on wages exceeding $200,000.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - 1.45% is the employer's share of Medicare tax, not Social Security tax. Remember that FICA consists of both Social Security and Medicare taxes.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - The employer's share of Social Security tax (part of FICA) is 6.2% of the employee's wages, up to the Social Security wage base. The employee also pays 6.2%, for a total of 12.4%.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - 7.65% represents the *total* of the employer's share of both Social Security (6.2%) and Medicare (1.45%) taxes. This option doesn't isolate only the Social Security portion.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - 12.4% represents the *total* Social Security tax, including both the employer's and employee's portions. The employer only pays half of this amount.

questions.educational: FICA taxes, comprising Social Security and Medicare, are crucial for funding federal programs that provide benefits to retirees, the disabled, and those needing medical care. Employers are responsible for withholding the employee's share of these taxes and remitting both the employee's and employer's portions to the IRS. Understanding the nuances of employment taxes is vital for accurate payroll processing and compliance, a key area for Enrolled Agents.
questions.examTip: On the EA exam, pay close attention to whether a question asks for the employer's share, the employee's share, or the total amount of FICA taxes, and remember the wage base limitation for Social Security tax.
questions.memoryAid: FICA: 6.2 + 1.45 (employer share). Think 'Six and a bit' for Social Security.

questions.question: Form 941 (Employer's Quarterly Federal Tax Return) is filed:
questions.explanation: Form 941 is filed quarterly to report wages paid, tips, federal income tax withheld, and both employer and employee shares of FICA taxes. The due dates are April 30, July 31, October 31, and January 31 for the four calendar quarters. Very small employers (annual liability of $1,000 or less) may file Form 944 annually instead.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - Form 941 is not filed annually in most cases. While some very small employers with annual tax liabilities of $1,000 or less can file Form 944 annually, Form 941 is generally a quarterly return.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - Form 941, Employer's Quarterly Federal Tax Return, is filed every quarter to report income taxes, Social Security tax, and Medicare tax withheld from employee's wages, as well as the employer's portion of Social Security and Medicare taxes. The quarterly due dates are April 30, July 31, October 31, and January 31, unless these dates fall on a weekend or holiday, in which case the due date is the next business day.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - Form 941 is not filed monthly. While some employers may deposit employment taxes monthly or semi-weekly depending on their lookback period liability, the *reporting* of these taxes is done quarterly on Form 941.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - Form 941 is not filed semi-annually. The filing frequency for Form 941 is quarterly, providing the IRS with regular updates on employment tax liabilities.

questions.educational: Employment taxes are a critical area for the EA exam because they impact nearly every business. Form 941 serves as the primary mechanism for employers to report and remit these taxes to the IRS on a quarterly basis. Understanding the nuances of employment tax reporting, including deposit schedules and form requirements, is essential for providing accurate and compliant advice to clients.
questions.examTip: On the EA exam, pay close attention to the employer's size and tax liability, as this can affect their filing requirements (e.g., Form 944 for very small employers).
questions.memoryAid: 941 = Quarterly, think 'Q' for both.

questions.question: The trust fund recovery penalty under IRC §6672 may be assessed against:
options: Any responsible person who willfully fails to collect, account for, or pay over withholding taxes
Only the CEO of the corporation
All tax return preparers who file the return
questions.explanation: The IRC §6672 trust fund recovery penalty (equal to 100% of unpaid trust fund taxes) may be assessed personally against any responsible person who willfully fails to collect, account for, or pay over withholding taxes (income tax and employee share of FICA taxes). Responsible persons include officers, directors, shareholders with authority, or anyone with control over the company's financial decisions.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - While the corporation is initially liable for employment taxes, the trust fund recovery penalty under IRC §6672 targets *individuals* responsible for the failure to remit those taxes, not the entity itself. The penalty is a mechanism to recover unpaid taxes from those with control over the funds.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - IRC §6672 specifically allows the IRS to assess a penalty equal to 100% of the unpaid trust fund taxes against any 'responsible person' who 'willfully' failed to collect, account for, or pay over these taxes. A responsible person has the duty and authority to ensure the taxes are paid, and 'willfully' means voluntarily, consciously, and intentionally, not necessarily with fraudulent intent.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - While the CEO might be a responsible person, the trust fund recovery penalty isn't automatically assessed solely based on their title. The IRS assesses the penalty based on whether the individual had the authority and responsibility to ensure the taxes were paid, regardless of their specific job title.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - Tax return preparers generally do not have the authority or responsibility to pay a company's employment taxes. While preparers can be penalized for errors on the return, the trust fund recovery penalty under IRC §6672 is specifically aimed at those with control over the company's finances and the payment of taxes.

questions.educational: The trust fund recovery penalty exists to ensure that employment taxes, which are held 'in trust' for the government, are actually remitted. This penalty is a powerful tool for the IRS because it allows them to pursue individuals who made the decision not to pay these taxes, even if the business itself is insolvent. Understanding this penalty is crucial for EAs advising business clients on their employment tax obligations.
questions.examTip: On the EA exam, carefully analyze the facts to determine if an individual meets the definition of a 'responsible person' and acted 'willfully' in failing to pay employment taxes. Look for indicators of control over finances and knowledge of the unpaid taxes.
questions.memoryAid: TFRP = Trust Fund Recovery Penalty targets Responsible Persons who Willfully fail to pay.

questions.question: The Federal Unemployment Tax Act (FUTA) tax rate is applied to the first how many dollars of each employee's wages per year?
questions.explanation: The FUTA tax rate of 6.0% is applied to the first $7,000 of wages paid to each employee per calendar year. However, employers receive a credit of up to 5.4% for state unemployment taxes paid, resulting in an effective FUTA rate of 0.6% ($42 per employee per year). FUTA is paid entirely by the employer; it is not withheld from employee wages.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - The FUTA wage base is not $3,000. While some states may have lower unemployment wage bases, the federal standard for FUTA is different.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - The Federal Unemployment Tax Act (FUTA) imposes a tax on employers to fund state workforce agencies. This tax applies to the first $7,000 of wages paid to each employee during the calendar year. This wage base is specifically defined in the Internal Revenue Code.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - $15,000 is not the correct wage base for FUTA tax. This amount is significantly higher than the actual FUTA wage base.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - $50,000 is not the correct wage base for FUTA tax. This amount is far higher than the actual FUTA wage base of $7,000.

questions.educational: The FUTA tax is designed to provide funds for unemployment compensation programs administered by the states. The $7,000 wage base is a threshold established by Congress to balance the need for adequate funding with the burden on employers. Understanding employment tax responsibilities is crucial for Enrolled Agents as they advise businesses on compliance and tax planning strategies, impacting both federal and state tax liabilities.
questions.examTip: Remember that the FUTA tax is paid solely by the employer and is not deducted from employee wages; focus on memorizing the $7,000 wage base and the potential FUTA credit against state unemployment taxes paid.
questions.memoryAid: FUTA: First Under 7,000 Applies.

questions.question: Section 382 limits the use of a corporation's net operating losses (NOLs) following:
options: Any change in the corporation's fiscal year
An ownership change of more than 50 percentage points
A change in the corporation's accounting method
The appointment of a new CEO
questions.explanation: IRC §382 limits the amount of pre-change NOLs that can be used annually after an ownership change. An ownership change occurs when the percentage of stock owned by 5% shareholders increases by more than 50 percentage points over a 3-year testing period. The annual limitation equals the value of the loss corporation multiplied by the long-term tax-exempt rate. This prevents trafficking in NOL carryforwards.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - A change in the corporation's fiscal year does not trigger a Section 382 limitation. While a change in accounting period requires IRS approval, it doesn't impact the ownership structure that Section 382 addresses.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - Section 382 of the Internal Revenue Code specifically limits the use of a corporation's net operating losses (NOLs) after an ownership change of more than 50 percentage points within a 3-year testing period. This is designed to prevent companies from being acquired solely for their NOLs, a practice known as 'trafficking' in loss carryforwards.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - A change in the corporation's accounting method, while potentially affecting taxable income, does not trigger the Section 382 limitation. Accounting method changes are governed by other sections of the IRC and relate to how income and expenses are recognized, not ownership.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - The appointment of a new CEO, by itself, does not constitute an ownership change under Section 382. The focus of Section 382 is on the change in ownership of the corporation's stock, not its management.

questions.educational: Section 382 exists to prevent the trafficking of NOLs. Without this limitation, a profitable company could acquire a loss company solely to offset its income with the acquired NOLs, effectively buying a tax benefit. The limitation ensures that the NOLs are primarily used to benefit the ongoing business that generated them, even after an ownership change, albeit with restrictions.
questions.examTip: Remember that the Section 382 limitation is triggered by a cumulative ownership change exceeding 50 percentage points within a 3-year testing period. As an EA, you'll need to calculate this change carefully when advising clients involved in mergers, acquisitions, or significant stock transactions.
questions.memoryAid: 50/382: Over 50% ownership change triggers Section 382 limitations.

questions.question: An S corporation may have a maximum of how many shareholders?
questions.explanation: An S corporation is limited to 100 shareholders. However, members of a family (as defined under IRC §1361(c)(1)) may be treated as a single shareholder. This includes a common ancestor, lineal descendants, and spouses (or former spouses) of these individuals, within 6 generations.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - An S corporation is not limited to 35 shareholders. This number is significantly lower than the actual limit, making it an incorrect choice.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - An S corporation is not limited to 75 shareholders. While closer to the correct number, it still falls short of the maximum allowed, making it incorrect.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is CORRECT - An S corporation, as defined under Subchapter S of the Internal Revenue Code, is limited to a maximum of 100 shareholders. This limitation is a key characteristic distinguishing S corporations from C corporations and partnerships, impacting their tax treatment and operational structure.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - An S corporation is not permitted to have 500 shareholders. This number greatly exceeds the maximum shareholder limit imposed by the IRS for S corporations.

questions.educational: The 100-shareholder limit for S corporations is in place to balance the benefits of pass-through taxation with the administrative complexity of managing a large number of owners. This limitation helps maintain the simplicity of S corporation taxation, which is a key advantage over C corporations. Understanding the shareholder limitations is crucial for advising clients on the appropriate business structure for their needs.
questions.examTip: Remember that the 100-shareholder limit applies to the number of shareholders, not the number of shares issued. Family members can be treated as one shareholder under specific circumstances, so pay attention to family relationships in exam questions.
questions.memoryAid: S-corp: 'S'tands for 'Small,' think 'Small' = 100 shareholders max.

questions.question: Under the disguised sale rules of IRC §707(a)(2)(B), a contribution of property followed by a related distribution from the partnership may be recharacterized as:
options: A sale of the property to the partnership
A gift to the other partners
questions.explanation: Under IRC §707(a)(2)(B) and Treas. Reg. §1.707-3, if a partner contributes property and receives a distribution from the partnership within 2 years, the transactions may be presumed to be a disguised sale. If recharacterized as a sale, the contributing partner recognizes gain or loss on the property transfer, and the transaction is treated as occurring between the partner (as a non-partner) and the partnership.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - A disguised sale is designed to treat a transaction that appears to be a contribution as a sale, resulting in taxable gain or loss. A tax-free exchange, such as under IRC §721 for partnership contributions, is the opposite of what the disguised sale rules aim to achieve.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - IRC §707(a)(2)(B) specifically addresses situations where a contribution of property to a partnership, followed by a distribution to the contributing partner, is in substance a sale of the property to the partnership. The transaction is recharacterized as a sale between the partner acting in a non-partner capacity and the partnership.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - While a contribution to a partnership can indirectly benefit other partners by increasing the partnership's capital, a disguised sale is not treated as a gift. The contributing partner receives consideration (the distribution) in exchange for the property, indicating a sale rather than a gratuitous transfer.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - A guaranteed payment under IRC §707(c) is a payment to a partner for services or the use of capital, determined without regard to partnership income. A disguised sale involves the transfer of property, not services or capital usage, and the distribution is directly linked to the property contributed, not a guaranteed return.

questions.educational: The disguised sale rules under IRC §707(a)(2)(B) prevent partners from disguising taxable sales of property to a partnership as tax-free contributions under IRC §721. These rules are crucial because they ensure that partners recognize gain or loss when they are effectively selling property to the partnership, rather than merely contributing it. Understanding these rules is important for the SEE2 exam as it relates to partnership taxation and the proper characterization of transactions between partners and the partnership.
questions.examTip: On the EA exam, pay close attention to the timing and amount of distributions following a property contribution to determine if the disguised sale rules might apply. Look for distributions made within two years of the contribution, as these are presumed to be part of a disguised sale.
questions.memoryAid: Think 'Contribution then Cash = Could be a Cover-up (Disguised Sale)'

questions.question: A calendar-year C corporation that wants its S election effective January 1 of the next year must file Form 2553 by:
questions.explanation: Under IRC §1362(b), an S election made during the first 2 months and 15 days of the tax year is effective for that year. For a calendar-year corporation, Form 2553 filed by March 15 of the next year makes the election effective January 1 of that year. Alternatively, an election made after March 15 but during the current year is effective for the following year.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - Filing Form 2553 on January 1 of the next year is not sufficient to guarantee an S election effective that same year. The deadline is later, allowing for the first 2 months and 15 days of the tax year.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - Filing Form 2553 by March 15 of the *current* year would only make the S election effective for the *current* year, not the following year as the question specifies. The question requires the election to be effective January 1 of the *next* year.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is CORRECT - Under IRC §1362(b)(1)(B), a C corporation can elect S corporation status to be effective January 1 of the *next* year by filing Form 2553 anytime during the *current* year or on or before March 15 of the *next* year. This allows the IRS time to process the election and ensures the corporation meets the requirements for S status.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - While filing by December 31 of the current year *can* result in an S election effective January 1 of the next year, it is not the *latest* permissible date. The deadline extends to March 15 of the next year, so this option is not the most accurate.

questions.educational: The S election allows a corporation to pass its income, losses, deductions, and credits through to its shareholders, avoiding double taxation at the corporate level. This election is a key decision point when forming or restructuring a business, as it impacts the tax liabilities of both the entity and its owners. Understanding the timing requirements for Form 2553 is crucial for effective tax planning and compliance.
questions.examTip: Remember that the S election rules are strict; failing to meet the filing deadline can delay the election's effective date by a full year. Pay close attention to dates when calculating deadlines on the exam.
questions.memoryAid: Think 'March 15th Next Year' for the latest date to elect S status for the next year.

questions.question: When a C corporation sells Section 1250 property at a gain, what portion of the unrecaptured depreciation is treated as ordinary income under Section 291?
options: 20% of the amount that would be Section 1250 ordinary income if the property were Section 1245 property
questions.explanation: Under IRC §291(a)(1), when a C corporation disposes of Section 1250 property, 20% of the additional amount that would be ordinary income if the property were Section 1245 property is treated as ordinary income. This rule imposes a higher recapture burden on corporations than on individuals for real property depreciation. The remaining gain is Section 1231 gain.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - 100% of the unrecaptured depreciation is not treated as ordinary income under Section 291. Section 291 only applies to a portion of what *would* be Section 1245 gain if the property were treated as such.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - IRC §291(a)(1) dictates that when a C corporation sells Section 1250 property at a gain, 20% of the amount that would be ordinary income if the property were Section 1245 property is treated as ordinary income. This 'as if' calculation is crucial; it doesn't directly recapture 20% of the *actual* Section 1250 gain, but rather calculates what *would have been* ordinary income under the more stringent Section 1245 rules and then applies the 20% to that amount.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - 50% of total depreciation is not the correct calculation under Section 291. The calculation involves determining what *would be* Section 1245 gain and then applying the 20% rule.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - 28% of the capital gain is incorrect. While 28% is relevant for collectibles and small business stock, it doesn't apply to the Section 291 recapture rules for Section 1250 property.
whyWrong.4: Why option E is WRONG - This option does not exist.

questions.educational: Section 291 exists to partially equalize the tax treatment of depreciation recapture between corporations and individuals. Individuals generally benefit from lower capital gains rates on real property, while corporations face a higher ordinary income recapture on a portion of the gain. This provision aims to reduce the tax advantage corporations might otherwise have from depreciating real property.
questions.examTip: Remember that Section 291 only applies to C corporations and only to Section 1250 property. On the exam, carefully identify the entity type and asset involved to determine if this rule is applicable.
questions.memoryAid: C-CORP 20% (C Corp, 20% of potential 1245 gain)

questions.question: Which of the following costs is a deductible/amortizable organization expense for a partnership?
options: Commissions paid for selling partnership interests
Legal fees for negotiating the partnership agreement
Costs of printing and selling partnership interests to investors
Advertising expenses related to selling limited partnership interests
questions.explanation: Under IRC §709(b), partnership organizational expenses (such as legal fees for preparing the partnership agreement, filing fees, and accounting fees for setting up the partnership) may be deducted up to $5,000 and the remainder amortized over 180 months. Syndication costs (costs of issuing and selling partnership interests, including commissions, printing, and advertising) are never deductible or amortizable.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - Commissions paid for selling partnership interests are syndication costs, which are explicitly non-deductible and non-amortizable under IRC §709(a). These costs are treated as capital expenditures.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - Legal fees for negotiating the partnership agreement are considered organizational expenses under IRC §709(b). A partnership can deduct up to $5,000 of organizational expenses in the year the business begins, with the excess amortized over 180 months, starting with the month the partnership begins business. This deduction is phased out dollar for dollar when organizational expenses exceed $50,000.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - Costs of printing and selling partnership interests to investors are syndication costs. Syndication costs are specifically excluded from being deductible or amortizable under IRC §709(a) and must be capitalized.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - Advertising expenses related to selling limited partnership interests are syndication costs. These costs are incurred to raise capital and are not deductible or amortizable per IRC §709(a).

questions.educational: Partnership organizational expenses are costs directly related to creating the partnership as a legal entity, allowing for a deduction and amortization to reflect the long-term benefit. Syndication costs, on the other hand, are related to raising capital and are treated as capital expenditures, not deductible or amortizable. Understanding the distinction between organizational and syndication costs is crucial for accurate partnership tax reporting and is a frequently tested topic on the SEE exam.
questions.examTip: On the EA exam, carefully distinguish between organizational expenses (deductible/amortizable) and syndication expenses (never deductible/amortizable). Look for keywords like 'creating the partnership' (organizational) versus 'selling partnership interests' (syndication).
questions.memoryAid: Think 'Organize the Partnership, then Syndicate the Shares.' Organizational costs are deductible/amortizable; syndication costs are not.

questions.question: For the Section 199A QBI deduction, the W-2 wage and SSTB limitations begin to phase in when a single taxpayer's taxable income exceeds approximately:
questions.explanation: For 2024, the Section 199A phase-in thresholds are approximately $182,100 for single filers and $364,200 for married filing jointly (adjusted annually for inflation). Below these thresholds, the deduction is simply 20% of QBI without the W-2 wage/UBIA limitation and without SSTB restrictions. Above the thresholds plus $50,000 ($100,000 MFJ), the limitations are fully effective.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - $100,000 is significantly lower than the actual phase-in threshold for single filers in 2024. This amount does not relate to any specific threshold within the Section 199A deduction.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - The Section 199A deduction for qualified business income (QBI) has income limitations that trigger a phase-in range for the W-2 wage and SSTB limitations. For single filers in 2024, this phase-in range begins at approximately $182,100. This means that once a single taxpayer's taxable income exceeds this amount, the limitations on the QBI deduction start to apply, potentially reducing the amount of the deduction.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - $250,000 is not the correct phase-in threshold for single filers. While it is a significant number in tax law, it doesn't directly relate to the *start* of the Section 199A phase-in range for single taxpayers.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - $364,200 is the approximate *married filing jointly* phase-in threshold for the Section 199A deduction in 2024, not the single filer threshold. Selecting this answer indicates a misunderstanding of the different thresholds based on filing status.

questions.educational: The Section 199A deduction was created to provide a tax break for owners of pass-through businesses, aiming to level the playing field with the corporate tax rate reduction. The income thresholds and limitations, including the W-2 wage and SSTB limitations, were implemented to target the deduction towards small and medium-sized businesses, preventing high-income individuals in certain service industries from disproportionately benefiting. Understanding these phase-in ranges is crucial for accurately calculating the QBI deduction and advising clients on tax planning strategies.
questions.examTip: Pay close attention to the filing status when calculating the Section 199A deduction, as the income thresholds for the phase-in range differ significantly between single and married filing jointly. Remember to use the most current year's thresholds, as they are adjusted annually for inflation.
questions.memoryAid: Think of 'Single Starts Small' - the single filer threshold is smaller than the married filing jointly threshold for 199A.

questions.question: Under IRC §332, no gain or loss is recognized by a parent corporation on the receipt of property in a complete liquidation of its subsidiary if the parent owns at least:
questions.explanation: Under IRC §332, a parent corporation recognizes no gain or loss on the receipt of property distributed in complete liquidation of a subsidiary, provided the parent owns at least 80% of the subsidiary's voting power and value. Under IRC §334(b)(1), the parent takes a carryover basis in the subsidiary's assets. The subsidiary also recognizes no gain or loss under IRC §337.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - While 50% ownership represents control in many situations, IRC §332 specifically requires at least 80% ownership for non-recognition of gain or loss in a subsidiary liquidation. This lower percentage does not meet the statutory requirements for tax-free treatment.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - IRC §332 explicitly states that a parent corporation owning at least 80% of the subsidiary's stock (both in voting power and value) can liquidate the subsidiary without recognizing gain or loss. This provision aims to simplify corporate restructurings within a controlled group.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - Although 90% ownership signifies substantial control, IRC §332 uses a specific threshold of 80% for determining non-recognition of gain or loss in a subsidiary liquidation. Exceeding the 80% threshold doesn't change the applicable rule.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - While 100% ownership certainly qualifies for non-recognition under IRC §332, the statute only requires a minimum of 80% ownership. The 80% threshold provides a specific and sufficient condition for tax-free liquidation; 100% ownership is not a requirement.

questions.educational: IRC §332 allows a parent corporation to liquidate a subsidiary tax-free to simplify the corporate structure and eliminate potential double taxation. This provision encourages efficient resource allocation within a corporate group by allowing assets to be moved without immediate tax consequences. Understanding consolidated return rules and intercompany transactions is crucial for mastering this area of corporate taxation, a key component of the SEE2 exam.
questions.examTip: Remember the 80% ownership threshold for IRC §332 liquidations; this is a frequently tested concept on the EA exam. Pay close attention to the wording of the question to identify if a subsidiary liquidation is occurring.
questions.memoryAid: Think of '80% is GREAT' for tax-free subsidiary liquidations under Section 332.

questions.question: An S corporation is restricted to having:
options: Only common stock with no voting rights
One class of stock (differences in voting rights permitted)
Multiple classes of stock with identical par values
Both preferred and common stock in equal amounts
questions.explanation: Under IRC §1361(b)(1)(D), an S corporation may have only one class of stock outstanding. However, differences in voting rights among shares of common stock are permitted and do not create a second class of stock. The one-class-of-stock requirement means all outstanding shares must have identical rights to distribution and liquidation proceeds.
questions.subtopic: S Corp One Class of Stock

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - While an S corporation can only have common stock, it's not restricted from having voting rights attached to that stock. Differences in voting rights are permitted within the single class of stock.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - IRC §1361(b)(1)(D) states that an S corporation can only have one class of stock. However, differences in voting rights among shares of common stock are permitted and do not violate the one-class-of-stock requirement, as long as all shares have identical rights to distribution and liquidation proceeds.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - The one-class-of-stock rule prohibits multiple classes of stock, regardless of whether they have identical par values. The key is that all shares must have identical rights to distribution and liquidation proceeds, not just identical par values.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - S corporations are not permitted to have both preferred and common stock. The one-class-of-stock rule restricts the corporation to a single class of stock, which effectively eliminates the possibility of having both preferred and common stock.

questions.educational: The one-class-of-stock rule for S corporations is designed to simplify the allocation of income, losses, deductions, and credits to shareholders. If an S corporation had multiple classes of stock with differing rights to distributions, it would complicate the allocation process and potentially violate the pass-through nature of S corporations, a key concept tested on the SEE exam.
questions.examTip: On the EA exam, pay close attention to the wording of S corporation questions. The one-class-of-stock rule is strictly enforced, but differences in voting rights are an important exception to remember.
questions.memoryAid: S-Corps: 'Single Class, Same Slice' - One class of stock, same slice of the pie (distributions).

questions.question: In what order are adjustments made to a partner's basis in their partnership interest at year-end?
options: Increase for income items, decrease for distributions, decrease for losses and deductions
Decrease for distributions first, then increase for income
The order does not matter as all adjustments are simultaneous
Decrease for losses first, then increase for income, then decrease for distributions
questions.explanation: Under IRC §705, the basis ordering rules require: (1) First, increase basis for all income items (ordinary and separately stated, including tax-exempt income); (2) Then decrease basis for distributions; (3) Then decrease basis for nondeductible, noncapital expenditures; (4) Finally, decrease basis for losses and deductions. This ordering ensures that distributions and losses are properly limited.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is CORRECT - IRC §705 dictates the order of adjustments to a partner's basis. Basis is first increased by income items (including tax-exempt income), then decreased by distributions, and finally decreased by losses and deductions. This order ensures that distributions and losses are only allowed to the extent of the partner's basis.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - While distributions do decrease basis and income does increase it, the order is incorrect. Income items are accounted for *before* distributions are subtracted from the partner's basis.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - The order of adjustments to a partner's basis is crucial and definitely matters. IRC §705 specifically outlines the order to properly limit distributions and loss deductions to the partner's basis.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - While losses do decrease basis and income increases it, the order is incorrect. Income items are accounted for *before* losses and distributions are subtracted from the partner's basis, and distributions are accounted for before losses.

questions.educational: The basis of a partnership interest is crucial for determining the taxable gain or loss upon the sale of the partnership interest, as well as the deductibility of partnership losses. The ordering rules ensure that partners can only deduct losses and receive distributions up to their adjusted basis, preventing the recognition of artificial losses. This concept is heavily tested in the context of partnership taxation and is essential for understanding the limitations on loss deductions and the tax consequences of distributions.
questions.examTip: Remember the basis ordering rules! On the EA exam, you'll likely encounter scenarios where you need to calculate a partner's adjusted basis to determine the deductibility of losses or the taxability of distributions. Pay close attention to the timing of income, distributions, and losses.
questions.memoryAid: I-D-L: Income, Distributions, Losses. Remember this acronym to recall the order of basis adjustments.

questions.question: Qualified improvement property (QIP) placed in service after December 31, 2017, is depreciated over what MACRS recovery period?
questions.explanation: The CARES Act corrected the TCJA drafting error and assigned qualified improvement property a 15-year MACRS recovery period (20 years under ADS), making it eligible for bonus depreciation. QIP includes improvements to the interior of nonresidential real property placed in service after the building was first placed in service, excluding elevators, escalators, structural framework, and building enlargements.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - A 7-year recovery period generally applies to assets like office furniture and fixtures, not qualified improvement property. While some assets have a 7-year life, QIP specifically falls under a different category due to its nature as an improvement to nonresidential real property.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - The CARES Act retroactively corrected a drafting error in the Tax Cuts and Jobs Act (TCJA) and specifically assigned a 15-year MACRS recovery period to qualified improvement property (QIP) placed in service after December 31, 2017. This correction also made QIP eligible for bonus depreciation, a significant tax benefit.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - A 27.5-year recovery period is used for residential rental property. Qualified improvement property, by definition, relates to improvements made to the interior of *nonresidential* real property, making this recovery period inapplicable.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - A 39-year recovery period applies to nonresidential real property itself (the building shell), not to qualified improvements made to the interior of that property after it was initially placed in service. QIP is a specific category with its own designated recovery period.

questions.educational: Depreciation is a crucial aspect of business taxation, allowing businesses to deduct the cost of assets over their useful lives. The MACRS system categorizes assets into different classes with assigned recovery periods. Understanding these classifications, especially for assets like QIP which have undergone legislative changes, is vital for accurate tax calculations and planning.
questions.examTip: On the EA exam, pay close attention to the dates associated with tax law changes, particularly those related to depreciation. The CARES Act's retroactive correction for QIP is a common area for test questions.
questions.memoryAid: QIP = 15 (years). Think: 'Quality Improvements are Fifteen years of value.'

questions.question: A monthly depositor must deposit employment taxes accumulated during a calendar month by the:
options: Next day after the accumulation exceeds $100,000
Last day of the same month
Last day of the following quarter
questions.explanation: Under the monthly deposit rule, an employer must deposit employment taxes accumulated during a calendar month by the 15th day of the following month. The semi-weekly deposit rule requires deposits within 3 banking days for larger employers. The $100,000 next-day deposit rule applies when undeposited taxes reach $100,000 or more on any day, regardless of the employer's usual schedule.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - The next-day deposit rule applies only when the employer accumulates $100,000 or more in employment taxes on any single day during the deposit period, regardless of their usual deposit schedule (monthly or semi-weekly). This option incorrectly suggests it applies to monthly depositors generally.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - Under the monthly deposit schedule, employers are required to deposit employment taxes accumulated during a calendar month on or before the 15th day of the following month. This allows employers a short period to calculate and remit the taxes due.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - Depositing employment taxes by the last day of the same month is not compliant with IRS regulations for monthly depositors. The deadline is the 15th of the following month, providing a short window for calculation and payment.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - The last day of the following quarter is the deadline for filing Form 941, Employer's Quarterly Federal Tax Return, not for depositing employment taxes. Deposits are required more frequently, either monthly or semi-weekly, depending on the employer's tax liability.

questions.educational: The IRS mandates timely deposit of employment taxes (federal income tax withholding, Social Security, and Medicare taxes) to ensure the government receives these funds regularly. The deposit schedule (monthly or semi-weekly) depends on the employer's total tax liability during a look-back period. Understanding these rules is crucial for avoiding penalties and interest, a key aspect of an Enrolled Agent's role in tax compliance.
questions.examTip: On the EA exam, pay close attention to the employer's deposit schedule (monthly or semi-weekly) and the $100,000 next-day deposit rule trigger. Misunderstanding these rules is a common source of errors in employment tax questions.
questions.memoryAid: Monthly depositors: Think '15th of Next' for the deposit deadline.

questions.question: For employment tax purposes, a single-member LLC that is a disregarded entity:
options: Reports employment taxes under its own EIN and is treated as a separate entity
Must use the owner's SSN for all employment tax returns
questions.explanation: While a single-member LLC is disregarded for income tax purposes, it is treated as a separate entity for employment tax and certain excise tax purposes. The LLC must obtain its own EIN and file employment tax returns (Forms 941, 940, W-2) under its own name and EIN. This rule was established by final regulations in 2009.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - A single-member LLC (SMLLC), even if disregarded for income tax purposes, can absolutely have employees. The disregarded status only affects how the income is reported, not the ability to hire.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - According to IRS regulations (specifically, final regulations issued in 2009), a disregarded SMLLC is treated as a separate entity for employment tax purposes. This means it must obtain its own Employer Identification Number (EIN) and file all employment tax returns (e.g., Form 941, Form 940, W-2s) under that EIN.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - The owner's Social Security Number (SSN) is not used for the SMLLC's employment tax returns. The SMLLC, as a separate entity for these purposes, must use its own EIN, not the owner's SSN, for all employment tax filings.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - SMLLCs are not exempt from employment taxes simply because they are disregarded for income tax purposes. If the SMLLC has employees, it is subject to the same employment tax obligations as any other business entity with employees.

questions.educational: The treatment of SMLLCs for employment tax purposes highlights the distinction between income tax and employment tax regulations. While an SMLLC's income is reported on the owner's individual income tax return (Schedule C), the IRS treats it as a separate entity for employment taxes to ensure proper withholding, reporting, and payment of these taxes. This separation is crucial for maintaining accurate records and compliance with federal employment tax laws, a key area of concern for Enrolled Agents advising small business clients.
questions.examTip: Remember that the disregarded entity status of an SMLLC applies primarily to income tax. Always check if the SMLLC has employees, as it will need its own EIN and must file separate employment tax returns.
questions.memoryAid: Income Tax: Disregarded. Employment Tax: Separate. (IDES)

questions.question: A closely held C corporation (not a PSC) that has passive activity losses may offset those losses against:
questions.explanation: Under IRC §469(e)(2), a closely held C corporation (other than a PSC) may use passive activity losses to offset active business income but not portfolio income. This is more favorable than the rules for individuals and PSCs, which cannot offset passive losses against either active or portfolio income. A closely held corporation is one in which 5 or fewer individuals own more than 50% of the stock.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - Closely held C corporations, while allowed to offset passive losses against active income, are specifically prohibited from offsetting those losses against portfolio income under IRC §469(e)(2). This option incorrectly includes portfolio income as an allowable offset.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - IRC §469(e)(2) explicitly states that a closely held C corporation (that is not a personal service corporation) can offset passive activity losses against its active business income. This treatment provides a limited exception to the general passive activity loss rules.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - Closely held C corporations are not permitted to offset passive activity losses solely against portfolio income. The tax code specifically allows them to offset against active income, making this option incorrect.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - This option is incorrect because it states that passive losses cannot offset either active or portfolio income. IRC §469(e)(2) provides a specific exception allowing closely held C corporations to offset passive losses against active income.

questions.educational: The passive activity loss (PAL) rules were enacted to prevent taxpayers from using passive losses to shelter active income. Closely held C corporations receive a slight break because their owners are often actively involved in the business, blurring the line between passive and active participation. Understanding the nuances of these rules is crucial for advising business clients on tax planning and compliance.
questions.examTip: Remember the distinction between the passive activity loss rules for individuals/PSCs and closely held C corporations. Closely held C corps get the limited benefit of offsetting passive losses against active income, a key detail for the SEE exam.
questions.memoryAid: C-Corps Can Conquer Active Income (Passive Losses). Think 'CCC-AA'.
